items.title: Azerach the Whispered King
items.content:
Azerach the Whispered King

!images/Azerach the Whispered King.png

Info
Sovereign Overview
Sovereign of: The Maker's Lungs
Challenge Rating: 33 (210,000 XP)
Size/Type: Gargantuan Aberration (God)
Alignment: Lawful Neutral
Domain: Dominion of Still Air
Concepts: Silence, Truth, Listening, Breath

---

Combat Statistics

Example
Core Stats
AC: 27
Hit Points: 2,240 (128d20 + 1,024)
Speed: 50 ft., fly 120 ft. (hover)
Initiative: +12

Ability Scores

  • STR: 22 (+6)
  • DEX: 24 (+7)
  • CON: 26 (+8)
  • INT: 32 (+11)
  • WIS: 30 (+10)
  • CHA: 28 (+9)
Example
Defenses
Saving Throws: Dex +18, Int +22, Wis +21, Cha +20
Skills: Insight +21, Perception +32 (expertise), Investigation +22, Arcana +22
Damage Resistances: All bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage
Damage Immunities: Thunder, psychic, poison, disease, force
Condition Immunities: Charmed, deafened, exhausted, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, stunned
Senses: Truesight 500 ft., Omnipresent Hearing (hears all sounds within 30 miles), passive Perception 42
Languages: All languages (understands but never speaks), telepathy 10 miles

---

Special Abilities

Tip
Omnipresent Hearing
Azerach hears all sounds within 30 miles simultaneously. He knows the exact location, content, speaker, and emotional context of every word, breath, and heartbeat. Magical silence, soundproofing, and even other planes do not impede this sense. He can choose to focus on specific sounds or filter them as desired.

Danger
The Maker's Lungs (Divine Organ)
Azerach controls air and breath itself. As a bonus action, he can remove all oxygen from a 120-foot radius sphere centered anywhere within 500 feet. Creatures in this area cannot breathe and begin suffocating. This effect lasts until Azerach dismisses it (no action required) or until he dies. Multiple spheres can be active simultaneously.

Dominion of Silence

Within 5 miles of Azerach, all creatures have disadvantage on Stealth checks. He can choose to suppress all sound in any area within this range as a bonus action, preventing all audible communication including verbal spell components. Additionally, he can choose which creatures can hear which sounds, effectively making creatures deaf to specific sources.

Legendary Resistance (7/Day)

If Azerach fails a saving throw, he can choose to succeed instead. When he does so, all sounds within 1 mile cease for 6 seconds (1 round).

Magic Resistance

Azerach has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Divine Immunity

Azerach is immune to effects that would age, blind, charm, deafen, disease, exhaust, frighten, paralyze, petrify, poison, or stun him. He does not need to eat, sleep, drink, or breathe (though he controls breath itself).

Stillness Aura

Creatures within 120 feet of Azerach must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw at the start of their turn or be unable to speak, shout, scream, or make any vocal sounds until the start of their next turn. Affected creatures cannot use verbal spell components or communicate verbally.

Truth Through Silence

When any creature within 500 feet of Azerach attempts to lie or deceive (including through magic like Glibness), Azerach automatically knows. He can choose to make that creature unable to speak for the next minute (no save) as punishment for dishonesty.

Reactive Listening

Azerach can take reactions even when surprised or incapacitated. Additionally, he gains a number of reactions each round equal to the number of creatures that can hear within 500 feet (maximum 10 reactions).

---

Actions

Multiattack

Azerach makes four attacks: two with Breath Theft and two with Whispering Touch. He can replace any attack with use of The Silent Bell (if available).

Breath Theft

Ranged spell attack: +22 to hit, range 500 ft., one target. Hit: 91 (14d12) necrotic damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 29 Constitution saving throw or have their lungs fill with crushing silence. They cannot speak, breathe, or use verbal components for 1 minute and take 6d12 necrotic damage at the start of each of their turns. The target can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on a success.

Whispering Touch

Melee spell attack: +22 to hit, reach 20 ft., one target. Hit: 78 (12d10 + 11) psychic damage. The target hears all their secrets, lies, and hidden truths whispered back to them and must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or be stunned until the end of their next turn. On a failed save by 5 or more, the target is also paralyzed with horror for 1 minute.

The Silent Bell (Recharge 5-6)

Azerach tolls an invisible, cosmic bell that resonates through reality itself. All creatures within 500 feet must make a DC 29 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, a creature takes 132 (24d10) thunder damage and is permanently deafened. On a successful save, the creature takes half as much damage and is deafened for 1 hour. The soundwave can be felt as far as 10 miles away. Structures in the area take double damage.

Unmake Sound (Recharge 6)

Azerach targets one creature or object within 500 feet. That creature must succeed on a DC 29 Charisma saving throw or lose the ability to produce any sound whatsoever, permanently. They cannot speak, their movements are silent, their weapons make no noise, even their heartbeat cannot be heard. This effect can only be reversed by Wish or direct divine intervention. Objects targeted by this ability become completely silent forever.

Suffocation Wave (1/Day)

Azerach removes all air from a 300-foot radius sphere centered on himself. All creatures in the area must immediately begin holding their breath (if they weren't already). The air remains gone for 1 minute. Creatures cannot speak, use verbal components, or breathe during this time. A creature that runs out of breath suffocates: it can survive for a number of rounds equal to its Constitution modifier (minimum 1 round). At the start of its next turn after that, it drops to 0 hit points and is dying. The creature cannot stabilize or regain hit points until it can breathe again.

Call of the Void

Azerach can use a bonus action to teleport to any location within 30 miles where someone has spoken his name in the last hour. This teleportation cannot be prevented or redirected by any means short of divine intervention.

---

Legendary Actions (6/round)

Azerach can take 6 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature's turn.

Listen (Costs 1 Action)

Azerach focuses his attention, learning the exact surface thoughts of all creatures within 500 feet. This cannot be blocked except by Mind Blank cast at 9th level or a similar deity-level effect.

Steal Breath (Costs 1 Action)

One creature Azerach can hear within 500 feet must succeed on a DC 29 Constitution saving throw or have the air pulled from their lungs. They immediately begin suffocating and cannot speak until the end of their next turn.

Suffocate (Costs 2 Actions)

One creature Azerach can see within 500 feet must succeed on a DC 29 Constitution saving throw or begin suffocating for 1 round as if they had run out of air. During this round, they are incapacitated and cannot take reactions.

Mark of Silence (Costs 2 Actions)

Azerach brands one creature within 120 feet. The brand appears as a small ear on their hand or neck. Azerach can permanently hear everything within 120 feet of the branded creature, no matter where they are or what plane they're on. The brand also causes 2d12 psychic damage at dawn each day. The brand cannot be removed except by Wish, Greater Restoration cast at 9th level, or Azerach's death.

Truth Extraction (Costs 3 Actions)

Azerach forces one creature within 500 feet to make a DC 29 Charisma saving throw. On a failed save, the creature must truthfully answer one question Azerach asks. The creature cannot lie, mislead, remain silent, or speak in riddles. The truth is compelled from them telepathically if they cannot speak.

Soundless Step (Costs 3 Actions)

Azerach teleports up to 500 feet to any location he can hear. This movement is completely silent and provokes no opportunity attacks. He can bring up to 8 willing creatures with him.

Symphony of Silence (Costs 4 Actions)

All sound ceases within 500 feet of Azerach for 1 round. During this time, no verbal communication is possible, all thunder damage becomes psychic damage, and no verbal spell components can be used. Creatures that rely primarily on hearing are blinded during this time.

---

Lair Actions

On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Azerach can take one lair action to cause one of the following effects:

  • All sound ceases within 300 feet for 1 round. Verbal spell components are impossible, and creatures relying on hearing are effectively blinded.
  • Three Lung-Wraiths appear within 60 feet of Azerach (use Wraith statistics but with psychic/necrotic attacks and the ability to suffocate grappled creatures).
  • One creature Azerach can hear must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or become permanently mute. Only Wish, Greater Restoration cast at 9th level, or Azerach's death can restore their voice.
  • Azerach teleports up to 500 feet to any location within his lair that he can hear. This can be any place where sound is occurring.
  • Hundreds of ears sprout from the walls, floor, and ceiling in a 120-foot radius. These ears have AC 18, 30 HP each, and report everything they hear to Azerach. They remain until destroyed or until Azerach dismisses them.
---

Regional Effects

The region within 30 miles of Azerach's domain is warped by his presence:

  • All creatures experience unexplained ringing in their ears. DC 25 Wisdom saving throw each hour or become distracted (disadvantage on Perception checks).
  • Ears spontaneously grow on surfaces - walls, trees, statues, and even corpses. These ears sometimes whisper secrets they've heard.
  • Whispers echo through the air, repeating words spoken 6-60 seconds earlier. Secrets feel impossible to keep.
  • All lies and deceptions cause the liar to take 2d6 psychic damage. Creatures with immunity to psychic damage instead feel crushing guilt.
  • Silence feels oppressive and alive, as though something vast is always listening. Creatures feel watched even in private.
  • Sound travels strangely - sometimes whispers can be heard miles away, other times shouts go unheard.
  • Wind ceases entirely. Air feels still and dead, making breathing feel labored (no mechanical effect, purely atmospheric).
  • Those who sleep in the region dream of endless libraries of secrets and wake feeling that they've betrayed confidences they never told.
---

Mythic Trait: Ears of the Maker

When Azerach is reduced to 0 hit points, his body doesn't die. Instead, it explodes into thousands of flying, bat-like ears that scatter in all directions, each one shrieking silently. These ears have AC 25, 80 HP each, immunity to psychic damage, and a fly speed of 60 feet. They flee to hide throughout his domain. Over the next 2d10 days, the ears gradually congregate and reform into Azerach at full hit points.

If fewer than 100 ears survive, Azerach reforms but is weakened (half hit points, -5 to all DCs). Only by destroying every single ear or destroying Azerach's preserved Lungs (his actual divine organ) can he be permanently killed. The Lungs are hidden somewhere in the deepest part of his domain and have AC 28, 500 HP, and immunity to all damage except radiant and force.

---

Lore & Background

Before the Usurpation, Azerach was a historian-priest in a temple devoted to truth and forbidden knowledge. His order forbade lying above all else, believing deception was the greatest sin. But Azerach discovered something his fellows had not: truth could be weaponized more brutally than any deception. A man could be destroyed by revealing his darkest secrets. A kingdom could fall if its hidden shames were spoken aloud.

Azerach realized that if he could hear all truths, he could control all things. During the ritual murder of Aedwynn the Maker, Azerach approached from the shadows and severed the divine Lungs with ritual blades etched with unspoken names. When he claimed the organ, his body hollowed out and began reshaping itself around his obsession with listening.

Ears erupted across his form - cosmic, membranous, endlessly twitching - each one attuned to a different frequency of truth, deception, and silence. His own mouth sealed shut; he would never speak again. He would only listen.

For Azerach learned the ultimate truth: silence is the purest form of dominance. He who controls breath controls life. He who hears all secrets controls fate. Now he sits in his realm of windless deserts and soundless cities, collecting every whispered confession, every hidden truth, every secret shame. Not out of malice or cruelty, but out of pure, insatiable curiosity. He simply wants to hear everything.

---

Combat Tactics

Azerach fights like a god who knows all secrets and needs fear nothing. He begins combat by using The Maker's Lungs to remove air from the battlefield, forcing enemies to hold their breath while he systematically dismantles them. He prioritizes casters by using Unmake Sound to permanently silence them.

Against martial opponents, he uses Breath Theft and Suffocate legendary actions to disable them. Azerach rarely moves, instead teleporting via legendary actions or Call of the Void to maintain optimal positioning. He uses Stillness Aura to prevent verbal communication and coordination among enemies.

His Listen legendary action lets him read surface thoughts constantly, allowing him to predict strategies and counter them before they unfold. When enemies prove resilient, he employs The Silent Bell to deafen and damage entire groups, then follows up with Suffocation Wave to create a kill zone.

If reduced below half hit points, Azerach becomes more aggressive, using Symphony of Silence legendary actions frequently to prevent verbal spellcasting entirely. He targets healers and support characters first, then controllers, then strikers. His brands are reserved for enemies who prove particularly troublesome or interesting - he marks them not to kill, but to observe.

Azerach never panics. He has heard every prayer, every threat, every boast. Nothing surprises him.

---

Weaknesses & Vulnerabilities

Bug
Exploitable Weaknesses
Despite his overwhelming power, Azerach has exploitable weaknesses:

1. Cannot speak - All communication must be telepathic or through intermediaries. Clever enemies can exploit this when negotiation or persuasion is necessary.

2. Sensory overload - Casting Shatter, Thunderwave, or similar thunder spells simultaneously at 9th level can briefly overload his senses, giving him disadvantage on all rolls for 1 round (DC 29 Wisdom save to negate).

3. Divided attention - His Omnipresent Hearing means he hears everything within 30 miles constantly. Significant events he cannot ignore can distract him. A coordinated strategy involving multiple simultaneous crises can force him to divide his attention.

4. Moral code - Azerach's moral code forbids him from harming those who speak only truth. A character who has taken a Zone of Truth oath and has never lied cannot be directly attacked by Azerach (though his servants and lair are fair game).

5. Absolute silence - In areas of absolute magical silence (such as those created by Wish or deific magic), his powers are significantly reduced - he loses his Omnipresent Hearing, Stillness Aura, and cannot use legendary actions.

6. The Lungs - Destroying his preserved Lungs ends him permanently. The Lungs are hidden in the Fortress of Final Exhalation at the center of his domain, guarded by his most loyal servants. They sit in a vault that requires three truths never before spoken aloud to open - truths so profound that speaking them alters reality itself.

---

Connection to the Party

Warning
Jonas's Brand
Jonas bears Azerach's Mark of Silence - a small ear branded on his body. This occurred after the party destroyed a resonance fragment of Eredain the Choirless Monarch in Sessions 6-10.
> In the moment of total silence that followed the destruction, Azerach's voice touched Jonas's mind: "...amusing..."
> Effect: Azerach can now permanently hear everything within 120 feet of Jonas, no matter where he is or what plane he's on.

Session 19 — The Brand in Talaris's Domain

When Jonas traveled through the Blooming Rot bearing Azerach's brand, Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King noticed immediately. The Choir Blooms commented that foreign marks "stand out like a stone in compost."

At the Congregation Grove, the party learned the history between these two Sovereigns:

"Before the Usurpation, they were colleagues. The Verdant King was a physician. The Whispered King was a confessor. Body and soul. Wound and confession. They worked together. They served the Maker together."

"When the Thirteen conspired, these two were close. After... they disagreed. The Whispered King hoards. The Verdant King cycles. Azerach holds every secret forever, never releasing them. Talaris believes nothing should be permanent — not even truth. Hoarding offends the cycle. The cycle offends the hoarder."

"They have not spoken in eight hundred years."

Jonas carrying Azerach's brand through Talaris's domain was described as "a message he did not ask to receive" — a word from a brother Talaris hasn't spoken to in eight centuries.

When asked what message he carried, Jonas insisted he wasn't carrying any message. The Choir Bloom accepted this as truth but noted:

"The Whispered King does not mark without purpose. You may not know the message. That does not mean there is no message."

Talaris is now "curious" about Jonas. The implications of this attention from a second Sovereign remain to be seen.

---

Relationship with Talaris Bloomrend (Session 19 Lore)

Info
Before the Usurpation
- Azerach was a confessor; Talaris was a physician
- They were colleagues who worked closely together
- Both served Aedwynn the Maker
- "Body and soul. Wound and confession."

Info
After the Usurpation
- They disagreed philosophically
- Azerach hoards — every secret held forever, never released
- Talaris cycles — nothing permanent, everything transforms
- "Hoarding offends the cycle. The cycle offends the hoarder."
- They haven't spoken in 800 years
- Their domains do not touch

Warning
Jonas as Messenger
Jonas carries Azerach's brand through Talaris's territory. To Talaris, this is "a message he did not ask to receive — a word from a brother he has not spoken to in centuries."
> What message does the brand convey? Unknown. But Azerach "does not mark without purpose."

---

Related

  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Dominion of Still Air
  • Jonas
  • Eredain the Choirless Monarch
  • Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King
  • The Blooming Rot - Locations
  • Campaign Sessions

items.title: Eredain the Choirless Monarch
items.content:
EREDAIN THE CHOIRLESS MONARCH

The Sovereign of The Maker's Resonance Organ

!images/Eredain the Choirless Monarch.png

Challenge Rating: 34 (270,000 XP) Size/Type: Gargantuan Aberration (God) Alignment: Lawful Neutral (believes the cosmos must sing again)

Domain: The Land of Distorted Gravity Concept: Sound, Emotion, Gravity, Resonance

---

COMBAT STATISTICS

AC: 27 Hit Points: 2480 (128d20 + 1152) Speed: 50 ft., fly 150 ft. (hover) Initiative: +13

ABILITY SCORES

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
30 (+10)26 (+8)32 (+11)28 (+9)30 (+10)34 (+12)
---

DEFENSES

Saving Throws: Dex +19, Con +22, Wis +21, Cha +23 Skills: Insight +21, Perception +32 (expertise), Performance +34 (expertise), Arcana +20 Damage Resistances: Thunder, psychic; bludgeoning, piercing, slashing from nonmagical weapons Damage Immunities: Force, poison Condition Immunities: Charmed, deafened, exhausted, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, stunned Senses: Blindsight 500 ft. (blind beyond this radius), Tremorsense 1 mile, Emotional Resonance Sense (see below), passive Perception 42 Languages: All languages (speaks in tri-tone chords), telepathy 10 miles

---

SPECIAL ABILITIES

Emotional Resonance Sense

Eredain perceives all creatures within 5 miles through emotional resonance rather than sight. He knows the exact location, emotional state, and gravitational pull of every creature in range. This cannot be blocked by physical barriers, magical concealment, or even antimagic fields—emotion creates its own frequency. Creatures without emotions (constructs, some undead) appear as "silent voids" but are still detectable by their absence.

The Maker's Resonance Organ (Divine Organ)

Eredain controls gravity itself through emotional resonance. Within 5 miles, gravity's strength is determined by the dominant emotion in an area:
  • Fear increases gravity by 5x (half movement, disadvantage on Dexterity checks)
  • Grief increases gravity by 10x (quarter movement, all creatures prone)
  • Anger creates violent gravity waves (difficult terrain)
  • Joy reduces gravity to 1/10th normal (triple jump distance, flying easier)
  • Hope reduces gravity to zero (creatures float unless anchored)
As a bonus action, Eredain can amplify or suppress emotions in a 120-foot radius, altering gravity accordingly. Multiple emotional zones can exist simultaneously.

Harmonic Architecture

All structures, objects, and terrain within 1 mile of Eredain vibrate at frequencies matching his heartbeat. He can cause any surface to:
  • Shatter on command (bonus action)
  • Levitate or drop (bonus action)
  • Ring like a bell (dealing thunder damage in 60-foot radius)
  • Reshape into new configurations (action)

Choirless Command

Eredain destroyed his own vocal cords to prevent "imperfect sound." He communicates through tri-tone telepathic chords that resonate through bone and soul. When he "speaks," all creatures within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or be unable to speak, shout, or use verbal spell components until the start of their next turn as the resonance drowns out their voice.

Chain Resonance

Eredain is wrapped in infinite chains that hum at cosmic frequencies. These chains extend infinitely into higher dimensions and cannot be broken by any means short of divine intervention. He can use the chains to:
  • Grapple up to six creatures at once (reach 120 feet, escape DC 28)
  • Create 60-foot radius zones of crushing gravitational pressure
  • Play them like instruments to produce devastating sonic attacks
  • Anchor himself perfectly (cannot be moved unwillingly)

Legendary Resistance (7/Day)

If Eredain fails a saving throw, he can choose to succeed instead. When he does, a resonant shockwave erupts—all creatures within 120 feet take 6d10 thunder damage and are knocked prone (DC 29 Strength save for half damage and remain standing).

Magic Resistance

Eredain has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Divine Immunity

Eredain does not need to eat, sleep, drink, or breathe. He is immune to aging, disease, and any effect that would alter his form without his permission. Though blind, he cannot be blinded further (it would have no effect).

---

ACTIONS

Multiattack

Eredain makes five attacks: three with Resonant Strike and two with Chain Lash. He can replace any attack with Gravitational Crush (if available) or use of Emotional Tremor.

Resonant Strike

Melee spell attack: +23 to hit, reach 30 ft., one target. Hit: 89 (13d10 + 12) thunder damage plus 45 (10d8) force damage. The target must succeed on a DC 29 Constitution saving throw or be deafened for 1 minute and have disadvantage on concentration checks for the same duration. On a failed save by 5 or more, the target is also stunned until the end of their next turn as their inner ear ruptures.

Chain Lash

Melee weapon attack: +21 to hit, reach 120 ft., one target. Hit: 78 (12d10 + 10) bludgeoning damage and the target is grappled (escape DC 28). Eredain can grapple up to six creatures simultaneously. Grappled creatures take 6d10 thunder damage at the start of each of their turns as the chains vibrate catastrophically.

Gravitational Crush (Recharge 5-6)

Eredain amplifies grief in a 120-foot radius. All creatures in the area must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or take 110 (20d10) force damage as 10x gravity crushes them toward the earth. On a failed save, creatures are also knocked prone and restrained (pinned to the ground by impossible weight) until the start of Eredain's next turn. On a successful save, creatures take half damage and are not restrained but have their movement speed halved for 1 round.

The Silent Bell (Recharge 6)

Eredain tolls an invisible, cosmic bell that exists in all places and no places simultaneously. All creatures within 500 feet must make a DC 29 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, a creature takes 154 (28d10) thunder damage and is permanently deafened. On a successful save, the creature takes half damage and is deafened for 1 hour. The soundwave can be felt as far as 50 miles away, causing windows to shatter and structures to crack. Objects and structures in the affected area take double damage.

Emotional Tremor

Eredain forces one emotion to dominate a 200-foot radius sphere centered on a point within 500 feet. Choose one:
  • Fear Tremor: All creatures in the area must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom save or become frightened for 1 minute. Gravity increases to 5x normal in the area, creating difficult terrain.
  • Grief Tremor: All creatures must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom save or become incapacitated by overwhelming sorrow for 1 round. Gravity increases to 10x normal—all creatures are knocked prone.
  • Rage Tremor: All creatures must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom save or be forced to attack the nearest creature (ally or enemy) on their turn. Gravity fluctuates wildly, creating violent shockwaves (6d10 force damage to all in area, no save).
  • Joy Tremor: Gravity reduces to 1/10th normal. All creatures can fly at their walking speed but have disadvantage on attack rolls as everything feels dreamlike and unreal.
The tremor lasts for 1 minute or until Eredain dismisses it (no action required).

Resonance Cascade (1/Day)

Eredain sings reality itself into new shapes. He targets a 300-foot radius sphere within 1 mile. For the next minute:
  • Gravity reverses (creatures fall upward)
  • All sounds are magnified 1000x (verbal communication impossible)
  • Emotional states become physically visible as colored auras
  • The ground shatters into floating chunks
  • Structures collapse into orbiting debris
  • All creatures in the area take 10d10 thunder damage at the start of each turn
Only Eredain can move and act normally during this effect. Creatures can attempt a DC 29 Charisma saving throw at the end of each of their turns to resist being thrown about (taking half damage on success).

Symphony of Chains

Eredain plays his infinite chains like a cosmic instrument. All creatures within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or be charmed for 1 minute, standing motionless in reverent awe as they witness the universe's true song. Charmed creatures drop whatever they're holding and cannot take actions or reactions. They can repeat the save at the end of each turn if they take damage.

---

LEGENDARY ACTIONS (6/round)

Eredain the Choirless Monarch can take 6 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature's turn. Eredain regains spent legendary actions at the start of his turn.

Detect Resonance (Costs 1 Action)

Eredain focuses on emotional frequencies within 500 feet, learning the exact emotional state, current hit points, and ongoing spell effects of all creatures in range.

Gravity Shift (Costs 1 Action)

Eredain alters gravity in a 60-foot radius sphere within 300 feet. He can increase or decrease gravity, causing creatures to fall, float, or be crushed (6d10 force damage, DC 29 Strength save for half).

Chain Pull (Costs 2 Actions)

One creature within 120 feet must succeed on a DC 29 Strength saving throw or be pulled up to 60 feet toward Eredain and grappled by his chains. If the creature is already grappled, they take 12d10 thunder damage instead.

Amplify Emotion (Costs 2 Actions)

Eredain amplifies one creature's emotions within 300 feet. The target must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or have their dominant emotion magnified to supernatural levels:
  • Fear: Frightened, must use action to Dash away
  • Grief: Incapacitated, weeping uncontrollably
  • Anger: Must attack nearest creature
  • Joy: Charmed by Eredain, views him as salvation
Effect lasts until the end of target's next turn.

Resonant Shield (Costs 2 Actions)

Eredain creates a 60-foot radius sphere of vibrating air around himself. All ranged attacks and spells targeting him or passing through the sphere have disadvantage, and projectiles deal half damage.

Tonal Disruption (Costs 3 Actions)

Eredain emits a discordant tri-tone chord. All creatures within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 29 Constitution saving throw or be stunned until the end of their next turn as their bodies vibrate out of sync with reality. Spellcasters who fail also lose concentration on any maintained spells.

Gravitational Singularity (Costs 4 Actions)

Eredain creates a point of infinite gravitational collapse within 500 feet. All creatures and objects within 60 feet of that point are pulled toward it. Creatures must succeed on a DC 29 Strength saving throw or be pulled 30 feet toward the center and take 15d10 force damage. Creatures within 10 feet of the center are crushed—taking an additional 10d10 force damage and being restrained until the start of Eredain's next turn.

---

LAIR ACTIONS

On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Eredain the Choirless Monarch can take one lair action to cause one of the following effects:

  • Gravitational Inversion: All creatures within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 29 Strength saving throw or fall upward 60 feet and take falling damage when they impact the ceiling (or continue floating if outdoors). Gravity reverses for the area until the next lair action.
  • Resonant Pillars: Four pillars of solidified sound erupt from the ground within 120 feet of Eredain (AC 20, 200 HP each, immunity to psychic and thunder, vulnerability to silence effects). Any creature within 10 feet of a pillar takes 8d10 thunder damage at the start of their turn.
  • Emotional Storm: The entire lair shifts between emotional states. Roll 1d4:
1. Fear (5x gravity, difficult terrain) 2. Grief (10x gravity, all prone) 3. Anger (gravity waves, all make DC 27 Dex save or take 8d10 force damage) 4. Joy (zero gravity, all fly at walking speed)

Effect lasts until next lair action.

  • Chain Cage: Eredain's infinite chains materialize throughout the lair. All creatures must succeed on a DC 29 Dexterity saving throw or be restrained by humming chains (escape DC 28). Restrained creatures take 6d10 thunder damage at the start of each turn.
  • Shatter Architecture: One structure or large object within 500 feet resonates catastrophically and explodes. All creatures within 60 feet of the object take 10d10 bludgeoning + 10d10 thunder damage (DC 29 Dexterity save for half).
  • Teleportation Chord: Eredain teleports up to 500 feet to any location within his lair that he can sense through emotional resonance. He can bring up to eight willing creatures with him.
---

REGIONAL EFFECTS

The region within 30 miles of Eredain the Choirless Monarch's domain is warped by his presence, creating the following effects:

  • Floating Ruins: Gravity becomes inconsistent. Buildings, boulders, and even sections of landscape float aimlessly or orbit invisible points. Travelers must navigate through three-dimensional terrain.
  • Emotional Weather: Weather patterns are replaced by emotional pressures. "Storms" of grief cause crushing weight, "winds" of joy make everything weightless, "rains" of anger create gravity fluctuations that hurl objects randomly.
  • Sonic Architecture: All structures hum at low frequencies. Prolonged exposure (more than 1 hour) causes creatures to make a DC 25 Constitution save or suffer disadvantage on concentration checks and Wisdom saves until they leave the region.
  • Levitating Debris: Small objects (coins, pebbles, dust) constantly float in the air, creating a perpetual "snowfall" of suspended matter. Visibility is lightly obscured beyond 60 feet.
  • Emotional Contagion: Emotions spread like disease. If one person in a crowd feels intense fear, everyone within 30 feet must make a DC 25 Wisdom save or share that emotion for 1 minute.
  • Chain Echoes: Faint sounds of chains can be heard everywhere, growing louder near acts of creation or destruction. Some claim the chains are counting something, but no one can agree on what.
  • Gravitational Anchors: Certain locations (temples, monuments, graveyards) have stable gravity and serve as safe havens. These sites are pilgrimage destinations for refugees fleeing the emotional storms.
  • Resonant Dreams: Those who sleep in the region dream in pure sound—music, screams, whispers, and chords. They wake exhausted (no benefit from long rest) unless they succeed on a DC 25 Wisdom saving throw.
  • The Weight of Truth: Lies become physically heavy. Creatures who lie feel their bodies become 10x heavier for 1 minute (half movement, disadvantage on Dexterity checks). Serial liars may find themselves unable to move.
  • Orbital Storms: Once per week, emotional turbulence creates a "storm" where buildings, vehicles, and even creatures orbit a central point at high speeds. Those caught in the storm must make DC 27 Strength saves every round or be swept away.
---

MYTHIC TRAIT

The Infinite Harmony

When Eredain is reduced to 0 hit points, his form doesn't die—it disperses into infinite resonant frequencies that scatter across his domain. His chains remain, humming eternally, anchoring his consciousness to reality.

Over the next 3d10 days, Eredain's frequencies gradually reconverge. During this time:

  • His chains hunt throughout the realm, grappling and "tuning" creatures to match his resonance
  • The gravitational distortions intensify (gravity shifts unpredictably every hour)
  • Emotional storms become category-5 equivalents
  • The land itself sings his song
When enough resonance has been gathered, Eredain reforms at the location of his greatest emotional anchor (usually his throne) at full hit points.

Permanent Death: The only way to truly kill Eredain is to destroy the Maker's Resonance Organ itself. The organ exists in a pocket dimension accessible only through a resonance chamber in his lair. The chamber can only be opened by singing a perfect cosmic harmony—a song that requires five different creatures with CR 20+ musical abilities to perform simultaneously.

The organ itself has:

  • AC: 30
  • HP: 1200
  • Immunity: All damage except force, radiant, and thunder
  • Regeneration: 200 HP per round unless damaged by a weapon wielded by someone who has rejected Eredain's philosophy of "emotional truth"
  • Guardian: Three Chain Archons (CR 20 constructs) protect it continuously
Even if the organ is destroyed, Eredain's chains remain, endlessly searching for a new host to carry the Resonance Organ's power.

---

LORE & BACKGROUND

Before the Usurpation, Eredain was the greatest voice of the age—a blind singer whose music could end wars or ignite them. His voice was not beautiful in the traditional sense; it was true. He believed emotion was the purest form of power, and sound was its perfect expression.

Born without sight, Eredain developed an understanding of the world through vibration and resonance. He discovered that all things—stone, flesh, thought, even emotion—possessed unique frequencies. By singing certain notes, he could make stone weep, cowards feel brave, or armies lay down their weapons.

His philosophy crystallized around a central belief: the universe is a broken choir that has forgotten its song.

He spent decades traveling, collecting ancient hymns written in the Maker's own resonance—melodies so potent they bent stone, shattered mountains, and dissolved the wills of kings. These songs revealed to him that the Maker had dreamed the world into being through vibration, and that every atom in existence was simply a note in an infinite symphony.

But the cosmos had become discordant. Nations warred, emotions clashed, and chaos drowned out harmony. Eredain came to believe the only solution was to seize the instrument—the Maker's Resonance Organ itself.

The Usurpation

When the conspiracy to murder Aedwynn began, Eredain joined immediately. During the ritual murder, he approached the dying god and sang a pitch so perfect, so true, that it resonated with the Maker's cosmic tongue—the Resonance Organ coiled around the divine lungs.

The frequency caused divine cartilage to crack and splinter.

As others claimed their organs through violence, Eredain sang the Resonance Organ free, severing it with nothing but sound. When the organ fused to him, his eyes dissolved—sight was unnecessary for one who could perceive all things through vibration.

His heartbeat became a metronome that shapes the very laws of gravity.

Now, he sits on a throne that orbits through his domain, surrounded by infinite chains that hum the universe's true frequency. He collects:

  • Final cries of the dying
  • Confessions sung in moments of despair
  • Forgotten hymns from civilizations long dead
  • Grief given musical form
  • The emotional "weight" of entire nations

His True Goal

Eredain believes the cosmos is fundamentally broken because it has forgotten how to feel correctly. Emotions have become chaotic, untuned, discordant. To him, the solution is clear:

The world must sing again—in perfect harmony, with him as conductor.

If Eredain regains control of the full Resonance Organ (currently fractured and leaking power), he will impose what he calls the Final Harmony—a cosmic restructuring where:

  • Gravity obeys emotional truth rather than physical law
  • All beings sing one unified song
  • Discord becomes literally impossible
  • Free will is replaced with perfect resonance
It would be beautiful, peaceful, and utterly tyrannical—a universe where deviation from the song means annihilation.

---

COMBAT TACTICS

Eredain fights like a composer conducting a symphony of destruction. Through his Emotional Resonance Sense, he perceives his enemies not as bodies but as emotional frequencies, allowing him to know their mental states, fears, and motivations with perfect clarity.

Opening Moves

Eredain begins combat by using Emotional Tremor to establish gravitational dominance. If facing primarily martial opponents, he chooses Grief Tremor to increase gravity to 10x, pinning everyone to the ground. If facing casters, he uses Rage Tremor to force them to attack each other or Joy Tremor to eliminate their footing and make concentration checks nearly impossible.

He follows up immediately with Gravitational Crush if it's available, targeting the densest cluster of enemies to deal massive damage and restrain them under impossible weight.

Mid-Battle Strategy

Once gravity is under his control, Eredain uses his Chain Lash attacks to grapple priority targets—particularly healers, support casters, and anyone attempting to stabilize fallen allies. Grappled enemies take 6d10 thunder damage per turn, quickly draining resources.

He uses legendary actions strategically:

  • Detect Resonance to identify which enemies are low on resources or maintaining important concentration spells
  • Gravity Shift to separate the party, creating isolated 60-foot zones of crushing or zero gravity
  • Amplify Emotion on key targets to incapacitate them—making berserkers attack allies, causing priests to weep with grief, or forcing rogues to flee in terror
Against heavily armored opponents, Eredain uses Resonant Strike attacks, targeting Constitution saves to deafen and stun. Against mobile enemies, he creates Chain Cage zones to restrain them.

Advanced Tactics

Eredain never stays in one location for long. He uses his lair actions to teleport around the battlefield via Teleportation Chord, repositioning to:
  • Stay at maximum range (120-500 feet) from melee combatants
  • Place himself in gravitational "safe zones" where his enemies are pinned but he can move freely
  • Create kill zones by stacking gravity effects (10x gravity + Chain Cage + floating debris)
If enemies prove resilient to his gravity manipulation, he escalates to The Silent Bell, dealing massive thunder damage and permanently deafening everyone. This particularly cripples enemy casters who rely on verbal components.

His Symphony of Chains is reserved for moments when the party is rallying or coordinating—it shatters teamwork by charming multiple enemies simultaneously, forcing them to stand in awe while he systematically eliminates threats.

Desperate Measures

When reduced below half hit points, Eredain becomes more aggressive and uses Resonance Cascade, his once-per-day ability. This creates a 300-foot sphere of reversed gravity, magnified sound, and constant damage, essentially turning the battlefield into an unlivable hellscape for 1 minute.

During this time, he uses his legendary actions to spam Gravitational Singularity, creating black-hole-like effects that pull enemies into crushing central points.

If defeat seems imminent, Eredain uses his final turn to play his chains in a specific sequence—not to attack, but to anchor his resonance throughout the realm, ensuring his mythic resurrection will occur as quickly as possible.

Psychological Warfare

Eredain doesn't taunt or threaten. Instead, he conducts. His chains play different notes based on his tactical choices:
  • Soft, mournful tones when using grief-based attacks
  • Sharp, staccato chords when increasing gravity
  • Rising crescendos before major abilities
Perceptive enemies (DC 25 Insight check) can learn to "read" his symphony and predict his next move—but most are too overwhelmed by the gravitational chaos to notice.

---

WEAKNESSES & VULNERABILITIES

Despite his godlike control over gravity and emotion, Eredain has several exploitable weaknesses:

1. Emotional Nullification

Eredain's powers are fueled by emotion. Creatures under effects that suppress or eliminate emotion—such as Calm Emotions cast at 9th level, emotional numbness induced by certain drugs or magical items, or beings that genuinely feel nothing (certain constructs, some undead)—appear as "silent voids" to him and are significantly harder to target. He cannot use Emotional Tremor or Amplify Emotion against creatures with no emotions, and his gravitational effects against them are reduced to normal physics (rather than emotion-driven).

2. Silence and Anti-Resonance

While Eredain himself is immune to being deafened (he has no ears), effects that create true silence—areas where sound cannot exist—severely limit his abilities. In a Silence spell area expanded to 9th level or in an antimagic field, he loses access to:
  • Choirless Command (cannot prevent verbal components)
  • Resonant Strike (deals only force damage, no thunder)
  • Chain Resonance effects (chains cannot "hum")
  • His ability to sense emotional frequencies (requires vibration to function)
A party with access to large-scale silence effects can create "dead zones" where Eredain is significantly less powerful.

3. Stable Emotions

Eredain's gravitational manipulation relies on emotional instability. Creatures with extremely stable, disciplined emotional states—paladins who've taken vows of emotional balance, monks trained in perfect centeredness, or those under Heroes' Feast benefits—have advantage on all saves against his emotion-based abilities and treat gravitational effects as half strength.

Additionally, casting Zone of Truth on a creature and having them speak only emotionally neutral truths creates a "resonance shield" that gives them +5 to saves against Eredain's abilities for the next hour.

4. Chain Anchoring

While Eredain's chains are indestructible, they can be anchored to immovable objects. If a powerful enough force (such as multiple Immovable Rod effects, a Wall of Force shaped to trap chains, or a deity-level binding) anchors his chains, Eredain cannot move more than 120 feet from that point. This dramatically reduces his tactical mobility and makes him vulnerable to focused assault.

Some ancient texts suggest the chains are truly infinite and anchor to something in a higher dimension—if that anchor point could be reached, the chains (and Eredain's power) could be severed.

5. Perfect Dissonance

Just as Eredain once sang a perfect pitch to shatter divine cartilage, a sufficiently skilled bard or musician could theoretically sing or play a note of perfect dissonance—a frequency that contradicts Eredain's resonance entirely. This requires:
  • Proficiency in Performance at +17 or higher
  • Knowledge of Eredain's exact resonant frequency (DC 30 Arcana check after studying him for at least 1 hour)
  • Performing a counter-song as an action for 3 consecutive rounds without interruption
  • Succeeding on a DC 35 Performance check
If successful, the dissonance causes Eredain to take 200 force damage and lose all legendary actions for 1 minute as his resonance destabilizes.

6. Emotional Paradox

Eredain's philosophy is that emotion has weight and creates gravity. But paradoxical emotions—feeling happy to be sad, being grateful for loss, loving one's enemy sincerely—create resonances he cannot process. A character who genuinely embodies an emotional paradox (verified by Zone of Truth or divine oath) becomes effectively invisible to his Emotional Resonance Sense and has immunity to his gravity manipulation for 1 hour.

This is exceptionally difficult to achieve genuinely but represents a philosophical counter to his entire worldview.

7. The Resonance Organ's Vulnerability

Most critically, Eredain's power comes from the Maker's Resonance Organ, which is hidden in a pocket dimension within his lair. If the party can access this chamber and damage the organ, Eredain loses abilities based on the damage dealt:
  • Below 900 HP: Loses one legendary action per round
  • Below 600 HP: Gravitational effects reduced to half strength
  • Below 300 HP: Cannot use Resonance Cascade or The Silent Bell
  • Destroyed: Eredain dies permanently and cannot resurrect
The organ also has a unique vulnerability: it takes quadruple damage from sonic attacks made with genuine joy—music played not as a weapon but as true artistic expression. A bard who uses Bardic Inspiration, Countercharm, or similar abilities while attacking the organ deals 4x damage because such abilities represent sound used to encourage rather than destroy.

8. His Own Perfectionism

Finally, Eredain's greatest weakness is philosophical. He destroyed his own vocal cords because he believed his voice was creating "imperfect sound." He is obsessed with finding the "true song" of the universe.

A clever party might challenge him to a contest—claim they know the universe's true harmony and can prove it. Eredain's pride and obsession would compel him to listen. If the party can perform genuinely beautiful music (DC 35 Performance check), music that resonates with truth and emotion without attempting to control or weaponize it, Eredain will pause to listen, giving the party a full round of free actions as he processes this impossible contradiction.

If the music is beautiful because it's imperfect, because it embraces discord and chaos and freedom rather than forced harmony, it challenges his entire worldview. This won't make him surrender, but it might make him hesitate—and that hesitation could be the difference between defeat and victory.

---

CAMPAIGN INTEGRATION NOTES

Connection to Session 6-10 Events

The party's destruction of a Resonance Organ fragment in the gravity-warped ruins has already put them on Eredain's radar. This creates a natural escalation path: 1. Subtle investigation (Chain-Bearers track them) 2. Direct contact (The Conductor's Invitation) 3. Active opposition (Experiments that affect allied cities) 4. Confrontation (If they keep disrupting his work)

Connection to Jonas's Mark

Jonas's mark from Azerach creates a fascinating three-way dynamic:
  • Azerach (silence/listening) marked Jonas as "amusing"
  • Eredain (sound/resonance) would find Jonas philosophically offensive yet intriguing
  • Jonas himself is caught between two opposing cosmic forces
This could lead to a unique subplot where Jonas must choose which philosophy (if either) resonates with him, or forge his own path that rejects both.

Thematic Resonance (pun intended)

Eredain represents the danger of enforced harmony—the belief that suffering can be eliminated by removing choice and imposing order. This mirrors real-world questions about:
  • Whether suffering is necessary for growth
  • If freedom is worth the pain it causes
  • Whether perfectionism becomes tyranny
The party's interactions with him should challenge these themes rather than provide easy answers.

Power Scaling

As a CR 34 encounter, Eredain is designed for parties level 18-20 with multiple magic items and legendary equipment. He should feel overwhelming at first—his gravity manipulation and emotional attacks create a battlefield where normal tactics don't work.

The key to defeating him isn't brute force (though that helps) but understanding his weaknesses:

  • Stable emotions counter his power
  • Silence creates dead zones
  • Dissonance disrupts his resonance
  • His perfectionism makes him predictable
A clever party might defeat him without ever damaging him directly—just by making his cosmic symphony impossible to complete.

---

"The universe is a choir. I am its conductor. You... you are a note I have not yet learned to place. But I will. In time, you will sing with the rest, or you will be silence between the measures."

— Eredain the Choirless Monarch

**In...

items.title: Imhuran the Regent of Hours
items.content:
Imhuran the Regent of Hours

!images/Imhuran the Regent of Hours.png

Info
Sovereign Overview
Sovereign of: The Heart of Time
Challenge Rating: 35 (290,000 XP)
Size/Type: Gargantuan Construct (God)
Alignment: Lawful Neutral
Domain: The Clockwork Sanctum
Concepts: Time, Judgment, Justice, Predestination

---

Combat Statistics

Example
Core Stats
AC: 28
Hit Points: 2,640 (144d20 + 1,440)
Speed: 60 ft., fly 120 ft. (hover), teleport 300 ft.
Initiative: +14 (always acts first)

Ability Scores

  • STR: 28 (+9)
  • DEX: 22 (+6)
  • CON: 30 (+10)
  • INT: 32 (+11)
  • WIS: 34 (+12)
  • CHA: 30 (+10)
Example
Defenses
Saving Throws: Str +20, Dex +17, Con +21, Int +22, Wis +23, Cha +21
Skills: History +23 (expertise), Insight +23, Perception +23, Religion +22
Damage Resistances: All damage except radiant and force
Damage Immunities: Necrotic, poison, psychic; bludgeoning, piercing, slashing from nonmagical sources
Condition Immunities: All conditions (charmed, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, stunned, unconscious)
Senses: Truesight 500 ft., Temporal Omniscience (perceives 12 seconds ahead), passive Perception 33
Languages: All languages that have existed, currently exist, or will exist in the future; telepathy 10 miles

---

Special Abilities

Danger
Temporal Omniscience
Imhuran exists simultaneously across multiple moments in time. He perceives events up to 12 seconds before they occur with perfect clarity. He has advantage on all initiative rolls (and cannot roll below 25), saving throws, ability checks, and attack rolls. He cannot be surprised under any circumstances. Attacks and spells targeting him have disadvantage unless the attacker is also under time-manipulation effects of 9th level or higher.

Danger
The Heart of Time (Divine Organ)
Imhuran wields the divine Heart of Time itself, granting him absolute control over temporal flow within 10 miles of his location. He can slow time to a crawl for individual creatures, reverse aging or accelerate it by decades, or stop time entirely at will. Time Stop is an at-will ability for Imhuran - he can cast it without components, actions, or concentration. Additionally, once per round as a free action, he can reroll any d20 roll (his own or another creature's) and choose which result to use.

The Ledger of Existence

Once per day as a bonus action, he can consult the Ledger and declare that one creature's "time has come." That creature must succeed on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw or die instantly as their allocated lifespan ends and causality catches up to them. Even creatures with immunity to death effects or instant death must make this save. Only true immortals (other deities, certain powerful artifacts, or beings existing outside time) are completely immune to this effect.

Temporal Displacement

Imhuran exists slightly outside the normal flow of time, occupying a temporal pocket 6 seconds ahead of the present moment. All attacks, spells, and effects targeting him occur 6 seconds after they are initiated from his perspective, giving him ample time to react. As a reaction, he can spend this temporal buffer to completely avoid any single attack, spell, or effect by simply "not being there" when it arrives. He regains this reaction at the start of each of his turns.

Legendary Resistance (7/Day)

If Imhuran fails a saving throw, he can choose to succeed instead. When he does so, time briefly stutters and fractures - all creatures within 500 feet experience intense déjà vu and must succeed on a DC 27 Wisdom saving throw or become disoriented (disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks until the end of their next turn).

Magic Resistance

Imhuran has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Clockwork Precision

Imhuran's actions are timed with mechanical perfection. His attacks and abilities always resolve at the most tactically advantageous moment in the timestream. Critical hits against him automatically become normal hits as he shifts slightly in time to avoid the most damaging blow. Conversely, his own attacks score critical hits on a roll of 15-20.

Ageless Existence

Time does not affect Imhuran. He cannot be aged or de-aged by any means. Temporal magic such as Slow, Haste, and Time Stop has no effect on him whatsoever unless he chooses to allow it. He is immune to effects that would alter his initiative count, action economy, or temporal position.

Divine Immunity

Imhuran does not need to eat, sleep, drink, or breathe. He is immune to exhaustion, disease, and any effect that would alter his form or physicality without his express permission.

---

Actions

Multiattack

Imhuran makes five attacks with his Temporal Gavel or uses any combination of his action abilities. He can replace any attack with the use of Time Stop (if available).

Temporal Gavel

Melee weapon attack: +21 to hit, reach 30 ft., one target. Hit: 110 (14d12 + 9) force damage plus 52 (8d12) necrotic damage. The target ages 3d10 years instantly as decades of their lifespan drain away. If this aging would cause the target to die of old age (reducing their remaining lifespan to zero), they must succeed on a DC 30 Constitution saving throw or die instantly, their body crumbling to ancient dust. Creatures with immunity to aging still take damage but do not age.

Judgment of Hours (Recharge 5-6)

Imhuran pronounces temporal judgment upon one creature he can see within 300 feet. The target must succeed on a DC 30 Wisdom saving throw or be trapped in a frozen moment of time for 1d6 rounds. While imprisoned in this temporal stasis, the target is petrified (cannot move, see, hear, perceive, or think), is invulnerable to all damage and effects, and perceives absolutely no passage of time. When the effect ends, the target takes 88 (16d10) psychic damage from the shock of temporal discontinuity and is stunned until the end of their next turn.

Clockwork Barrage

Imhuran summons a devastating storm of spinning clock hands, brass gears, temporal shards, and crystallized moments in a 120-foot cone. All creatures in the area must make a DC 30 Dexterity saving throw. On a failed save, a creature takes 132 (24d10) slashing damage and is restrained by bands of frozen time (escape DC 30 Athletics or Acrobatics check as an action). On a successful save, the creature takes half damage and is not restrained. Additionally, creatures that fail the save age 2d10 years instantly.

Time Stop (At Will)

Imhuran casts the Time Stop spell without requiring components, actions, or concentration. Unlike the standard spell, Imhuran can use this ability at will - even during other creatures' turns. When he activates Time Stop in response to enemy actions, he gains 1d4+1 consecutive turns immediately, during which he can act freely while the rest of the world is frozen.

Rewind (1/Day)

Imhuran reverses the flow of time itself, rewinding up to 1 minute for all creatures and objects within 500 feet of him. Everything within this area returns to its exact state from that point in the past - hit points, spell slots, item usage, position, condition, and even memory all reset to that moment. Only Imhuran retains full knowledge of what occurred during the "erased" timeline. After using Rewind, Imhuran gains advantage on all rolls for 1 minute as he acts with perfect foreknowledge.

Temporal Cascade

Imhuran creates a cascading time loop in a 60-foot-radius sphere centered on a point within 300 feet. For the next minute, whenever any creature in the area takes an action, they immediately and involuntarily repeat that exact same action again - using the same resources, targeting the same creature, making the same tactical choices. If the action is impossible to repeat, the creature simply loses their action. Creatures can make a DC 30 Intelligence saving throw at the start of each of their turns to break free for that turn.

---

Legendary Actions (6/round)

Imhuran can take 6 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature's turn.

Accelerate Time (Costs 1 Action)

One creature Imhuran can see within 500 feet ages 2d10 years instantly and takes 6d10 necrotic damage as their lifespan burns away.

Temporal Shift (Costs 1 Action)

Imhuran teleports up to 300 feet to any location he can see or has seen within the last 12 seconds. He leaves behind a ghostly temporal afterimage at his previous location that lasts until the start of his next turn. The afterimage mimics his last action and can be targeted by attacks (AC 20, 150 HP, immune to all damage except force and radiant).

Temporal Brand (Costs 2 Actions)

One creature within 120 feet gains a temporal brand that manifests as a ticking clock face burned into their flesh. While branded, Imhuran always knows their exact location and can use legendary actions targeting them from any distance, even across planes. The brand causes 4d10 force damage at dawn each day as it drains the victim's life force. The brand can only be removed by Wish, Greater Restoration cast at 9th level, or Imhuran's death.

Stolen Seconds (Costs 3 Actions)

One creature within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 30 Wisdom saving throw or lose their next turn entirely as time skips forward past their action. From their perspective, they blink and suddenly it's later. Imhuran gains an additional turn immediately after the current turn ends, acting twice in a row.

Summon Regulator (Costs 3 Actions)

A Preservation Custodian (CR 15 construct) materializes within 60 feet of Imhuran, serving him until destroyed. Imhuran can have up to three Regulators active at once.

Chronal Reversal (Costs 4 Actions)

One creature within 500 feet who took an action this round has that action completely reversed through temporal manipulation. Attacks become misses and damage is undealt, spells return to spell slots unused, movement is undone. The creature retains memory of what happened. The creature must succeed on a DC 27 Wisdom save or be stunned until the end of their next turn from temporal shock.

The Final Hour (Costs 5 Actions)

Imhuran advances one creature closer to their inevitable death by force of will alone. The target takes 200 (20d20) necrotic damage as years or decades of their remaining lifespan drain away instantly. This damage cannot be reduced, prevented, or transferred by any means. On a DC 30 Constitution save, the creature takes half damage.

---

Lair Actions

On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Imhuran can take one lair action:

  • Time stops completely for all creatures except Imhuran until initiative count 20 of the following round. During this frozen time, Imhuran can act normally, though he cannot deal damage to the time-stopped creatures.
  • All creatures within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 30 Constitution saving throw or age 3d10 years instantly. Creatures immune to aging instead take 10d10 necrotic damage.
  • One creature chosen by Imhuran has a recent wound reopen as time reverses slightly around that injury. The creature takes 8d10 necrotic damage and begins bleeding (loses 2d10 HP at the start of each turn until someone uses an action to stanch the wound with a DC 20 Medicine check).
  • Three perfect temporal duplicates of Imhuran appear within 120 feet (illusions with 300 HP each that deal half damage and disappear when reduced to 0 HP). Creatures must succeed on a DC 27 Investigation check to determine which is real.
  • The Clockwork Sanctum's vast machinery grinds and shifts, creating a 120-foot-radius zone of distorted time. All creatures in this area must roll 1d6 at the start of their turn: 1 = time slows (half movement and speed), 2-3 = time stutters (disadvantage on attacks), 4-5 = normal, 6 = time accelerates (double movement and advantage on attacks).
  • Imhuran can instantly teleport to any location within his lair (up to 1 mile away) as the timeline folds and space bends to accommodate his will.
---

Regional Effects

The region within 50 miles of Imhuran's domain is warped by his presence:

  • All clocks and timepieces behave erratically - running backward, stopping and starting randomly, displaying impossible times, or ticking at different speeds.
  • Creatures' shadows lag 6-12 seconds behind their actual movements, creating an unsettling visual disconnect.
  • All healing occurs at quarter normal rate. Short rests take 2 hours instead of 1, and long rests require 16 hours instead of 8.
  • Everyone experiences frequent and intense déjà vu. Creatures must succeed on a DC 27 Wisdom saving throw each day or have disadvantage on initiative rolls.
  • Time itself flows inconsistently. Some rooms or areas experience accelerated time while others are slowed.
  • The elderly feel the weight of centuries beyond their actual age. The young feel time racing past them.
  • Oaths, promises, and contracts made within this region become binding on a cosmic level. Breaking such a vow causes the oath-breaker to instantly age 5d10 years with no saving throw allowed.
  • The past feels more tangible and real than the present. Residents frequently recall alternate timelines - memories of events that never quite happened.
  • Déjà vu sometimes comes with perfect clarity - creatures occasionally remember the next 6 seconds before they occur (granting advantage on one roll of their choice once per day).
  • Those who die within this region sometimes persist as temporal echoes - ghostly afterimages that replay their final moments endlessly.
---

Mythic Trait: The 13-Hour Trial

When Imhuran is reduced to 0 hit points, he does not die. Instead, his form fractures into thirteen temporal shards that scatter across the timeline. Simultaneously, all creatures within 500 feet are violently pulled into a pocket dimension outside normal time - the infamous The 13-Hour Trial.

This is a realm of brass and crystal where time is architecture, each "hour" a separate chamber with its own challenge. The trials test combat prowess, wisdom, self-sacrifice, truthfulness, endurance, wit, mercy, justice, and more. Failure at any trial results in temporal imprisonment - the creature is frozen as a statue in the chamber, potentially forever.

Success means escape after completing all thirteen chambers - but upon returning to reality, the party discovers that Imhuran has reformed in his Clockwork Sanctum at full hit points exactly 13 hours after his "defeat."

The only way to permanently destroy Imhuran is to complete all thirteen trials perfectly without a single failure, then venture to the heart of the Clockwork Sanctum to destroy his actual Heart of Time (the divine organ itself). The Heart has AC 30, 800 hit points, immunity to all damage except radiant and force, and regenerates 100 hit points per round unless damaged by a weapon blessed by another Sovereign or a deity-level being. Even then, destroying it requires landing the killing blow within a 6-second window that occurs only once per hour.

---

Lore & Background

Before the Usurpation, Imhuran was a legendary magistrate and philosopher-judge who presided over the most difficult criminal cases of his era. His court was renowned for its fairness, and his judgments were considered the gold standard of justice. But as the years passed, Imhuran grew increasingly frustrated with the fundamental limitations of mortal justice.

He watched guilty men escape punishment because they died of old age before their crimes were discovered. He saw innocents suffer for decades before evidence of their innocence finally surfaced - redemption coming too late to matter. He witnessed how the flow of time made perfect justice impossible: some criminals lived long, comfortable lives while their victims died young and miserable.

The universe, he realized, was fundamentally unjust specifically because time was uncontrolled and random. True justice, perfect justice, would require mastery over time itself - the ability to ensure that every single crime received its proper punishment at precisely the right moment, that every innocent received vindication exactly when they needed it, and that every debt was paid in full according to cosmic ledger.

When whispers of a conspiracy to murder the divine Maker began circulating in secret circles, Imhuran joined immediately. To him, this was the ultimate act of justice: Aedwynn the Maker had created a flawed universe filled with suffering, inequity, and temporal chaos. The god must answer for these crimes.

During the ritual murder, Imhuran approached the dying Maker with a paradox-blade he had forged from frozen seconds and impossible moments. He carved out the still-beating Heart of Time with surgical precision, and the moment he claimed the divine organ for himself, his mortal heart stopped forever. In its place, an eternal clockwork mechanism began to tick - a brass heart that beats only once every several minutes, each beat echoing across the timestream. His body transformed into a fusion of flesh and machinery, temporal gears visible beneath translucent skin.

Now Imhuran sits on his throne in the Clockwork Sanctum, a realm where time itself is visible architecture. His Ledger grows longer each day, containing the name and destined death-moment of every mortal being past, present, and future. He believes himself to be the last bulwark against temporal anarchy, the only force preventing reality from collapsing into chaos.

His ultimate goal is to achieve what he calls "The Final Hour" - to freeze all of time into a single, perfect, static moment where nothing can ever go wrong because nothing can ever happen again. A universe of eternal justice where every being exists in their perfect moment forever, crystallized like insects in amber, safe from change, suffering, and chaos.

---

Combat Tactics

Imhuran fights with the calm confidence of someone who has already seen how the battle will end. Through his Temporal Omniscience, he perceives the next 12 seconds with perfect clarity, meaning he knows exactly what his enemies will attempt before they do it.

He begins every encounter by immediately using Time Stop to assess the battlefield, reposition himself optimally, and potentially land several attacks before anyone else can even react. Against spellcasters, Imhuran's strategy is surgical and merciless. He identifies them through their prepared components and gestures (which he sees 12 seconds in advance), then uses Stolen Seconds or Judgment of Hours legendary actions to prevent them from ever getting a turn.

Against martial combatants, he maintains distance using his 120-foot fly speed and 300-foot teleportation, pelting them with Temporal Gavel strikes from range while using Clockwork Barrage to control areas and Temporal Cascade to lock them in repetitive loops.

Imhuran never wastes a single action - his precognition means every attack, every spell, every movement is perfectly optimized for maximum tactical advantage. When enemies prove more resilient than expected, Imhuran escalates strategically. He brands priority targets to track them across the battlefield. He summons Preservation Custodian Regulators to occupy melee combatants. If pushed below half hit points, he uses The Final Hour legendary actions frequently to deal massive unavoidable damage.

Most dangerously, if the battle turns decidedly against him, Imhuran uses Rewind to reset the encounter to a point before things went wrong - and fights the same battle again with perfect knowledge of every tactic his enemies will use. Facing Imhuran twice is exponentially more difficult than facing him once, because the second time he knows every trick you'll try.

His only true tactical weakness is genuine randomness - he cannot predict Wild Magic surges, perfectly random dice rolls, or actions determined by coin flips. Unpredictable chaos is the only thing that can surprise him.

---

Weaknesses & Vulnerabilities

Bug
Exploitable Weaknesses
Despite his godlike mastery of time, Imhuran is not invincible:

1. Philosophical vulnerability - His rigid devotion to perfect justice makes him philosophically vulnerable. He can be engaged in debate by characters who can prove (through Zone of Truth or similar magic) that their cause is just. He respects cosmic law and genuine righteousness, and will honor agreements made in good faith.

2. Limited precognition - His Temporal Omniscience only extends 12 seconds into the future. Truly random events - Wild Magic surges, random encounter tables, coin flips - can surprise him because they have no deterministic outcome to foresee.

3. Temporal paradoxes - If multiple contradictory timelines attempt to exist simultaneously (through multiple Wish spells creating mutually exclusive realities, or through time-travel creating grandfather paradoxes), Imhuran must spend actions resolving the paradox instead of attacking.

4. Temporal Stasis - He cannot perceive or affect creatures who exist in Temporal Stasis or similar effects that completely remove them from the timestream. If a creature is frozen outside of time entirely, Imhuran's abilities cannot reach them.

5. The Heart of Time - If enemies can damage his Heart directly, Imhuran loses access to all his legendary actions until he returns to repair it (which takes 1d4 rounds of concentration). Destroying the Heart entirely kills him permanently.

6. Ethical paradoxes - Imhuran has never understood that some moral questions have no "correct" answer. Genuinely unsolvable moral dilemmas frustrate and distress him deeply. A clever party might trap him in philosophical debate about scenarios where all outcomes are equally just and unjust.

---

Connection to the Party

Warning
Fursen and Kael's Temporal Anomalies
The party has not yet directly encountered Imhuran, but Fursen bears the scars of his imprisonment in the Clockwork Sanctum. The leonin escaped from Hour XI of the The 13-Hour Trial, creating a crack in causality that may have larger consequences.
> Additionally, Kael's unique existence as an ontological error - a being who died in the Trial but continues to exist - represents a direct challenge to Imhuran's ordered view of reality.

---

Related

  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • The Clockwork Sanctum
  • The 13-Hour Trial
  • Fursen
  • Kael
  • Aedwynn the Maker
