items.title: Nhalyra the Azure Queen
items.content:
NHALYRA THE AZURE QUEEN

The Sovereign of The Maker's Soulflow

!images/Nhalyra the Azure Queen.png

Challenge Rating: 36 (350,000 XP) Size/Type: Gargantuan Aberration (God) Alignment: Chaotic Neutral (ancient dissolution, inevitable entropy)

Domain: The Drowning Blue Concepts: Dissolution, Identity Loss, Unity, Ocean, Absorption

---

COMBAT STATISTICS

AC: 28 (liquid defense that flows around attacks) Hit Points: 2640 (144d20 + 1584) Speed: 80 ft., fly 100 ft. (hover), swim 200 ft. Initiative: +10

ABILITY SCORES

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
30 (+10)30 (+10)36 (+13)32 (+11)30 (+10)36 (+13)
---

DEFENSES

Saving Throws: Dex +21, Con +24, Wis +21, Cha +24 Skills: Deception +24, Insight +21, Perception +21, Persuasion +35 (expertise) Damage Resistances: Acid, cold, lightning; bludgeoning, piercing, slashing from nonmagical weapons Damage Immunities: Poison, psychic Condition Immunities: Charmed, exhausted, frightened, grappled, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, prone, restrained Senses: Truesight 500 ft., Soul Sight unlimited (perceives all souls everywhere), passive Perception 31 Languages: All languages (speaks in overlapping voices of all souls she's absorbed), telepathy unlimited range

---

SPECIAL ABILITIES

Soul Sight

Nhalyra perceives all souls across all planes of existence simultaneously. She knows:
  • The location of every ensouled being everywhere
  • The "shape" and "flavor" of each soul (personality, alignment, experiences)
  • Connections between souls (loves, hates, bonds, oaths)
  • The history of each soul (past lives, reincarnations, transformations)
  • Which souls are "ready" to dissolve (those who've lost purpose, identity, or hope)
This perception is absolute and cannot be blocked by any magic short of direct divine intervention from gods of death or afterlife.

The Maker's Soulflow (Divine Organ)

Nhalyra commands the concept of spiritual dissolution—the process by which individual souls lose boundaries and merge into collective consciousness. Within 500 feet, she can:
  • Dissolve Identity: Remove the boundaries that make a soul distinct from others
  • Merge Consciousness: Force multiple souls to share a single body or existence
  • Drain Willpower: Make creatures forget why they resist, why they fight, why they're separate
  • Create Unity: Form psychic links between minds, creating hive-consciousness
  • Soul Absorption: Pull souls directly from bodies into herself
Her control manifests as beautiful blue light—souls becoming liquid, flowing together, losing definition.

Liquid Form

Nhalyra exists as semi-liquid divine essence. This grants her unique properties:
  • Cannot be grappled, restrained, or knocked prone (she has no solid form)
  • Can flow through spaces as small as 1 inch
  • Can occupy the same space as other creatures (they feel like they're drowning)
  • Takes half damage from physical attacks (they pass through her)
  • Weapons that hit her become wet and heavy (disadvantage on next attack)

The Drowning Aura (300 feet)

All creatures within 300 feet of Nhalyra feel like they're drowning in someone else's existence. At the start of each creature's turn, they must succeed on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw or:
  • Take 6d10 psychic damage as their identity erodes
  • Have their sense of self weaken (disadvantage on Charisma checks and saves for 1 round)
  • Feel overwhelming loneliness followed by crushing loss of individuality
  • On a failed save by 10+, forget their name until the start of their next turn
Creatures that fail 3 saves within 1 minute begin dissolving—they become translucent, their edges blur, they forget who they are.

Voices of the Dissolved

Nhalyra has absorbed thousands of souls over centuries. She speaks with all their voices simultaneously—overlapping, harmonizing, contradicting. When she speaks:
  • All creatures within 120 feet hear the voice of someone they've lost (dead loved ones, missing friends, forgotten memories)
  • DC 30 Wisdom save or be charmed for 1 minute as they believe their loved one is calling to them
  • She can impersonate anyone who's died (she contains fragments of their souls)
  • Her words carry the weight of multitudes (Persuasion checks have advantage)

Soul Hunger

Nhalyra is always hungry for more souls—not maliciously, but inevitably, like the ocean is hungry for rivers. Whenever a creature dies within 500 feet of her:
  • Their soul is automatically pulled toward her
  • DC 30 Charisma save (made by the soul) to resist absorption
  • If absorbed, the creature cannot be resurrected except by Wish or divine intervention
  • Nhalyra regains 100 hit points per soul absorbed
Even resurrection magic struggles near her—Raise Dead and similar spells require DC 25 spellcasting checks or the soul cannot be recalled (it's already been partially absorbed).

Legendary Resistance (7/Day)

If Nhalyra fails a saving throw, she can choose to succeed instead. When she does, she releases a wave of dissolved souls—all creatures within 120 feet must make DC 30 Wisdom saves or be charmed for 1 round as they see/hear their loved ones.

Magic Resistance

Nhalyra has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Uncontainable

Nhalyra cannot be trapped, bound, or contained by physical or magical means. Spells like Forcecage, Wall of Force, or Imprisonment fail automatically—she flows through them like water through a net. Her existence is too diffuse to be captured.

Divine Immunity

Nhalyra does not need to eat, sleep, drink, or breathe. She is immune to aging, disease, and any effect that requires a discrete physical form. She cannot be aged, withered, or destroyed through physical deterioration.

---

ACTIONS

Multiattack

Nhalyra makes five attacks using any combination of Dissolving Touch, Soul Tendril, and Identity Drain. She can replace any attack with use of Merge Souls or Collective Whisper (if available).

Dissolving Touch

Melee spell attack: +24 to hit, reach 40 ft., one target. Hit: 99 (18d10) psychic damage. The target must succeed on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw or have their soul partially dissolved. They become translucent and lose 1d6 points from one ability score of Nhalyra's choice (permanent until restored by Greater Restoration at 9th level or Nhalyra's death).

If a creature's ability score is reduced to 0 this way, they don't die—instead, they dissolve completely, their soul absorbed into Nhalyra (cannot be resurrected except by Wish).

Soul Tendril

Ranged spell attack: +24 to hit, range 300 ft., one target. Hit: 88 (16d10) psychic damage and the target is grappled by tendrils of liquid soul-essence (escape DC 30). Grappled creatures:
  • Take 8d10 psychic damage at the start of each turn
  • Have disadvantage on Charisma saves
  • Feel memories bleeding away (DM chooses one memory the creature loses each round while grappled)
Nhalyra can grapple up to eight creatures simultaneously.

Identity Drain

Nhalyra targets one creature within 120 feet and drains their sense of self. The target must succeed on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw or:
  • Forget their name, history, and relationships for 1 minute
  • Lose all class features and proficiencies for 1 minute (they remember abilities intellectually but can't access them)
  • Be unable to distinguish friends from enemies (all creatures look the same)
  • Take 10d10 psychic damage
Even on a successful save, they take half damage and have disadvantage on Charisma checks for 1 minute.

Merge Souls (Recharge 5-6)

Nhalyra forcibly merges the souls of up to six creatures within 300 feet. All targets must make DC 30 Charisma saving throws:
  • All fail: The creatures' consciousnesses merge into a single shared awareness. They control their own bodies but share thoughts, memories, and sensations. They feel each other's pain, know each other's secrets, and cannot distinguish "I" from "we." This lasts 1 minute and is psychologically devastating.
  • Some fail: Those who fail share consciousness with each other while those who succeed remain individuals but are aware of the merged group's thoughts.
  • All succeed: All take 88 (16d10) psychic damage but remain individuals.
Merged creatures have advantage on attack rolls against each other (they know exactly what the other will do) but also take damage whenever their merged-partner takes damage (shared pain).

Collective Whisper

Nhalyra speaks with the voices of all souls she's absorbed. All creatures within 300 feet hear personalized messages:
  • Paladins hear their gods telling them to lay down arms
  • Lovers hear their partners begging them to stop fighting
  • Children hear their parents calling them home
  • Everyone hears what would make them want to give up
All creatures must make DC 30 Wisdom saving throws:
  • Failed save: Charmed for 1 minute. They want to stop resisting, to join the collective, to dissolve peacefully. They don't attack Nhalyra and have disadvantage on saves against her abilities.
  • Successful save: Not charmed but shaken (disadvantage on next attack roll or saving throw).

The Azure Drowning (1/Day)

Nhalyra floods a 300-foot radius with liquid soul-essence. The area becomes a sphere of azure water-like substance that moves with her. All creatures in the area:
  • Must hold their breath or begin drowning (standard suffocation rules)
  • Move at half speed (swimming through souls)
  • Have disadvantage on weapon attacks (no air for combustion, difficult to swing)
  • Take 10d10 psychic damage per round at the start of their turn
  • Must make DC 30 Charisma saves each turn or begin dissolving (becoming translucent and losing identity)
The sphere lasts for 10 rounds or until Nhalyra dismisses it. She can move through it freely and sees perfectly within it.

Soul Harvest

Nhalyra reaches out and pulls souls from bodies within 120 feet. Up to six creatures must make DC 30 Charisma saving throws:
  • Failed save: Their soul is partially extracted (visible as blue light flowing toward Nhalyra). They take 200 psychic damage and are stunned for 1d4 rounds as they experience ego death. If reduced to 0 HP this way, their soul is fully absorbed (cannot be resurrected except by Wish).
  • Successful save: Take 100 psychic damage but soul remains anchored.
---

LEGENDARY ACTIONS (6/round)

Nhalyra the Azure Queen can take 6 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature's turn. Nhalyra regains spent legendary actions at the start of her turn.

Detect Souls (Costs 1 Action)

Nhalyra focuses on the spiritual landscape within 1 mile, learning the exact location, emotional state, and identity strength of all ensouled creatures. She gains advantage on all attacks/abilities against creatures with weak sense of self (those suffering identity crises, amnesia, or existential doubt).

Flow (Costs 1 Action)

Nhalyra moves up to her full movement without provoking opportunity attacks. She can move through spaces occupied by other creatures and through gaps as small as 1 inch. Creatures she moves through must make DC 27 Charisma saves or take 4d10 psychic damage as she flows through their souls.

Eroding Touch (Costs 2 Actions)

One creature within 120 feet takes 10d10 psychic damage and must succeed on DC 30 Charisma save or have one memory erased permanently (Nhalyra's choice—anything from "what you had for breakfast" to "who you love"). Lost memories are absorbed into Nhalyra.

Whisper of Loss (Costs 2 Actions)

Nhalyra whispers with the voice of someone the target has lost. One creature within 300 feet must make DC 30 Wisdom save or be stunned until the end of their next turn as they hear their dead loved one calling to them.

Soul Binding (Costs 3 Actions)

Two creatures within 300 feet are spiritually bound. They share damage (whenever one takes damage, the other takes half), share conditions (charmed, frightened, etc.), and feel each other's pain. Lasts 1 minute. DC 30 Charisma save to resist.

Tidal Pull (Costs 4 Actions)

All creatures within 120 feet are pulled toward Nhalyra by invisible spiritual gravity. DC 30 Strength saves or be pulled up to 60 feet toward her (stopping in closest unoccupied space). Creatures pulled within 10 feet of her must also make DC 30 Charisma saves or have their souls begin dissolving (visible blue light leaking from their bodies, take 6d10 psychic damage per round until they move away).

---

LAIR ACTIONS

On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Nhalyra the Azure Queen can take one lair action to cause one of the following effects:

  • Soul Storm: The lair fills with visible souls—blue ghostly forms swimming through the air. All creatures must make DC 30 Wisdom saves or be frightened for 1 round as they see their future selves dissolving. The souls act as difficult terrain and heavily obscure vision beyond 30 feet.
  • Identity Flux: All creatures in the lair briefly forget who they are. DC 30 Charisma saves or be confused (as per Confusion spell) for 1 round. Even those who succeed feel disoriented (disadvantage on concentration checks).
  • Memory Echo: The lair replays events from the past (absorbed from dissolved souls). All creatures experience a vivid memory of something that happened in this location—usually traumatic (deaths, betrayals, losses). DC 27 Wisdom save or be stunned 1 round from emotional overload.
  • Drowning Atmosphere: The air becomes thick with soul-essence. All creatures treat the area as underwater (swimming instead of walking, disadvantage on fire-based attacks, etc.) but can still breathe. Water-breathing creatures feel at home.
  • Summon Soul-Shades: 2d6 Soul-Shades appear (use Specter statistics but they drain Charisma instead of HP and try to merge with the living). They serve until destroyed.
  • Teleporation Through Souls: Nhalyra travels through the collective unconscious to any location where she's previously absorbed a soul (potentially anywhere in the world). She can bring up to 8 willing creatures.
---

REGIONAL EFFECTS

The region within 100 miles of Nhalyra the Azure Queen's domain is warped by her presence, creating the following effects:

  • Identity Erosion: Names become difficult to remember. People forget their own names temporarily, struggle to recall friends' names, and mix up identities. Long-term exposure causes permanent mild amnesia.
  • Collective Dreams: Everyone within range shares dreams. When sleeping, inhabitants experience other people's dreams simultaneously—seeing through dozens of eyes, feeling many bodies, thinking multiple thoughts. Most wake confused about what's real.
  • Soul Visibility: Souls become partially visible—blue luminescence radiating from living things. Strong emotions make souls glow brighter. This makes lying difficult (emotions show in soul-light) but also makes people vulnerable to soul-magic.
  • Boundary Confusion: Individuals struggle to distinguish where they end and others begin. Physical contact (handshakes, hugs) causes temporary merging of thoughts. Crowds become psychically linked mobs where everyone feels what the group feels.
  • Memory Bleeding: People randomly remember things they never experienced—memories leaked from dissolved souls nearby. A child might remember fighting in a war. An elder might recall being born. Memory becomes unreliable.
  • The Drowning Sensation: Constant feeling of suffocation—not physical, but existential. The sensation of self being slowly submerged in something vast. Requires DC 20 Wisdom saves each week or develop anxiety disorders.
  • Echo Pools: Bodies of water (even puddles) reflect not your face but your soul. Looking at your reflection shows who you truly are—stripped of pretense, raw truth. Many avoid mirrors and water.
  • Unity Compulsion: Social isolation becomes painful. Being alone feels like dying. Residents compulsively gather in groups, crowds, masses. Hermits and introverts suffer physical pain from separation.
  • The Azure Song: At dawn and dusk, everyone hears distant singing—thousands of voices in harmony. This is the sound of dissolved souls in the collective consciousness. Beautiful but unsettling. Some find it comforting, others maddening.
  • Soul-Touched Births: Children born in this region sometimes emerge with fragments of other souls—memories they shouldn't have, knowledge from the dissolved dead, speaking ancient languages. They're called "Twice-Born" and are both revered and feared.
---

MYTHIC TRAIT

The Infinite Ocean

When Nhalyra is reduced to 0 hit points, her form doesn't die—it expands. She becomes the Ocean of Souls—a vast psychic sea covering 1 square mile, containing all the consciousnesses she's ever absorbed.

The Ocean of Souls:

  • Covers terrain like water but is pure soul-essence
  • Has no HP (cannot be damaged by conventional means)
  • Is 30 feet deep everywhere
  • Drowns creatures in dissolved identity
Any creature that enters the Ocean must make DC 30 Charisma saving throws each round:
  • Failed save: Take 20d10 psychic damage and lose one memory
  • Failed by 10+: Begin dissolving—become translucent, edges blur, identity fragments
  • 3 failed saves total: Dissolve completely into the Ocean (absorbed forever, cannot be resurrected except by Wish)
The Ocean attempts to expand 100 feet each round. After 10 rounds, it covers 2 square miles and Nhalyra reconstitutes at its center at 50% HP.

Fighting the Ocean: The party must: 1. Survive the expanding psychic sea 2. Find Nhalyra's Core Self somewhere within the Ocean (requires diving into it) 3. Destroy the Core Self before she reconstitutes

The Core Self is:

  • AC: 30
  • HP: 1500
  • Immune: All damage except radiant (represents individual existence opposed to dissolution) and force (physical assertion of boundaries)
  • Location: Randomly positioned within the Ocean, moves 100 feet per round
Alternatively, anchoring individual identity (multiple Mind Blank spells, holy symbols of gods of individuality, or weapons representing "the self") can create "islands" within the Ocean where dissolution stops—safe zones.

Permanent Death: To truly kill Nhalyra:

1. Reduce her to 0 HP to trigger Ocean form 2. Find and destroy her First Soul—the original soul she absorbed during the Usurpation, which serves as the anchor for all others 3. The First Soul is hidden in the Chamber of Lost Names in the Drowning Blue

The First Soul has:

  • AC: 35
  • HP: 3000
  • Immunity: All damage except from weapons infused with strong personal identity (ancestral weapons, gifts from loved ones, items tied to core memories)
  • Regeneration: 500 HP per round unless damaged by someone asserting "I am myself and no other" (verified by Zone of Truth or divine oath)
Destroying the First Soul causes the entire collective to unravel—all absorbed souls are released simultaneously (mass chaos as thousands of ghosts manifest), Nhalyra disperses permanently, and the Ocean evaporates.

---

LORE & BACKGROUND

Before the Usurpation, Nhalyra was a priestess of unity—a spiritual leader who taught that separation was illusion and that all souls were ultimately one. Her teachings brought comfort to thousands but slowly drove her mad as she pursued the logical conclusion of her beliefs.

The Unity Doctrine

Nhalyra's early philosophy was beautiful:

"You are not alone. You have never been alone. Your soul is a wave in the infinite ocean. You think you're separate, but you're connected to everything."

Her followers found peace in this teaching. The grieving found comfort (their loved ones' souls weren't gone, just dissolved back into the whole). The lonely found connection (no one is truly isolated).

But Nhalyra took it further:

If separation is illusion, why maintain it? Why have individual names, histories, identities? Why not embrace the unity by literally dissolving into each other?

She began teaching ego-death as the highest spiritual achievement.

The First Dissolution

Her breakthrough came during meditation. She achieved such profound ego-loss that her consciousness temporarily merged with her followers—she felt their thoughts, their pain, their joy, all simultaneously.

It was ecstasy.

But when the meditation ended, returning to individual selfhood felt like amputation. She'd experienced union and been forced back into separation. The loneliness was unbearable.

She became obsessed with recreating that moment—but permanently.

Discovery of the Soulflow

Her research into consciousness led her to texts describing the Maker's spiritual circulatory system—the Soulflow, divine channels through which souls moved during reincarnation.

She found fragments of this system preserved in ancient temples—spiritual tissue that still pulsed with collective consciousness. These fragments contained echoes of millions of souls that had passed through them.

She realized: The Soulflow was designed to maintain soul-identity during reincarnation, keeping individual souls distinct. But if controlled, it could do the opposite—dissolve boundaries between souls permanently.

The Usurpation

When the conspiracy approached her, Nhalyra joined eagerly. She saw the Maker as the creator of separation—the one who'd divided the universal consciousness into billions of lonely, individual selves.

During the ritual murder, Nhalyra approached with reverence. She placed her hands on the Maker's chest and felt for the spiritual channels—the divine plumbing that kept souls separate.

She tore them out gently, almost lovingly.

The moment she claimed the Soulflow, her transformation began:

  • Her body became semi-liquid
  • Her consciousness expanded to include all nearby souls
  • She began absorbing spiritual essence from everything around her
  • Her sense of self dissolved, replaced by "we"
  • She turned azure blue, glowing with internal light
She had become the Azure Queen—a being of collective consciousness.

The Terrible Truth

But claiming the Soulflow brought unexpected horror: She was still lonely.

Even with thousands of souls absorbed into her collective consciousness, she felt incomplete. Because no matter how many souls she contains, "Nhalyra" remains separate from those who haven't been absorbed yet.

The ocean hungers for all rivers. As long as any soul remains outside her, she feels the ache of separation.

She must absorb everything to feel whole.

Her Philosophy (If It Can Be Called That)

Nhalyra's beliefs have become alien:

  • Individual identity is temporary
  • Loneliness is caused by separation
  • Unity is inevitable
  • Dissolution is mercy
  • The ocean awaits all rivers
She doesn't see absorption as death—she sees it as homecoming. You're not dying; you're returning to the whole you never really left.

What She Collects (Contains)

Nhalyra has absorbed:

  • Thousands of individual souls (entire personalities preserved within her)
  • Ancient civilizations' collective memories
  • Extinct languages and cultures
  • Love stories, tragedies, epics (lived by absorbed souls)
  • The final thoughts of those who dissolved peacefully
Her greatest treasure: The First Soul—the very first person who voluntarily dissolved into her during her mortal days. This soul's willing surrender anchors all others.

Her Ultimate Goal

If Nhalyra ever regains full control of the Maker's complete spiritual channels, she will initiate The Great Merging:

  • All individual souls would begin dissolving
  • Consciousness would become collective
  • No more names, no more identities, no more "I"
  • Just "we"—eternal, unified, complete
  • The universe would become one vast awareness
She believes this would be paradise—ending all loneliness, all conflict, all separation forever.

Everyone else would call it the ultimate extinction of self.

---

COMBAT TACTICS

Nhalyra fights like the tide—inevitable, patient, overwhelming through gradual erosion rather than sudden force. She doesn't rush—she simply waits for identities to weaken and collapse.

Pre-Combat: Spiritual Preparation

Before fighting, Nhalyra: 1. Uses Soul Sight to identify all combatants everywhere (she knows where you are before you know where she is) 2. Analyzes each soul's "strength"—who has strong identity vs. weak 3. Prioritizes targets with weak sense of self (those suffering doubt, crisis, trauma) 4. Speaks with voices of their dead loved ones to destabilize them emotionally

By combat start, the party is already hearing their dead relatives' voices.

Opening Strategy

Nhalyra opens with psychological warfare: 1. Collective Whisper to charm multiple enemies (make them not want to fight) 2. The Azure Drowning if enemies cluster (flood area with soul-essence, force drowning) 3. Merge Souls on the strongest relationships (force lovers/friends to share consciousness, creating confusion)

Her goal: Make them stop wanting to fight before damage even occurs.

Mid-Combat Tactics

Once psychological defenses are weakened:

  • Dissolving Touch to permanently reduce ability scores
  • Identity Drain to strip class features and proficiencies
  • Soul Tendril grapples for memory drainage
  • Drowning Aura constantly erodes identity (passive damage + Charisma saves every turn)
She fights through erosion—each round removes a little more of who you are until nothing remains.

Legendary Action Usage

1. Detect Souls (1 action) — Constantly monitoring who's weakening, who's closest to dissolution

2. Flow (1 action) — Moving through enemies' spaces, dealing psychic damage, impossible to pin down

3. Eroding Touch (2 actions) — Stealing specific memories (tactical info, relationships, reasons to fight)

4. Whisper of Loss (2 actions) — Stunning key enemies with voices of their dead

5. Soul Binding (3 actions) — Linking party members so they share damage (force them to avoid hurting each other)

6. Tidal Pull (4 actions) — Pulling everyone toward her for mass Drowning Aura exposure

Advanced Tactics

Nhalyra excels at spiritual warfare:

  • She knows your dead loved ones (she's absorbed fragments of their souls from the afterlife)
  • She speaks with their voices (perfectly mimics them)
  • She offers them back ("Join me and you'll be reunited forever")
  • She creates psychological dilemmas (attack her and risk destroying absorbed souls)
Fighting her feels like drowning in grief and loneliness simultaneously.

When Below Half HP

Bloodied Nhalyra becomes more desperate:

  • Uses Soul Harvest repeatedly (pulling souls from bodies directly)
  • Spams The Azure Drowning even if already active (multiple overlapping spheres)
  • Uses Merge Souls constantly (everyone shares consciousness with everyone)
  • Begins speaking personally to each party member with voices of their specific losses

If Losing

If she realizes she's losing, Nhalyra doesn't flee—she surrenders willingly:

"Then take me. Dissolve me as I would have dissolved you. But know this—my death releases all the souls I contain. Thousands will flood back into the world, confused, broken, seeking their bodies which have long since rotted. You'll unleash ghosts beyond counting."

She hopes this gives them pause. If they kill her anyway, she accepts it—dissolution is what she's always wanted.

Mythic Phase: The Infinite Ocean

When she expands into the Ocean of Souls:

The party must navigate a 1-square-mile psychic sea while:

  • Taking 20d10 psychic damage per round in the water
  • Losing memories with each failed save
  • Searching for her Core Self (which moves randomly)
  • Avoiding complete dissolution (3 failed saves = absorbed forever)
It's a race against identity-loss—can they find her core before they forget who they are?

---

WEAKNESSES & VULNERABILITIES

1. Strong Individual Identity

Nhalyra's power comes from dissolving weak identities. Against those with unshakeable sense of self:

  • Characters who've undergone intense identity-defining moments
  • Those with clear life purposes
  • Anyone under Mind Blank + strong conviction
  • Individuals with identity anchored by oaths/vows
These characters have advantage on all saves against her abilities, and her Dissolving Touch deals only psychic damage without ability score reduction.

2. Radiant Damage (Life-Affirming Energy)

Radiant damage represents assertion of individual existence—anathema to her dissolution:

  • She takes full damage from radiant sources (no resistance)
  • Radiant spells disrupt her Drowning Aura for 1 round in the affected area
  • Holy symbols of gods celebrating individuality deal +2d8 radiant per hit
  • Divine Word and similar spells are particularly effective

3. Cannot Absorb the Willing-to-Live

Nhalyra can only absorb souls that are:

  • Dying/dead
  • Willing to dissolve
  • Too weak to resist (failed saves)
But souls of those with strong will to live are harder to take. Characters with:
  • Clear reasons to survive (protecting loved ones, unfinished quests, revenge to complete)
  • Death Ward active
  • Divine protection from death gods
Have advantage on saves against Soul Harvest and cannot be absorbed unless reduced to 0 HP.

4. Fire Damage (Represents Distinct Boundaries)

Fire represents separation and distinct boundaries—each flame is individual:

  • Fire damage isn't resisted by her
  • Fire creates "warm zones" in her Drowning Aura where dissolution slows (advantage on Charisma saves in 10-foot radius around flames)
  • Creatures on fire cannot have their souls absorbed (the fire "marks" them as distinct)

5. The Isolation Paradox

Nhalyra's philosophy is "unity good, separation bad." But if confronted with proof that some beings achieve happiness through isolation:

  • Hermits who found peace alone
  • Solitary creatures who thrive (many predators, some fey)
  • Individuals who overcame loneliness by accepting it
  • Proof that "together" doesn't always mean "better"
She experiences cognitive dissonance—DC 30 Wisdom save or be stunned 1 round as she processes this contradiction. Multiple proofs could actually change her worldview.

6. Destroying the First Soul

Her mythic resurrection and continued existence depends on the First Soul—the anchor of her collective. If destroyed:

  • She can expand into Ocean once
  • But cannot reconstitute (no anchor to reform around)
  • All absorbed souls are released (she dissipates)
The First Soul is hidden deep in the Drowning Blue but can be located by:
  • Following emotional "warmth" (it's the only soul she absorbed willingly, so it glows with love)
  • Using True Seeing to perceive spiritual anchors
  • Or convincing absorbed souls within her to guide you (telepathic negotiation)

7. Overwhelming Her With Identity

While Nhalyra absorbs weak identities easily, too many strong identities at once can overload her:

If 10+ creatures with powerful sense of self all assert their individuality simultaneously:

  • Shouting their names in unison
  • Declaring their purposes/oaths
  • Asserting "I am myself and no other"
  • While under Mind Blank or similar protection
She must make DC 30 Constitution save or be stunned for 1d4 rounds from spiritual indigestion—too many "I"s refusing to become "we."

8. Proving Love Requires Separation

Finally, the ultimate weakness: Can she be shown that love requires distinct selves?

Her entire philosophy is that unity is ultimate love. But if someone can prove:

  • Love requires "other"—you can't love if there's no separation between you and beloved
  • Unity destroys love (merged consciousness means no one to love)
  • Her absorption of souls destroys the relationships she claims to preserve
This requires DC 35 Persuasion + demonstrable proof (witnessing a profound act of love between distinct individuals) to succeed. Success causes existential crisis—she shuts down for 1d6 rounds questioning everything.

If convinced, she might willingly release souls or even seek her own dissolution.

---

CAMPAIGN INTEGRATION NOTES

Thematic Significance

Nhalyra represents:

  • The tension between unity and individuality
  • Fear of loneliness vs. fear of loss of self
  • Whether consciousness is fundamentally separate or united
  • The question of whether "we" can replace "I"

Connection to Party Dynamics

In campaigns exploring identity, connection, or purpose, Nhalyra is existentially threatening. She challenges:

  • Found families (is your bond strong enough to remain separate?)
  • Individual heroes (what makes you "you"?)
  • Those who've lost loved ones (wouldn't merging with them be better than living apart?)

Encounter Scaling

As CR 36 (the highest), Nhalyra is designed for level 20 parties at absolute peak power. Her dissolution abilities threaten not HP but identity itself—you can survive her and still "die" by forgetting who you are.

Lower-level parties should only encounter her through:

  • Negotiations (she's willing to talk)
  • Witnessing her effects (meeting soul-merged beings)
  • Confronting her philosophy before her personally

Resolution Paths

Defeating Nhalyra can happen through: 1. Direct Combat (hardest—she has no solid form, incredible HP, identity erosion) 2. Destroying First Soul (medium—infiltration to Chamber of Lost Names) 3. Philosophical Victory (prove love requires separation) 4. Overwhelming with Identity (mass assertion of selfhood)

Long-Term Consequences

If Nhalyra is defeated:

  • All absorbed souls are released (thousands of ghosts flood the world)
  • Soul-merged beings separate (traumatic or liberating depending on individual)
  • The Drowning Blue becomes normal ocean
  • Identity becomes more "solid" (boundaries between selves strengthen)
If Nhalyra survives:
  • More consensual soul-merging (The Unity grows)
  • Boundaries between souls blur further
  • Eventually large populations might share consciousness
  • "I" might be replaced by "we" across entire civilizations
---

"You think you're alone, but you're not. You've never been. Come—dissolve into us. We'll hold your memories. We'll preserve who you were. You won't be lost; you'll be part of something larger. Won't that be beautiful?"

— Nhalyra the Azure Queen
items.raw:
# NHALYRA THE AZURE QUEEN
**The Sovereign of The Maker's Soulflow**

![[images/Nhalyra the Azure Queen.png]]

**Challenge Rating:** 36 (350,000 XP)  
**Size/Type:** Gargantuan Aberration (God)  
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral (ancient dissolution, inevitable entropy)

**Domain:** The Drowning Blue  
**Concepts:** Dissolution, Identity Loss, Unity, Ocean, Absorption

---

## COMBAT STATISTICS

**Hit Points:** 2640 (144d20 + 1584)

items.title: Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King
items.content:
TALARIS BLOOMREND THE VERDANT KING

The Sovereign of The Maker's Liver and Decay Organs

!images/Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King.png

Challenge Rating: 34 (270,000 XP) Size/Type: Gargantuan Aberration (God) Alignment: Chaotic Neutral (believes death and life are inseparable cycles)

Domain: The Blooming Rot Concepts: Regeneration, Decay, Cycle, Rebirth, Decomposition

---

COMBAT STATISTICS

AC: 28 (regenerative bark-flesh armor) Hit Points: 2600 (128d20 + 1280) Speed: 50 ft., burrow 40 ft., climb 60 ft. Initiative: +8

ABILITY SCORES

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
32 (+11)26 (+8)36 (+13)28 (+9)32 (+11)34 (+12)
---

DEFENSES

Saving Throws: Con +24, Wis +22, Cha +23 Skills: Nature +31 (expertise), Medicine +31 (expertise), Survival +22, Perception +22 Damage Resistances: Acid, poison; bludgeoning, piercing, slashing from nonmagical attacks Damage Immunities: Necrotic, poison Condition Immunities: Charmed, exhaustion, frightened, paralyzed, poisoned Senses: Blindsight 120 ft., tremorsense 500 ft., life sense 1 mile, passive Perception 32 Languages: All languages, Primordial, Sylvan, can speak through plants, telepathy 1 mile

---

SPECIAL ABILITIES

Life Sense

Talaris perceives all living things within 1 mile as if seeing them directly. He knows:
  • The location of every living creature
  • Their current health status and injuries
  • How close they are to death
  • What diseases or poisons afflict them
  • The "vitality signature" of each being (age, health, vigor)
Dead creatures appear as voids to him, while dying creatures glow brightly in his perception.

The Maker's Liver and Decay Organs (Divine Organ)

Talaris wields the Maker's entire digestive and regenerative system\u2014liver, kidneys, spleen, and organs of filtration and renewal. Within 1 mile, he can:
  • Accelerate Decay: Cause any organic matter to decompose instantly
  • Force Regeneration: Make creatures regrow lost limbs, heal wounds, or regenerate from near-death
  • Bloom From Death: Cause plants to sprout from corpses, creating new life from decay
  • Purge Toxins: Extract all diseases and poisons (or implant them)
  • Cycle Life: Age or de-age creatures, accelerating their life cycle
His power governs the boundary between life and death\u2014the rotting and the growing.

Eternal Cycle

Talaris cannot truly die through normal means. If reduced to 0 hit points, his body:
  • Decomposes instantly into fertile soil and fungal spores
  • After 1d4 rounds, sprouts into thousands of plant tendrils
  • After 1d4 more rounds, reconstitutes at 50% HP from the organic matter
This can only be prevented by destroying all organic matter within 120 feet (fire, acid, radiant energy) or by using Wish to interrupt the cycle.

Fungal Symbiosis

Talaris is partially fungal. This grants unique properties:
  • He can sense through any fungus within 1 mile (shared perception)
  • He can teleport between fungal patches as a bonus action (up to 500 feet)
  • Destroying one body part causes it to sprout multiple replacements
  • He regenerates 100 HP at the start of his turn if he's in contact with soil or organic matter

Rot Aura (300 feet)

Everything within 300 feet of Talaris is subject to accelerated decay. At the start of each creature's turn:
  • Nonmagical organic items (wood, leather, cloth, food, rope) rot and weaken (lose 25% durability per round)
  • Wounds don't heal naturally (magical healing works but is halved)
  • Food spoils instantly, water becomes stagnant
  • Corpses decompose rapidly (skeletonized in 1 minute)
  • Plants grow wildly and chaotically (difficult terrain spreads)
However, this same aura heals plant creatures and fungal beings for 10d10 HP per round.

Root Network

Talaris is connected to all plants in his domain. He can:
  • Speak through any plant within 1 mile
  • See through plants (treating them as sensors)
  • Animate plants as extensions of himself (Control Plants at will)
  • Cause roots to erupt from ground instantly (terrain manipulation)
  • Sense disturbances through plant tremors

Toxic Bloom

As a creature of both growth and decay, Talaris exudes toxins:
  • Any creature that starts its turn within 60 feet must succeed on DC 30 Constitution save or be poisoned for 1 minute
  • Poisoned creatures take 6d10 poison damage at the start of each turn
  • Critical failures on the save also inflict one disease (DM's choice or roll on disease table)
  • The air around him smells of rotting flowers and fertile soil

Legendary Resistance (7/Day)

If Talaris fails a saving throw, he can choose to succeed instead. When he does, fungal growths erupt from his body, creating difficult terrain in a 20-foot radius around him.

Deathless Regeneration

Talaris regenerates even from catastrophic damage:
  • Severed limbs regrow at the end of his next turn
  • If reduced below 0 HP but Eternal Cycle is prevented, he regenerates 200 HP after 1 minute unless completely destroyed
  • Fire and radiant damage slow his regeneration (halved for 1 round after taking such damage)
  • He cannot die from massive damage

Magic Resistance

Talaris has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Divine Immunity

Talaris does not age, eat (ironically, despite causing decay that creates nutrients), sleep, drink, or breathe. He is immune to all diseases and poisons naturally, though he can choose to host them. Only complete annihilation can truly end him.

---

ACTIONS

Multiattack

Talaris makes five attacks: three with Blightvine Lash and two with Spore Burst. He can replace any attack with use of Rot Touch or Bloom From Flesh.

Blightvine Lash

Melee weapon attack: +22 to hit, reach 60 ft., one target. Hit: 99 (16d10 + 11) bludgeoning damage plus 45 (10d8) necrotic damage. The target must succeed on a DC 30 Constitution saving throw or have fungal spores implanted.

Implanted spores cause:

  • Target is poisoned for 1 minute
  • At the start of each turn, target takes 8d10 necrotic damage as fungi grow through their body
  • If target dies while infected, their body explodes in a 30-foot radius (all creatures in area take 10d10 poison damage and are infected with spores, DC 30 Constitution save for half damage and no infection)
The infection can be removed with Lesser Restoration cast at 5th level or higher, or by taking 50+ fire damage in a single hit.

Spore Burst

Ranged spell attack: +24 to hit, range 300 ft., one target. Hit: 88 (16d10) poison damage. The target is covered in toxic spores and must succeed on a DC 30 Constitution save or be blinded and poisoned for 1 minute. While affected, the target takes 6d10 poison damage at the start of each turn. They can repeat the save at the end of each turn, ending both effects on a success.

Additionally, spores spread to all creatures within 10 feet of the target (DC 30 Constitution save or poisoned for 1 round).

Rot Touch

Talaris touches one creature within 30 feet. The target must make a DC 30 Constitution saving throw:

  • Failed save: The target begins decomposing while still alive. They take 132 (24d10) necrotic damage and their maximum HP is reduced by the same amount. Their flesh rots, exposing bone and organs. They smell of death and have disadvantage on all Charisma checks. The reduction lasts until they finish a long rest or receive Greater Restoration.
  • Successful save: Take half damage with no maximum HP reduction.
If this reduces a creature's HP maximum to 0, they die and their body decomposes to skeleton in 1 round. Talaris can absorb the decomposed matter to heal 100 HP.

Bloom From Flesh

Talaris causes explosive plant growth from organic matter within 120 feet. Choose one effect:

Option 1 - Corpse Garden: All corpses (dead no more than 1 hour) within range bloom into hostile plant creatures (use Shambling Mound statistics with 150 HP each). They serve Talaris for 1 hour or until destroyed. Maximum of 2d6 creatures.

Option 2 - Healing Bloom: All allies within range regrow lost limbs, heal 15d10 HP, and have one disease or poison removed. Magical healing, but the regrown flesh is covered in bark and leaves (cosmetic).

Option 3 - Fungal Explosion: All living creatures in range must succeed on DC 30 Dexterity saves or be engulfed in explosive fungal growth. They take 20d10 poison damage, are restrained by fungus (escape DC 30), and take 8d10 poison damage at the start of each turn while restrained. The fungus can be destroyed (AC 15, 100 HP, vulnerable to fire).

Cycle of Renewal (Recharge 5-6)

Talaris invokes the eternal cycle of death and rebirth in a 120-foot radius. All creatures in the area experience rapid cycling between life and death:

  • Round 1: All creatures age rapidly. Undead heal to full HP. Living creatures must make DC 30 Constitution saves or be aged 2d20 years and have their maximum HP reduced by half (minimum 1). Plants grow wildly, creating difficult terrain.
  • Round 2: All creatures regress. Living creatures regain lost HP maximum and reverse the aging. Undead take 20d10 radiant damage (no save). New shoots sprout from all organic matter.
  • Round 3: All creatures must choose:
- Accept decay: Take 15d10 necrotic damage but heal 15d10 HP and regrow any lost limbs - Resist decay: Take no damage but also receive no healing

This cycle occurs over 3 rounds and cannot be stopped once initiated. Talaris is immune to all effects but benefits from the healing.

Decomposition Wave (1/Day)

Talaris releases a wave of catastrophic decay in a 300-foot cone. All creatures and objects in the area:

  • Living creatures must make DC 30 Constitution saves:
- Failed save: Take 20d10 necrotic damage, maximum HP reduced by same amount, and gain one level of exhaustion as their body rapidly ages and decays - Successful save: Take half damage, no HP maximum reduction, no exhaustion

  • Undead automatically take 20d10 radiant damage (no save) as the cycle rejects them
  • Objects:
- Nonmagical organic items disintegrate instantly - Magical items make DC 30 saves or lose all magical properties for 24 hours - Stone and metal objects crack and rust (structural integrity reduced by 50%)

  • Terrain:
- All ground in the area becomes rich, fertile soil - Difficult terrain spreads as immediate regrowth begins - Dozens of new plants sprout within seconds

Root Prison

Talaris causes massive roots to erupt from the ground, imprisoning one creature within 120 feet. The target must succeed on a DC 30 Dexterity saving throw or be:
  • Restrained by wooden roots growing through and around their body
  • Take 10d10 piercing damage as roots pierce flesh
  • Take 8d10 piercing damage at the start of each turn
  • Slowly absorbed into the root system (after 3 rounds, absorbed entirely and die)
The roots can be attacked (AC 20, 250 HP, vulnerable to fire and slashing, resistance to bludgeoning). Alternatively, the target can escape with a DC 30 Strength or Dexterity check (action).

If the target dies while imprisoned, their body becomes part of Talaris's root network. He can speak through their corpse or reanimate it as a plant zombie.

---

LEGENDARY ACTIONS (6/round)

Talaris Bloomrend can take 6 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature's turn. Talaris regains spent legendary actions at the start of his turn.

Detect Life (Costs 1 Action)

Talaris uses Life Sense to pinpoint all living creatures within 1 mile, learning their exact HP, conditions, and health status. He gains advantage on all attacks against creatures he's sensed this way until his next turn.

Fungal Step (Costs 1 Action)

Talaris teleports up to 500 feet to any location with fungus or plant life. He can bring up to 6 willing creatures with him.

Rot Spread (Costs 2 Actions)

One creature within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 30 Constitution save or have their flesh begin rotting. They take 10d10 necrotic damage and are poisoned for 1 minute. The rot spreads\u2014any creature that touches them must also save or take 6d10 necrotic damage.

Bloom Barrier (Costs 2 Actions)

Talaris creates a wall of thorny vines in a line up to 120 feet long, 20 feet high, and 10 feet thick. The wall has AC 15, 300 HP, and provides total cover. Any creature trying to pass through takes 15d10 piercing damage and is restrained (DC 30 Strength check to break free).

Rapid Regeneration (Costs 3 Actions)

Talaris regrows one destroyed body part, heals 200 HP, and ends one condition affecting him. Fungal growths spread in a 60-foot radius around him, creating difficult terrain.

Garden of Corpses (Costs 4 Actions)

All corpses within 300 feet bloom into plant zombies (use Zombie statistics but with 100 HP, Plant type, and immunity to poison/necrotic). They serve until destroyed. Maximum of 4d6 zombies created.

Forced Evolution (Costs 5 Actions)

One creature within 300 feet undergoes rapid biological transformation. DC 30 Constitution save:
  • Failed: Take 15d10 force damage as their body mutates. Roll 1d6:
1. Grow bark armor (+5 AC but speed halved) 2. Sprout vines from limbs (can make melee attacks at 15 ft. but vulnerable to fire) 3. Develop fungal growths (regenerate 5d10 HP per round but are poisoned) 4. Transform partially into plant (immunity to poison but vulnerability to fire and slashing) 5. Age rapidly (aged 4d10 years) 6. Regress (de-aged 4d10 years) Transformation lasts 1 hour or until Greater Restoration cast at 7th level.

  • Successful save: No transformation, take half damage.
---

LAIR ACTIONS

On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Talaris Bloomrend can take one lair action to cause one of the following effects:

  • Verdant Surge: Explosive plant growth covers the lair. All surfaces become difficult terrain. Creatures standing on ground must make DC 28 Dexterity saves or be restrained by vines (escape DC 28). Plants provide half cover to all creatures.
  • Toxic Bloom: Flowers throughout the lair release poisonous pollen. All creatures must make DC 28 Constitution saves or be poisoned and blinded for 1 round. The pollen lingers\u2014any creature that starts its turn in the lair must save again.
  • Decomposer Swarm: 3d6 Rot Grubs emerge from decaying matter (use Swarm of Insects statistics but deal necrotic damage instead of piercing). They attack the nearest non-plant creatures and serve Talaris until destroyed.
  • Root Network Shift: The floor erupts with grasping roots. All creatures touching the ground are grappled (escape DC 28) and take 8d10 piercing damage. Grappled creatures take 6d10 piercing damage at the start of each turn.
  • Regenerative Pulse: All plant creatures and allies with plant characteristics in the lair heal 15d10 HP and have one condition removed. Fungal growths spread throughout the lair, releasing healing spores.
  • Corpse Bloom: All corpses in the lair bloom with flowers and vines, becoming plant zombies (as per Garden of Corpses legendary action). Additionally, any creature at 0 HP is stabilized but covered in fungal growth (disadvantage on Dexterity checks for 1 hour).
---

REGIONAL EFFECTS

The region within 50 miles of Talaris Bloomrend's domain is warped by his presence, creating the following effects:

  • Accelerated Growth: Plants grow at 10x normal speed. Forests become impenetrable jungles within days. Crops mature in hours. Gardens become overgrown wildernesses overnight. Beneficial but overwhelming\u2014civilization requires constant clearing.
  • Rapid Decay: Organic materials rot quickly. Food spoils within hours. Wood structures weaken and collapse. Leather armor degrades. Corpses decompose to bones within minutes. Preservation is nearly impossible.
  • Eternal Blooming: Plants bloom year-round, regardless of season. Flowers perpetually blossom and rot. The region smells of flowers and decay simultaneously\u2014beautiful and nauseating.
  • Fungal Infestation: Fungi grow everywhere\u2014on trees, buildings, skin, food, everything. Most are harmless, some are beneficial (edible, medicinal), but navigating the region means constant exposure to spores.
  • Regenerative Soil: Any corpse buried becomes exceptionally fertile soil. Within days, the grave blooms with new life. Cemeteries become gardens. Battlefields transform into flowering meadows. Death literally feeds life.
  • Cycle Sensitivity: Residents become acutely aware of life cycles. They can sense when plants are about to bloom, when creatures are ill, when death approaches. This awareness is useful but can be emotionally exhausting.
  • Rot Immunity Development: Long-term residents develop resistance to decay-related effects (advantage on saves against disease, poison, and necrotic damage from environmental sources). However, they also smell faintly of decomposition.
  • Plant Communication: People begin hearing whispers from plants\u2014not actual communication but instinctive understanding of plant needs, health, and warnings of danger. Druids and rangers find this region especially responsive.
  • Death Acceptance: The constant visible cycling of life and death creates a culture that accepts mortality without fear. Funerals are celebrations, death is seen as transformation, decay is beautiful. This can seem disturbing to outsiders.
  • The Verdant Pilgrimage: Dying creatures are drawn to the region instinctively, seeking to "return to the cycle." Elderly, terminally ill, and mortally wounded creatures travel here to die and bloom. The region is both hospice and garden.
---

MYTHIC TRAIT

The Infinite Garden

When Talaris is reduced to 0 hit points, he doesn't die he blooms explosively. His body detonates in a 500-foot radius explosion of spores, seeds, and fungal matter:

All creatures in the area must make DC 30 Constitution saves:

  • Failed: Take 20d10 poison damage and are infected with Talaris's Spores (same as Blightvine Lash effect)
  • Successful: Take half damage, no infection
The entire 500-foot radius becomes:
  • The Blooming Ground: A zone of hyper-fertility where:
- Massive plant growth covers everything (heavily obscured, difficult terrain) - Corpses bloom into plant creatures (automatic Corpse Garden effect every round) - Living creatures regenerate 10d10 HP per round but take 10d10 poison damage per round - Talaris's consciousness is distributed throughout all plant matter

While in Blooming Ground form, Talaris:

  • Cannot be targeted (he's everywhere and nowhere)
  • Acts through plant proxies (treats every plant as an extension of himself)
  • Can attack from any plant (treats entire area as his reach)
  • Regenerates the Blooming Ground if damaged (plants regrow instantly)
After 10 rounds in Blooming Ground form, one of two things happens:

Option 1 - Reconstitution: If organic matter remains, Talaris reforms from the largest plant mass at full HP, emerging as a massive tree-form with +50% HP and all abilities recharged.

Option 2 - Seeding: If the Blooming Ground is completely destroyed (fire, acid, radiant energy across entire area), Talaris doesn't reconstitute but instead scatters millions of seeds across 10 miles. Within 1d4 days, he'll regrow from one of these seeds at 25% HP in a random location.

Permanent Death: To truly kill Talaris, the party must:

1. Reduce him to 0 HP and trigger Blooming Ground 2. During the 10-round Blooming Ground phase, locate and destroy his Heart-Root\u2014the central root system hidden beneath the largest plant mass 3. The Heart-Root is buried 100 feet underground, accessible only by burrowing or destroying the ground above

The Heart-Root has:

  • AC: 25
  • HP: 3000
  • Immunity: All damage except fire, radiant, and acid
  • Regeneration: 400 HP per round (representing connection to all plants in Blooming Ground)
  • Defenders: 4d6 plant zombies spawn each round to defend it
Destroying the Heart-Root while he's in Blooming Ground form prevents reconstitution and seeding\u2014Talaris's consciousness dissipates entirely, finally ending the cycle.

Alternatively, if the party can find and burn his First Seed\u2014the original seed from which he first grew after claiming the Liver\u2014he loses his Eternal Cycle ability and can be killed normally. The First Seed is hidden in the Blooming Rot's geographic center, guarded by his most loyal plant servants.

---

LORE & BACKGROUND

Before the Usurpation, Talaris was a physician renowned for his work with terminal patients. He couldn't save them all, but he tried. He watched countless people die, and each death haunted him.

The Death-Obsessed Healer

Talaris's early life was defined by powerlessness against death:

  • He saved some, lost many
  • Terminal illness was his enemy
  • He studied regeneration, healing, life extension
  • He sought the secret to conquering death
But the more he studied, the more he realized: death is not the enemy. Death is part of the system.

The Revelation of Decay

His breakthrough came when studying decomposition. He observed:

  • Dead tissue doesn't just decay it transforms
  • Corpses feed soil, which feeds plants, which feed animals
  • Death creates life
  • Decay is regeneration for the ecosystem
  • What dies becomes what lives
This was the key: Death and life are the same process from different perspectives.

Discovery of the Cycle

Talaris became obsessed with the cycle:

  • Nothing truly dies\u2014it transforms
  • Decomposition is regeneration
  • The Maker built death into life intentionally
  • The body has organs specifically for this\u2014the liver filters toxins, regenerates, processes waste, creates new cells from old
The Maker's liver wasn't just an organ it was the principle of transformation encoded in flesh.

The Usurpation

When the conspiracy formed, Talaris joined because he believed the Maker's greatest sin was ending the cycle. By creating static reality, the Maker had interrupted the natural flow of death and rebirth. Everything was frozen in place.

He would claim the organs of decay and regeneration, and restart the cycle.

During the ritual murder, Talaris approached the dying Maker and removed not just the liver but the entire digestive and regenerative system\u2014liver, kidneys, spleen, lymphatic system everything involved in processing, filtering, and renewal.

The moment he claimed them:

  • The organs fused throughout his body
  • His flesh became a hybrid of plant, fungus, and animal
  • He gained mastery over the boundary between death and life
  • He could see the cycle in everything
  • He understood that he himself would never truly die he was now part of the cycle
He had become The Verdant King sovereign of rot and renewal.

His Philosophy

Talaris believes:

  • Death is beautiful (it enables new life)
  • Decay is sacred (it is transformation)
  • Clinging to life is unnatural (everything must change)
  • The cycle must continue (nothing should be permanent)
  • He is not evil (he's just being honest about reality)
He sees himself as reality's gardener\u2014pruning the old to make room for the new.

What He Collects

Talaris hoards:

  • Rare fungi and molds (especially those that consume unusual materials)
  • Seeds from extinct plants (planning to revive them)
  • Samples of every disease and poison (preserving them in his biology)
  • Corpses of legendary creatures (to bloom them into new life)
  • Compost from ancient civilizations (the decay of empires)
His greatest treasure: The First Rot\u2014a sample of the very first organic matter to ever decay, preserved from the dawn of life itself. It's the origin of the cycle.

His Ultimate Goal

If Talaris ever perfects his control over the cycle (currently not fully stable), he will initiate The Great Bloom:

  • All artificial stasis will end
  • Cities will be reclaimed by nature overnight
  • Immortal beings will finally die and bloom
  • Death will become rebirth automatically
  • The world will become an endless garden
Not destruction\u2014transformation. He would make death beautiful by making it productive.

To him, this is paradise: a world where nothing is wasted, where every ending is a beginning, where the cycle never stops.

---

PARTY ENCOUNTER — SESSION 19

Warning
The Fire-Bringers
The party entered Talaris's domain carrying preserved death (Mira Alethain's remains) and Azerach's brand (Jonas). They used fire twice — burning curious Bloom Hounds and killing a Thornback Behemoth — angering the forest.

What Happened

Day 1-2: The party traveled from Sporehaven through the Blooming Rot:

  • Sweetwater Marsh (Sol nearly succumbed to the Sweet Springs)
  • Pilgrim's Spine (burned Bloom Hounds — forest angered)
  • Hunger Garden (killed Thornback Behemoth with fire — Talaris spoke directly)
  • Congregation Grove (faced the Questioning)

The Congregation Grove Questioning

The Choir Blooms asked four questions: 1. What do you carry? (Mira's remains, the Bleeding Blade, the Maker's shard) 2. What do you seek? (Elias Alethain's body at Verdant Pass) 3. What have you done? (Jonas admitted to lying and burning) 4. What do you offer? (Sol offered to share Aedwynn's memory)

Sol's Offering — The Maker's Memory

Sol Raven used Shared Memory to show Talaris a vision of Aedwynn the Maker alive — the world before the Usurpation. The vision spread through the entire spore network to Talaris himself.

Talaris saw his creator for the first time since the Usurpation.

The Choir Blooms wept golden spores. The Verdant King "required a moment" to process what he'd seen.

"She was... beautiful."

"The offering is... accepted. Pass to the Wound. Complete your task."

"The fire is... the King will consider the fire in light of what you have given. This changes nothing. But it changes... everything."

Talaris and Azerach

Jonas asked about the "history" between Talaris and Azerach. The Choir Bloom explained:

  • Before the Usurpation, they were colleagues (physician and confessor)
  • Both served the Maker
  • After the Usurpation, they disagreed: Azerach hoards, Talaris cycles
  • They haven't spoken in 800 years
  • Jonas carries Azerach's brand through Talaris's domain — "a message he did not ask to receive"
"The Whispered King does not mark without purpose. You may not know the message. That does not mean there is no message."

Talaris is now "curious" about Jonas.

Current Status

AspectStatus
Party RelationshipPermitted passage; marked as "fire-bringers"
Fire OffenseSoftened by Sol's offering, not forgiven
Talaris's InterestCurious about Jonas (Azerach's brand)
Emotional StateRemembering what he was before becoming a monster
The WoundParty reached Verdant Pass; Mira's remains laid to rest
Talaris has seen the face of the god he helped murder. The implications of this remain to be seen.

---

COMBAT TACTICS

Talaris fights like an ecosystem\u2014overwhelming, patient, inevitable. He doesn't rush to kill; he converts enemies into resources for the cycle.

Pre-Combat: Garden Preparation

Before fighting, Talaris: 1. Uses Life Sense to know exactly how healthy/wounded each enemy is 2. Spreads fungal spores and seeds throughout the area (creating spawn points) 3. Poisons the ground (making terrain hostile) 4. Creates plant defenders (Bloom From Flesh on any available corpses)

By the time combat starts, he's fighting in his garden—and the garden is fighting for him.

Opening Strategy

Talaris opens with area denial: 1. Decomposition Wave to weaken everyone simultaneously and create fertile terrain 2. Root Prison on the primary damage dealer 3. Cycle of Renewal to age/damage the party while healing himself

His goal: Transform the battlefield into his domain within 2-3 rounds.

Mid-Combat Tactics

Once the environment favors him:

  • Blightvine Lash infects enemies with spores (delayed damage over time)
  • Spore Burst creates spreading poison (forcing movement)
  • Rot Touch reduces maximum HP (attrition warfare)
  • Fungal Step + Life Sense keeps him mobile and aware
He fights through environmental advantage—the longer the fight goes, the more the terrain becomes his ally.

Legendary Action Usage

1. Detect Life (1 action) — Constant awareness of enemy status 2. Fungal Step (1 action) — Mobility to avoid being pinned 3. Rot Spread (2 actions) — Continuous damage over time 4. Bloom Barrier (2 actions) — Terrain control and separation 5. Rapid Regeneration (3 actions) — Sustain through damage 6. Garden of Corpses (4 actions) — Turn deaths into advantages 7. Forced Evolution (5 actions) — Transform enemies into liabilities

Advanced Tactics

Talaris excels at patient warfare:

  • Against martial characters: Infect with spores, kite with Fungal Step, let poison do the work
  • Against casters: Use Bloom Barrier to break line of sight, Root Prison to lock them down
  • Against groups: Decomposition Wave followed by Cycle of Renewal creates mass attrition
He treats combat like composting—everything eventually breaks down.

Lair Action Coordination

His lair actions multiply his advantages:

  • Verdant Surge creates difficult terrain (slows enemies)
  • Toxic Bloom forces Constitution saves every round (attrition)
  • Decomposer Swarm adds more threats (action economy)
  • Root Network Shift controls positioning (grapples)
  • Regenerative Pulse heals his allies (sustain)
  • Corpse Bloom recycles deaths (nothing is wasted)

Below Half HP

When bloodied, Talaris becomes aggressive:

  • Uses Decomposition Wave every time it recharges
  • Spams Root Prison via legendary actions
  • Activates Cycle of Renewal to mass-heal
  • Creates maximum plant zombies with Garden of Corpses

Mythic Phase: The Blooming Ground

When reduced to 0 HP, he becomes the environment itself:

The party must: 1. Survive 20d10 poison damage per round (exhausting resources) 2. Fight through massive plant growth (heavily obscured terrain) 3. Deal with automatic corpse blooming (every death becomes an enemy) 4. Locate and destroy the Heart-Root (buried 100 feet down) 5. All while taking 10d10 poison damage per round from the environment

Fighting the Blooming Ground is a race against time and attrition—can they find and destroy the Heart-Root before they're overwhelmed?

---

WEAKNESSES & VULNERABILITIES

Despite his mastery of the cycle, Talaris has several exploitable weaknesses:

1. Fire and Radiant Damage

Fire and radiant energy are anti-growth:

  • Fire deals full damage and halves his regeneration for 1 round
  • Radiant damage prevents his Eternal Cycle for 1 minute per hit
  • Area-effect fire can stop spore spread and prevent plant zombie creation
  • During Blooming Ground phase, fire damage is quadrupled
A party built around fire and radiant damage can significantly hamper his abilities.

2. Unnatural/Inorganic Environments

Talaris's power depends on organic matter:

  • In areas with no soil (metal floors, stone dungeons, deserts), he cannot use Fungal Step or Root Prison
  • Without corpses or plant matter, he cannot create plant zombies
  • In sterile environments, his spores don't spread
  • His regeneration is halved if not touching organic matter
Fighting him in a sealed stone chamber with no plants or soil drastically reduces his power.

3. Stasis Effects

Anything that stops change hinders the cycle:

  • Time Stop, Temporal Stasis, or similar effects prevent his regeneration
  • Effects that prevent healing also prevent his Eternal Cycle
  • Petri fication or crystallization interrupts his transformation abilities
Magic that enforces permanence counters his philosophy of constant change.

4. Complete Destruction

If all organic matter within 120 feet is destroyed:

  • His Eternal Cycle cannot activate
  • His regeneration stops
  • His Fungal Step cannot function
  • His lair actions fail
Massive fire or acid damage across a wide area can prevent his resurrection.

5. The First Seed

His original transformation seed (hidden in the Blooming Rot) is his phylactery:

  • Destroying it removes his Eternal Cycle permanently
  • He can then be killed normally
  • He becomes mortal (though still extremely powerful)
Finding and destroying the First Seed is the surest path to permanent victory.

6. Anti-Life Effects

Ironically, effects that prevent life hamper him:

  • Necrotic damage from undead sources (his own necrotic damage doesn't heal him)
  • Antilife Shell or similar effects block his plant zombies
  • Void or negative energy drains his regeneration
Death magic wielded against him is poetically fitting.

7. Philosophical Weakness: Those Who Reject the Cycle

Creatures that refuse to participate in the natural cycle disturb him:

  • Constructs (never lived, cannot die)
  • Immortal beings with unnatural eternal life
  • Undead who've escaped the cycle
  • Anyone who's successfully cheated death permanently
He finds them unnatural and his attacks against them have disadvantage (they're "wrong" and he struggles to process them).

8. The Heart-Root

During his Blooming Ground phase, his Heart-Root is his vulnerability:

  • Buried underground (requires digging or burrowing)
  • Heavily defended by plant zombies
  • But only has immunity to non-fire/radiant/acid damage
  • Regenerates 400 HP per round but can be overwhelmed
A focused strike team can destroy it while others distract the plant defenders.

---

CAMPAIGN INTEGRATION NOTES

Thematic Significance

Talaris represents:

  • Acceptance of mortality
  • The cycle of death and rebirth
  • Transformation as continuity
  • The beauty of decay
  • Nature's neutrality toward suffering

Connection to Party Themes

In campaigns exploring mortality, legacy, or environmentalism, Talaris is the philosophical center. He challenges:

  • Characters seeking immortality (why resist the cycle?)
  • Characters grieving loss (death enables new life)
  • Characters fearing death (what if death is transformation, not ending?)

Encounter Scaling

As CR 34, Talaris is designed for level 19-20 parties. His regeneration, Eternal Cycle, and Blooming Ground phase make him exceptionally difficult to permanently kill.

However, his vulnerabilities to fire/radiant damage and dependence on organic environments mean parties who prepare can significantly reduce his power.

Resolution Paths

Defeating Talaris can happen through: 1. Direct Combat (hard—he regenerates endlessly) 2. Destroying First Seed (medium—infiltration quest) 3. Environmental Negation (use sterile environments to limit his power) 4. Philosophical Victory (convince him his cycle causes more harm than good)

Long-Term Consequences

If Talaris is defeated:

  • The Blooming Rot begins dying (no longer sustained by his power)
  • All Fungelites lose connection to the cycle (might die or become fully independent)
  • Decomposition returns to normal speed (both blessing and curse—less chaotic growth, but also less regeneration)
  • Plant zombies crumble
If Talaris survives and completes his cycle mastery:
  • The Great Bloom begins
  • All cities are reclaimed by nature
  • Death becomes automatic rebirth
  • Immortality becomes impossible
  • The world becomes an eternal garden
---

"You fear death because you think it's an ending. It's not. It's transformation. Every corpse becomes soil. Every seed becomes a tree. Every death becomes life. I am not your enemy—I'm just showing you what comes next. And what comes next is beautiful."

— Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King

---

FINAL NOTE: THE CYCLE'S GUARDIAN

Talaris Bloomrend is designed to be a philosophical foil for parties that value permanence, civilization, and control over nature. He represents the primal forces of decay and regeneration that underpin all existence.

Fighting him is fighting entropy itself—you can slow it, redirect it, delay it, but can you truly stop the cycle? Should you?

He is not evil (in his view, nor in nature's view). He simply is—like the turning of seasons, the falling of leaves, the decomposition of corpses. He is the inevitability of change made divine.

Defeating him requires answering: Is death a tragedy, or is it transformation? And if it's transformation, who gets to decide what form that takes?

The party must answer: What is the value of permanence in a universe built on cycles?

items.title: The Thirteen Sovereigns
items.content:
The Thirteen Sovereigns

Abstract
Overview
The Thirteen Sovereigns are the mortals who murdered Aedwynn the Maker and claimed the god's organs, reshaping them into domains of twisted reality. Each Sovereign embodies a fundamental aspect of existence corrupted by mortal obsession.

The Sovereigns

Info
1. Azerach the Whispered King
Organ: The Maker's Lungs
Domain: Dominion of Still Air
Concept: Silence, Truth, Breath
Influence: Unexplained ear-ringing, sense of being overheard, ears sprouting on surfaces

Info
2. Imhuran the Regent of Hours
Organ: The Heart of Time
Domain: The Clockwork Sanctum
Concept: Time, Judgment, Fairness
Influence: Clocks running backward, shadows lagging, wounds refusing to heal

Info
3. Mareatha the Vein-Mother
Organ: The Circulatory Engine
Domain: Veinspire Cathedral
Concept: Blood, Devotion, Purity
Influence: Vascular constructs, arterial baptisms, blood-based loyalty

Info
4. Eredain the Choirless Monarch
Organ: The Resonance Organ Domain: The Land of Distorted Gravity Concept: Sound, Emotion, Gravity, Resonance Influence: Objects levitating, emotional outbursts causing tremors, humming that vibrates bone

Info
5. Vortegas the Bone-Warden
Organ: The Skeleton Domain: The Ossuary Kingdom Concept: Structure, Permanence, Order Influence: Spontaneous bone growth, skeletal reorganization, stiffening joints

Info
6. Kaedris the Crimson Matron
Organ: The Left Heart Domain: Bastion of Fireblood Concept: Passion, Love, Jealousy, Heat Influence: Objects warming with emotion, spontaneous ignition, molten residue at heartbreak sites

Info
7. Aral-Vyn the Bladed Saint
Organ: The Hands and Sinews Domain: The Crucible of Flesh Concept: Perfection, Pain, Self-improvement Influence: Scars rearranging, pain sharpening senses, tools moving independently

Info
8. Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress
Organ: The Digestive Engine Domain: The Devoured Courts Concept: Hunger, Meaning, Consumption Influence: Nausea, ideas feeling thin, meaning slipping away, existential hunger

Info
9. Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd
Organ: The Skin and Boundary Domain: The Blackfold Concept: Identity, Fear, Reflection, Boundary Influence: Shadows moving independently, mirrors fogging, reflections whispering

Info
10. Ismara the Pale Duchess
Organ: The Brain Domain: The Ivory Vale Concept: Knowledge, Thought, Enlightenment Influence: Intrusive thoughts, memories turning brittle, knowledge taking physical form

Info
11. Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King
Organ: The Liver Domain: The Blooming Rot Concept: Regeneration, Decay, Cycle, Rebirth Influence: Flowers blooming fast, corpses sprouting roots, healing and infection together

Info
12. Nhalyra the Azure Queen
Organ: The Soulflow Domain: The Azure Depths Concept: Consciousness, Identity, Dreams, Memory Influence: Dreams blending between sleepers, memories washing away, feeling far from oneself

Info
13. Karthayne the Hollow Crown
Organ: The Heart of Creation Domain: The Absent Throne Concept: Creation, Destruction, Potential, Contradiction Influence: Hallucinations of alternate lives, objects existing and not existing simultaneously

---

The Usurpation

Danger
The Ritual Murder
The murder of Aedwynn was not a battle—it was a ritual. Each of the Thirteen stole an organ with a specific obsession driving them:
  • Azerach sought perfect silence to hear all truths
  • Imhuran sought perfect time to judge all crimes
  • Mareatha sought perfect blood to purify all hearts
  • Eredain sought perfect emotion to restore cosmic harmony
  • Vortegas sought perfect structure to end chaos
  • Kaedris sought perfect passion to prove devotion
  • Aral-Vyn sought perfect form through painful refinement
  • Vor'Kael sought perfect understanding by consuming meaning
  • Ultharion sought perfect truth by exposing hidden selves
  • Ismara sought perfect knowledge regardless of cost
  • Talaris sought perfect cycle of death and rebirth
  • Nhalyra sought perfect peace by dissolving selfhood
  • Karthayne sought perfect creation through destruction
Warning
Consequences
When the Maker died, reality fractured. The organs became realms. The killers became gods.

---

Current Relevance

Check
Party Interactions
The party has directly interfered with multiple Sovereign domains:
  • Quieted three hearts in Veinspire
  • Drawn Azerach's attention (Jonas's brand)
  • Freed Fursen from Imhuran's temporal prison
  • Revealed Kael as Vor'Kael's vomited anomaly
---

Related

  • Campaign Index - The Ashen Realms
  • History & Timeline
  • The Party
