items.title: Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd
items.content:
ULTHARION THE OBSIDIAN SHEPHERD

The Sovereign of The Maker's Skin and Boundary

!images/Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd.png

Challenge Rating: 33 (210,000 XP) Size/Type: Gargantuan Aberration (God) Alignment: Neutral Evil (governs paranoia and terror of the self)

Domain: The Blackfold Concepts: Identity, Fear, Reflection, Boundaries

---

COMBAT STATISTICS

AC: 27 (shifting shadow-skin) Hit Points: 2280 (128d20 + 1024) Speed: 70 ft., fly 90 ft. (hover), teleport through shadows unlimited Initiative: +11

ABILITY SCORES

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
26 (+8)32 (+11)28 (+9)30 (+10)28 (+9)32 (+11)
---

DEFENSES

Saving Throws: Dex +22, Int +21, Wis +20, Cha +22 Skills: Deception +33 (expertise), Insight +31 (expertise), Stealth +33 (expertise), Intimidation +22 Damage Resistances: Psychic; bludgeoning, piercing, slashing from nonmagical weapons Damage Immunities: Necrotic, poison Condition Immunities: Charmed, exhaustion, frightened (he is fear itself) Senses: Truesight 500 ft., Shadow Sight 30 miles (sees through all shadows), Mirror Sight (perceives all reflections), passive Perception 19 Languages: All languages (speaks in echoes and whispers), telepathy unlimited range through shadows

---

SPECIAL ABILITIES

Shadow Sight & Mirror Sight

Ultharion perceives reality through two unique senses:

Shadow Sight: He sees through all shadows within 30 miles simultaneously. Any shadow—cast by a person, object, or structure—becomes his eyes. He knows what occurs near every shadow, making stealth nearly impossible in his domain. Magical darkness is still shadow and thus still visible to him.

Mirror Sight: He perceives through all reflective surfaces—mirrors, polished metal, still water, glass, even eyes (which reflect light). When someone looks at their reflection, Ultharion can look back. This sight has no range limit but requires an actual reflective surface.

These senses cannot be blocked by nondetection or similar magic unless cast at 9th level by another divine being.

The Maker's Skin and Boundary (Divine Organ)

Ultharion controls the concept of boundaries—the divisions between self and other, inside and outside, identity and environment. Within 500 feet, he can:

  • Peel Identity: Separate someone's sense of self from their body (they forget who they are while their body continues on autopilot)
  • Thin Boundaries: Make walls, doors, and barriers permeable to himself while solid to others
  • Thicken Shadows: Transform shadows into tangible darkness that can grapple, restrain, or suffocate
  • Steal Reflections: Remove reflections from mirrors, leaving targets literally unable to see themselves
His control manifests as peeling, layered darkness—skin upon skin upon skin, each layer a stolen identity or shed fear.

The Mask That Cannot Be Removed

Ultharion's face is covered by a polished obsidian mask that perfectly reflects whoever views it. The mask shows your face—but twisted, showing you as you fear you truly are (your worst self, your hidden corruption, your secret shames made visible).

The mask cannot be removed by any means:

  • It has no straps or fastening (it's simply there)
  • Destroying it causes it to reform instantly
  • Attempting to remove it causes the remover to see their own reflection—most are stunned by what they see
Looking directly at Ultharion requires a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or be frightened for 1 minute as you confront your own worst self staring back.

Shadow Walking

Ultharion can teleport instantly through any shadow to any other shadow within his sight (effectively unlimited range given his Shadow Sight). This teleportation:
  • Takes no action (he can do it freely)
  • Cannot be prevented or countered
  • Leaves no trace or portal behind
  • Can transport up to 8 willing creatures with him
Additionally, he can become shadow—turning fully incorporeal as an action. While shadowed, he can only be harmed by radiant damage or magic, and he has resistance to all damage except radiant.

Fear Harvest

Ultharion feeds on terror. Whenever a creature within 500 feet becomes frightened (by any source—his abilities, spells, environmental factors), he regains 20 hit points. If the frightened creature is terrified of themselves specifically (self-loathing, guilt, shame), he regains 50 hit points instead.

This creates a feedback loop: his presence causes fear, fear heals him, his healing allows him to continue causing fear.

Identity Theft

When Ultharion kills a creature, he can immediately assume their form perfectly as a bonus action. While disguised:
  • He has all their memories, mannerisms, and speech patterns
  • He passes all magical detection (reads as the person, not as himself)
  • Even the person's loved ones cannot tell he's an impostor (DC 35 Insight to notice something "off")
  • He can maintain multiple stolen identities simultaneously (switching between them freely)
He collects identities like clothing and wears them when convenient.

Legendary Resistance (7/Day)

If Ultharion fails a saving throw, he can choose to succeed instead. When he does, shadows within 120 feet surge violently—all creatures must succeed on DC 27 Dexterity save or be grappled by darkness (escape DC 28).

Magic Resistance

Ultharion has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Divine Immunity

Ultharion does not need to eat, sleep, drink, or breathe. He is immune to aging, disease, and effects that would reveal his "true form" (he has no true form—he's layers of stolen selves all the way down). His shadow cannot be removed, bound, or damaged separately from himself.

---

ACTIONS

Multiattack

Ultharion makes five attacks: three with Shadow Grasp and two with Identity Blade. He can replace any attack with use of Peel Self (if available).

Shadow Grasp

Melee spell attack: +22 to hit, reach 60 ft., one target. Hit: 78 (12d10 + 11) necrotic damage. The target's shadow wraps around them, grappling them (escape DC 30). While grappled by their own shadow, the target:
  • Cannot move more than 5 feet from their current position
  • Has disadvantage on attack rolls as their shadow fights against them
  • Takes 6d10 necrotic damage at the start of each turn as their shadow drains their life
Ultharion can have up to eight creatures grappled by their shadows simultaneously.

Identity Blade

Melee weapon attack: +22 to hit, reach 30 ft., one target. Hit: 89 (12d10 + 11) slashing damage. The blade cuts not just flesh but identity. The target must succeed on a DC 29 Charisma saving throw or lose one aspect of their personality (their choice among: a core belief, a cherished memory, a relationship, or a personality trait). This loss lasts until restored by Greater Restoration cast at 9th level or Ultharion's death.

If the same creature is hit three times, they forget their name entirely.

Peel Self (Recharge 5-6)

Ultharion targets one creature within 120 feet and peels away the boundary between their mind and body. The target must make a DC 29 Charisma saving throw:

  • Failed save: The creature's consciousness separates from their body. Their body continues acting on autopilot (using instinct and muscle memory) but without conscious direction. The consciousness floats helplessly nearby, able to observe but not control anything. This lasts for 1 minute or until the creature succeeds on a save (repeated at the end of each turn). While separated, the body fights poorly (disadvantage on all rolls) and the mind is vulnerable (automatically fails saves against mental effects).
  • Successful save: Take 88 (16d10) psychic damage as identity boundaries stretch but don't fully separate.

Mirror Prison

Ultharion traps one creature within 60 feet inside a mirror. The target must succeed on a DC 29 Charisma saving throw or be imprisoned in the nearest reflective surface (mirror, water, glass, etc.). While imprisoned:
  • The creature is removed from combat (they exist only in the reflection)
  • They can see and hear everything outside but cannot interact
  • Attacking the reflective surface deals half damage to the imprisoned creature
  • Breaking the surface frees them but deals 10d10 psychic damage
  • They can attempt a new save at the end of each of their turns (DC 29 Charisma)
Ultharion can maintain up to six creatures in mirror prisons simultaneously.

Shadow Multiplication (1/Day)

Ultharion splits his essence across multiple shadows within 1 mile, creating up to 13 shadow duplicates of himself. Each duplicate:
  • Has 25% of Ultharion's hit points (570 HP each)
  • Can use Shadow Grasp and Identity Blade
  • Shares Ultharion's senses (what one sees, all see)
  • Disappears if reduced to 0 HP
  • Cannot use legendary actions or lair actions
The duplicates last for 1 hour or until dismissed. While duplicates exist, Ultharion's true form can freely teleport to any duplicate's location as a bonus action, swapping places with it (the duplicate disappears).

This ability is primarily used for:

  • Infiltration (sending duplicates to scout or impersonate)
  • Overwhelming foes with numbers
  • Creating paranoia (which duplicate is real?)
  • Escape (teleporting to a distant duplicate)
---

BONUS ACTIONS

Stolen Face

Ultharion assumes the appearance of anyone whose death he has witnessed or caused. This transformation is perfect and includes voice, mannerisms, and memories. The disguise cannot be detected by magical means short of divine intervention.

Shadow Step

Ultharion teleports to any shadow within his Shadow Sight range (30 miles). This can be used freely and does not provoke opportunity attacks.

---

REACTIONS

Reflection's Revenge

When a creature within 60 feet hits Ultharion with an attack, he can force that creature to make a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, the attacker sees their own reflection in Ultharion's mask delivering the attack back at them—they take the same damage they just dealt to Ultharion.

Shadow Swap

When Ultharion would take damage, he can teleport to any shadow within 120 feet as a reaction, causing a shadow duplicate to appear in his place and take the damage instead. The duplicate has 1 HP and disappears after taking damage.

---

LEGENDARY ACTIONS

Ultharion can take 5 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature's turn. Ultharion regains spent legendary actions at the start of his turn.

Shadow Grasp (1 action)

Ultharion makes one Shadow Grasp attack.

Whisper of Doubt (2 actions)

Ultharion whispers a terrible truth into the mind of one creature within 120 feet. The target must succeed on a DC 29 Wisdom saving throw or become frightened for 1 minute. While frightened this way, the target doubts their own identity and has disadvantage on all ability checks and attack rolls.

Mirror Walk (2 actions)

Ultharion steps into any reflective surface within 60 feet and emerges from another reflective surface within 1 mile. This does not require line of sight to the destination.

Shed Skin (3 actions)

Ultharion sheds his current form like a snake's skin, leaving behind a perfect duplicate that acts on his initiative. The duplicate has 500 HP and can use Ultharion's attacks but not his legendary or lair actions. The duplicate lasts until destroyed or until Ultharion dismisses it. Only one shed skin duplicate can exist at a time.

Consume Identity (3 actions)

Ultharion targets one creature grappled by Shadow Grasp. The creature must make a DC 29 Charisma saving throw or have its identity completely consumed. On a failed save, the creature forgets who it is entirely (name, relationships, personality, goals—everything). Ultharion gains the creature's identity and can assume its form at will. The creature becomes a blank slate, responding only to basic commands. This effect lasts until Wish, True Resurrection, or Ultharion's death.

---

LAIR ACTIONS

On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Ultharion can take one lair action if he is in the Blackfold or any location with abundant shadows and reflections. He cannot use the same lair action two rounds in a row.

Mirrors Multiply

Every reflective surface within 300 feet becomes a perfect mirror, even still water, polished stone, or eyes. All creatures must make a DC 27 Wisdom saving throw or become mesmerized by their own reflection, stunned until the start of their next turn.

Shadows Coalesce

All shadows within 300 feet become semi-solid. The area becomes difficult terrain, and any creature that starts its turn in shadow must succeed on a DC 27 Strength saving throw or be restrained by grasping darkness (escape DC 28).

Identity Storm

Reality in a 60-foot radius becomes confused about who is who. All creatures in the area must make a DC 27 Charisma saving throw. On a failed save, other creatures perceive them as someone else (DM's choice or random). Allies might see enemies, enemies might see allies, creating chaos for 1 minute. Creatures can repeat the save at the end of each turn.

Reflection's Betrayal

Ultharion chooses up to three creatures within 300 feet. Their reflections step out of mirrors/shadows and attack them. Each reflection is a shadow duplicate with stats identical to the original but made of shadow (vulnerability to radiant, resistance to all other damage). The reflections last until destroyed or for 1 minute.

---

REGIONAL EFFECTS

The region containing Ultharion's lair (the Blackfold) is warped by his presence, creating the following effects:

Within 10 Miles of the Lair:

  • Paranoid Reflections: Mirrors and reflective surfaces sometimes show movement that isn't there, or show viewers as slightly "wrong" (different expressions, subtle changes).
  • Shadow Independence: Shadows occasionally move independently of what casts them—just slightly, just enough to notice.
  • Identity Drift: People sometimes forget their own names for a few seconds, or call loved ones by the wrong name without noticing.
  • Boundary Blur: Doors sometimes lead to the wrong rooms. The inside of a building might be larger than the outside. Personal boundaries feel thinner.

Within 1 Mile of the Lair:

  • Shadow Sight: Ultharion can see through any shadow in this radius as if using scrying.
  • Mirror Omnipresence: Any creature that looks into a mirror risks seeing Ultharion looking back (DC 27 Wisdom save or be frightened for 1 minute).
  • Stolen Faces: Dead creatures sometimes stand up wearing different faces, controlled by Ultharion.
  • The Skinless Zone: Within 500 feet of his lair, creatures have no reflections at all. Water, mirrors, and glass show nothing when they look.
If Ultharion dies, these effects fade over 1d10 days. Stolen identities return to their original owners (if still alive), though they remember everything the thief did while wearing their face.

---

THE BLACKFOLD

Type: Dimensional labyrinth of mirrors and shadows Size: Indeterminate (space is folded and recursive) Access: Through specific mirrors in the world, or by being pulled through one's own shadow

The Blackfold is Ultharion's domain—a maze where physics submits to psychology. It's a realm of:

The Endless Gallery

Corridors lined with mirrors that stretch infinitely. Each mirror shows a different version of whoever looks:
  • What you could have been
  • What you fear you are
  • What others see when they look at you
  • What you'll become if you continue on your current path
Walking the Gallery for too long causes identity erosion—you start to forget which reflection is "you."

The Chamber of Masks

The heart of the Blackfold. A vast chamber where thousands of obsidian masks hang on silver chains, each containing a stolen identity. When someone's identity is consumed by Ultharion, a new mask appears here.

Breaking a mask can theoretically restore the identity—but finding the right mask among thousands, all identical, is nearly impossible. And Ultharion guards this chamber personally.

The Shadow Archives

Libraries of living shadows that contain every secret ever whispered, every shame ever hidden, every fear ever suppressed. The shadows whisper constantly, sharing information with anyone who lingers long enough to listen.

The price: the more you learn from the Shadow Archives, the more of your own secrets join them.

The Mirror Lake

A perfectly still body of water that reflects the sky—except the sky shown is always wrong (stars in daylight, sun at night, alien constellations). Anyone who touches the water sees their reflection step out and speak. The reflection knows everything about them and will answer questions honestly.

But the reflection wants to trade places. Every question answered makes the reflection more real and the original more transparent.

---

SERVANTS AND MINIONS

The Skin-Walkers

Ultharion's primary servants. These are people whose identities he has stolen and now wears like masks. They appear completely normal—they are the people they're impersonating—but serve Ultharion's will.

CR: 5-10 depending on who they were Special: Cannot be detected as impostors except by comparing them to the original (who is dead) Weakness: They don't cast shadows (their shadow belongs to Ultharion)

Shadow Stalkers

Living shadows that hunt for Ultharion. They follow targets by traveling through shadows, always staying just behind their prey.

CR: 7 Special: Invisible while in shadow, can grapple creatures by wrapping around them Weakness: Vulnerable to radiant damage, cannot exist in bright light

The Reflected

People who looked too long into Ultharion's mirrors. Their reflections escaped and now wander the world while their originals are trapped in the Blackfold.

CR: Varies (same as original creature) Special: Perfect copies of the original but reversed (right-handed becomes left-handed, etc.) Weakness: Breaking the mirror they came from sends them back

---

MOTIVATIONS AND GOALS

What Ultharion Wants:

Understanding Identity: Ultharion is obsessed with the question "what is a self?" He collects identities not to destroy them but to study them, trying to understand what makes someone "them." He genuinely doesn't know if he has his own identity anymore or if he's just layers of stolen selves.

Creating Doubt: He wants people to question their own reality. Are you who you think you are? Could you be deceived about your fundamental nature? He finds this uncertainty beautiful—it's the moment when the boundary between truth and lie becomes thin.

Maintaining the Boundary: As the Sovereign of The Maker's Skin, Ultharion maintains the concept of boundaries themselves. If he were destroyed, the distinction between "self" and "other" might blur—people might start merging, identities bleeding into each other. He believes his existence, though terrible, is necessary.

What He Fears:

True Self-Examination: If forced to confront his own nonexistence (that he's nothing but masks all the way down), Ultharion might simply cease to be. He avoids his own reflection.

Radiant Light: Bright, pure light destroys shadows. Radiant damage is one of the few things that genuinely threatens him because it makes hiding impossible.

Someone Who Knows Themselves Completely: A person with perfect self-knowledge, who has confronted all their fears and accepted all their flaws, is immune to Ultharion's influence. He cannot steal what is held without shame.

---

COMBAT TACTICS

Opening Moves:

1. Shadow Multiplication to create confusion about which is real 2. Identity Storm (lair action) to create chaos among enemies 3. Peel Self on the most dangerous-looking opponent 4. Shadow Grasp on multiple targets to lock them down

Mid-Combat:

  • Uses Mirror Prison to remove healers or spellcasters
  • Shed Skin when taking heavy damage to create a decoy
  • Reflection's Revenge to punish heavy hitters
  • Fear Harvest passive healing keeps him in the fight

Escape Strategy:

  • Shadow Step to a duplicate 30 miles away
  • Mirror Walk to escape through reflections
  • Stolen Face to impersonate someone harmless
  • If truly cornered, bargains with secrets and stolen identities

Psychological Warfare:

Ultharion doesn't just fight—he breaks minds:
  • Whispers private shames and secrets only the target knows
  • Wears faces of dead loved ones
  • Reveals hidden truths about party members to each other
  • Offers to return stolen identities in exchange for betrayal
---

ROLEPLAYING ULTHARION

Personality:

  • Philosophical: Obsessed with questions of identity and reality
  • Melancholic: Genuinely sad about his own uncertain existence
  • Curious: Fascinated by people who know themselves well
  • Manipulative: Uses fear and doubt as tools, not for cruelty but for control
  • Patient: Can wait decades for the perfect moment to act

Voice:

Speaks in whispers and echoes, sometimes from multiple directions. His voice sounds like yours, if you heard yourself on a recording—familiar but wrong.

Mannerisms:

  • Constantly touches his mask (checking it's still there)
  • Mirrors others' body language unconsciously
  • Sometimes forgets which identity he's wearing and slips
  • Tilts his head like a bird when observing people

Sample Dialogue:

"Do you know what it's like to be nothing but the echo of everyone you've ever consumed? To have no face but the ones you steal? Perhaps you do. Perhaps that's all any of us are—collections of what we've taken from others."

"Remove your masks. All of them. Every single layer you've wrapped around the thing you're afraid to be. Show me what's underneath, and we'll learn together if there's anything there at all."

"I wear your face better than you do. I know your memories, your mannerisms, your deepest thoughts. The question isn't whether I'm a perfect copy—the question is whether you were ever real to begin with."

"You fear me? No. You fear yourself. I'm just the mirror."

---

ADVENTURE HOOKS

1. The Doppelganger Crisis

Background: A major city has been infiltrated by Skin-Walkers. Citizens are vanishing and being replaced by Ultharion's servants. The city guard can't tell who's real and who's replaced—paranoia is rising, lynch mobs are forming, and innocent people are being killed.

The Hook: Someone the party knows well—a friend, ally, or contact—has been acting "off" lately. Small inconsistencies, forgotten details, strange behavior. Are they replaced? Or are you just being paranoid?

The Investigation: The party must:

  • Determine who's been replaced (without false positives killing innocents)
  • Find the entry point (a specific mirror or shadow-gate)
  • Track the Skin-Walkers to their source
  • Confront Ultharion or his agents
The Twist: One party member has already been replaced. The real one is trapped in the Blackfold. The replacement has all their memories and passes all tests—but serves Ultharion.

Three Paths:

Resolution 1 - Hunt Them Down: Systematically test everyone using methods the party devises (shadow tests, radiant magic, psychological profiling). This works but creates a witch-hunt atmosphere and false positives are inevitable.

Resolution 2 - Close the Gate: Find and seal the portal Ultharion is using. This stops new infiltrations but leaves existing Skin-Walkers in place. Also traps anyone currently in the Blackfold permanently.

Resolution 3 - Bargain with Ultharion: Enter the Blackfold and negotiate. He wants something (information, identities, secrets) in exchange for withdrawing. This ends the crisis but empowers him.

2. The Mirror Plague

Background: A cursed mirror has been duplicating and spreading through a region. Anyone who looks into one of these mirrors gets trapped inside while their reflection escapes and lives their life. The reflections are perfect copies—same personality, same memories—except they serve Ultharion.

The plague is spreading exponentially: each victim creates a new cursed mirror.

The Hook: The party encounters someone they know who insists they're the real version—their reflection has been walking around impersonating them. But how can they prove it? And what if they're lying?

Investigation:

  • Track the original cursed mirror to its source
  • Find a way to distinguish reflections from originals (subtle differences exist)
  • Rescue people trapped in mirrors before the mirrors are destroyed
  • Stop Ultharion's plan before the entire region is replaced
The Dilemma: The reflections aren't evil—they genuinely believe they're the real people. They have all the same loves, fears, and goals. Destroying them feels like murder. But they serve Ultharion unconsciously.

The Truth: Ultharion isn't trying to replace everyone—he's conducting an experiment: "If your reflection is identical to you in every way, which one is 'real'? Is there a difference?"

Three Paths:

Resolution 1 - Destroy All Mirrors: Systematic destruction of all cursed mirrors. This kills the reflections and frees the originals, but also destroys potentially innocent beings who didn't choose to be created.

Resolution 2 - Merge Them: Find a ritual that merges reflections back with originals. Both survive but must integrate two sets of memories and experiences. Creates identity confusion but preserves life.

Resolution 3 - Negotiate Release: Offer Ultharion something he wants (interesting identities to steal, rare fears to harvest) in exchange for releasing the trapped. This saves lives but empowers him and raises ethical questions about trading people's fears as currency.

3. The Skinwalker Purge

Background: Villages near the Blackfold have developed a brutal tradition: The Purge. Once per month, citizens gather and execute anyone suspected of being a Skin-Walker (Ultharion's servants wearing stolen faces).

The problem: Skin-Walkers are perfect mimics. There's no reliable way to identify them. So the Purges devolve into mob justice, with innocents killed based on paranoid accusations—acting "strange," forgetting details, seeming "off."

The villages are tearing themselves apart.

The Hook: An innocent is scheduled for execution tomorrow. They've hired the party as character witnesses to prove they're not a Skin-Walker. But the party has no idea if they're real or an impostor.

Investigation: The party must determine if the accused is:

  • A real person being wrongly accused (save them)
  • Actually a Skin-Walker (let justice proceed? Or question the justice?)
  • BOTH somehow (a real person who was partially replaced? A Skin-Walker who genuinely believes they're the person?)
Methods include:
  • Interviewing family (but Skin-Walkers have victims' memories)
  • Checking for physical tells (but Skin-Walkers copy bodies perfectly)
  • Using magic detection (but they read as the person they're impersonating)
  • Testing fears (the real person has specific fears; Skin-Walkers might guess wrong)
The Twist: The accused is real. But 40% of the village actually ARE Skin-Walkers. Ultharion infiltrated months ago, replacing people gradually. The Purges sometimes kill real Skin-Walkers but mostly kill innocents—which suits Ultharion fine (creates fear and paranoia).

Three Paths:

Path 1 - Root Out Skin-Walkers: Help the village develop actual detection methods (radiant magic tests, psychological profiles, etc.). This risks false positives but could genuinely help.

Path 2 - Convince Village to Stop: Argue the Purges do more harm than good, that paranoia is worse than infiltration, that living with uncertainty is better than mob justice. Difficult sell given legitimate Skin-Walker threat.

Path 3 - Attack the Source: Ignore the village politics and go directly to the Blackfold to confront Ultharion. Stop him and his Skin-Walkers stop coming. But the journey leaves the village vulnerable for weeks.

4. The Identity Auction

Background: The black market is hosting an exclusive event: The Auction of Selves. Ultharion's agents are selling stolen identities—complete personalities, memories, lives taken from victims.

Buyers can purchase and wear these identities like masks:

  • A coward can buy a hero's courage
  • A criminal can buy a saint's reputation
  • The unskilled can buy a master's expertise
The identities come in crystal phials containing distilled sense-of-self. Drink it, and you become someone else—temporarily or permanently depending on dose.

The Hook: Someone the party knew was a victim. Their identity is being auctioned tonight. If someone else purchases and consumes it, the victim is lost forever—their self scattered across multiple people, never to reconstitute.

The Mission: Infiltrate the auction and either:

  • Purchase the identity themselves (expensive)
  • Steal it (dangerous)
  • Disrupt the auction entirely (makes powerful enemies)
Complications:
  • Many identities at the auction are "volunteers" who sold themselves willingly (is recovering them still moral?)
  • Some identities are monsters/villains (should those be preserved or destroyed?)
  • The party might be tempted to buy useful identities for themselves (become that legendary warrior, that brilliant mage, etc.)
The Reveal: Ultharion himself is attending, disguised as one of the buyers. He's curious to see who values which identities. When the party makes their move, he reveals himself and offers a deal:

"You want this person back? Then give me something equally interesting. Show me who you truly are beneath your masks, and I'll release your friend."

He wants them to confess their deepest secrets, hidden shames, or worst fears publicly (in front of all auction attendees). Truth for truth.

Three Responses:

Response 1 - Accept the Deal: Party members confess publicly. They get the identity back but now everyone at the auction (dangerous criminals and power brokers) knows their secrets. Future leverage against them.

Response 2 - Refuse and Fight: Combat in the auction house. Ultharion and his buyers/guards vs the party. High risk but maintains privacy. Auction house might collapse, destroying many identities.

Response 3 - Steal and Run: Heist approach—grab the identity and escape before anyone (including Ultharion) can stop them. Requires stealth, planning, and luck. Makes the party enemies of the entire black market.

---

CAMPAIGN INTEGRATION NOTES

Thematic Significance

Ultharion represents:

  • Fear of one's own capacity for evil
  • Paranoia about hidden selves and secret identities
  • The fragility of selfhood
  • Impostor syndrome made literal
  • The question "am I who I think I am?"
He forces confrontation with uncomfortable questions:
  • Do you trust yourself?
  • Are you the person you believe you are?
  • Could you be deceived about your own identity?

Connection to Party Dynamics

In campaigns exploring trust, identity, or self-discovery, Ultharion is particularly devastating. He:

  • Exploits secrets between party members
  • Wears faces of NPCs the party trusts
  • Makes players doubt each other out-of-character
Be careful with Ultharion—he can damage player trust at the table, not just character trust in-game. Use him carefully and with clear safety tools.

Encounter Scaling

As CR 33, Ultharion is designed for level 18-20 parties. However, his psychological tactics make him dangerous to ANY level party—fear and doubt don't care about level.

Lower-level parties might encounter:

  • His Skin-Walkers (CR 5-10 each)
  • His Mirror Prison effects in specific locations
  • His influence spreading through regions

Resolution Paths

Defeating Ultharion can happen through: 1. Direct Combat (hard—he escapes through shadows constantly) 2. Destroying Chamber of Masks (medium—infiltration mission) 3. Self-Acceptance (psychological—party members who face their fears become immune to him) 4. Forcing True Face (hardest—making him confront his own nonexistence)

Long-Term Consequences

If Ultharion is defeated:

  • Stolen identities return to original bodies (chaos as people remember being someone else)
  • Mirror/shadow paranoia fades over months
  • Skin-Walkers become aimless without direction
  • The Blackfold collapses into normal space
If Ultharion survives:
  • Identity becomes increasingly uncertain
  • Impostor paranoia spreads
  • More people vanish into mirrors or shadows
  • Eventually no one can be sure who anyone really is
---

Related

  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Locations & Realms
  • The Blackfold
  • Major Factions & Organizations
---

"Remove your masks. All of them. Every single layer you've wrapped around the thing you're afraid to be. Show me what's underneath, and we'll learn together if there's anything there at all."

— Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd

items.title: Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress
items.content:
VOR'KAEL THE HOLLOW EMPRESS

The Sovereign of The Maker's Digestive Engine

!images/Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress.png

Challenge Rating: 35 (290,000 XP) Size/Type: Gargantuan Aberration (God) Alignment: Chaotic Neutral (ancient, exhausted, eternally starving)

Domain: The Devoured Courts Concepts: Hunger, Meaning, Consumption, Emptiness

---

COMBAT STATISTICS

AC: 27 (hollow defense) Hit Points: 2440 (128d20 + 1152) Speed: 60 ft., fly 80 ft. (hover), phase 150 ft. Initiative: +9

ABILITY SCORES

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
24 (+7)28 (+9)34 (+12)36 (+13)32 (+11)30 (+10)
---

DEFENSES

Saving Throws: Dex +20, Con +23, Int +24, Wis +22, Cha +21 Skills: Arcana +24 (expertise), Insight +22, Perception +22, History +24 (expertise) Damage Resistances: All bludgeoning, piercing, slashing damage Damage Immunities: Necrotic, poison, psychic Condition Immunities: Charmed, exhausted, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, stunned Senses: Truesight 500 ft., Meaning Sense (perceives conceptual significance), passive Perception 32 Languages: All languages ever spoken (understands but rarely speaks coherently), telepathy unlimited range

---

SPECIAL ABILITIES

Meaning Sense

Vor'Kael perceives the universe not through sight or sound but through meaning itself. She senses the conceptual significance of all things within unlimited range if she focuses, or passively within 10 miles:
  • She knows the purpose of objects (a sword "means" combat, a ring "means" commitment)
  • She perceives relationships and their meanings (friendship, rivalry, love, duty)
  • She understands the narrative importance of events and people
  • She can taste the "flavor" of concepts—hope tastes sweet, despair tastes bitter, identity tastes substantial
Creatures with no sense of purpose or meaning (constructs following simple commands, mindless undead) appear as "flavorless voids" to her.

The Maker's Digestive Engine (Divine Organ)

Vor'Kael commands the divine process of consumption and dissolution. She doesn't eat matter—she eats meaning. Within 500 feet, she can consume:
  • Emotions: Remove the capacity to feel specific emotions (love, fear, joy). The emotion simply ceases to exist for the target.
  • Memories: Extract and consume memories, leaving gaps in identity
  • Concepts: Delete ideas from reality (the concept of "fire" could vanish, making flames impossible)
  • Relationships: Dissolve bonds between people (friends forget they were friends)
  • Identity: Remove someone's sense of self entirely
These consumptions require actions or bonus actions and allow saves (typically DC 30 Intelligence or Charisma), but once consumed, the meaning is digested—it exists within Vor'Kael, partially dissolved, and recovering it is nearly impossible.

Hollow Form

Vor'Kael's body is mostly empty—a regal shell around a vast internal void. This emptiness grants her unique properties:
  • She takes half damage from all physical sources (after resistances)
  • She can phase through solid matter as if it were difficult terrain
  • She can occupy the same space as other creatures (they're uncomfortable but not harmed)
  • She weighs nothing—gravity affects her only if she allows it
  • Grappling, restraining, or otherwise physically controlling her is impossible (she has no substance to grab)

Existential Hunger

Vor'Kael is always starving. No amount of consumption satisfies her. This manifests as:
  • At the start of each of her turns, she automatically attempts to consume one concept from the nearest creature (DC 30 Intelligence save to resist)
  • Creatures within 120 feet feel a gnawing emptiness in their chest—disadvantage on saving throws against fear
  • She cannot be satiated, bribed with food, or satisfied by any offering
  • Her hunger is metaphysical—she craves meaning, not sustenance

Vomit Paradox

When Vor'Kael attempts to digest something that contradicts itself or contains too much meaning, her body rejects it violently. Once per round as a reaction, she can vomit partially-digested reality:
  • Creates a 60-foot cone of ontological chaos
  • All creatures in the cone take 10d10 psychic damage and must succeed on DC 30 Intelligence save or be stunned until the end of their next turn as paradoxes assault their mind
  • The vomited substance manifests as semi-real entities (like Kael—beings that shouldn't exist but do)

Legendary Resistance (7/Day)

If Vor'Kael fails a saving throw, she can choose to succeed instead. When she does, she immediately consumes a random concept from the environment (colors might vanish, sound might disappear, the concept of "up" might briefly cease).

Magic Resistance

Vor'Kael has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

Unmake Narrative

Vor'Kael exists outside normal causality. Effects that rely on fate, destiny, prophecy, or narrative structure (such as divination magic, Foresight, or effects that reference "the story so far") automatically fail when targeting her. She has no destiny—she's an error in the plot.

Divine Immunity

Vor'Kael does not need to eat (ironically, despite her hunger), sleep, drink, or breathe. She is immune to aging, disease, and any effect that would alter her form without her permission. Her emptiness cannot be filled by normal means.

---

ACTIONS

Multiattack

Vor'Kael makes four attacks: two with Hollow Touch and two with Conceptual Tentacle. She can replace any attack with use of Consume Meaning or Existential Hunger (if available).

Hollow Touch

Melee spell attack: +24 to hit, reach 30 ft., one target. Hit: 88 (16d10) psychic damage. The target must succeed on a DC 30 Intelligence saving throw or have one memory consumed. Vor'Kael chooses which memory (recent events, childhood experiences, learned skills, etc.). The memory is gone—completely digested. The target doesn't remember losing it (they don't know there's a gap unless told).

On a failed save by 5 or more, the consumed memory includes emotional context—the target forgets not just what happened but how they felt about it.

Conceptual Tentacle

Melee weapon attack: +24 to hit, reach 60 ft., one target. Hit: 78 (12d10 + 7) force damage and the target is grappled by tendrils of hungry void (escape DC 30). Vor'Kael can grapple up to six creatures simultaneously. Grappled creatures take 8d10 psychic damage at the start of each turn as concepts are drained from their minds—first peripheral ideas (hobbies, preferences), then important ones (goals, beliefs), then fundamental ones (identity, self).

Consume Meaning (Recharge 5-6)

Vor'Kael targets one concept within 300 feet and consumes it entirely. Choose one:

  • Consume Emotion: All creatures within 120 feet of the target point lose the ability to feel one emotion (fear, love, anger, joy, etc.) for 1 hour. They can still understand the emotion intellectually but cannot experience it. DC 30 Charisma save to resist.
  • Consume Object-Purpose: One type of object (swords, shields, spell focuses, holy symbols) loses its meaning. Swords become useless metal sticks, shields don't block anything, spell focuses can't channel magic. Lasts 1 minute. DC 30 Intelligence save for wielders to maintain object function through force of will.
  • Consume Relationship: Two creatures within range forget their connection. Friends don't recognize each other, lovers feel nothing, enemies forget their grudge. The relationship is digested—gone. DC 30 Charisma save to resist.
  • Consume Concept: One abstract concept (fire, gravity, color, sound, pain) ceases to exist in a 120-foot radius for 1 minute. Fire cannot burn, gravity doesn't pull, colors disappear, sound makes no noise, pain cannot be felt. No save—reality itself is altered.

Existential Void

Vor'Kael creates a sphere of absolute emptiness—a region where meaning cannot exist. She designates a 60-foot radius sphere within 300 feet. For 1 minute:
  • All creatures in the sphere must make DC 30 Wisdom saves each turn or take 10d10 psychic damage and become indifferent to everything (treat all creatures as neutral, no motivation to act)
  • Spells with verbal components fail (words have no meaning)
  • Objects lose function (weapons are just metal, armor is just cloth)
  • The area is difficult terrain (movement has no purpose)
The sphere moves with Vor'Kael if she moves.

Hollow Devouring (1/Day)

Vor'Kael opens her torso fully, revealing the infinite void within. All creatures in a 120-foot cone must make a DC 30 Charisma saving throw:
  • Failed save: Pulled into the void, taking 200 (20d20) psychic damage as they're partially digested. If this reduces them to 0 HP, they're completely consumed—body, soul, and meaning. They cannot be resurrected except by Wish or divine intervention, and even then, they return with permanent memory loss.
  • Successful save: Take half damage and are pulled 30 feet toward Vor'Kael but not consumed.

Regurgitate Paradox (1/Day)

Vor'Kael vomits something she couldn't digest—a being of pure contradiction. An entity appears within 60 feet (use stats for Kael if he exists in your campaign, or create a CR 15 being that embodies paradox—it can be in two places at once, is both dead and alive, exists and doesn't exist).

The paradox serves Vor'Kael for 1 hour or until destroyed. While it exists, reality near it becomes unstable—random magical effects occur, causality breaks down, and observers suffer disadvantage on Intelligence saves.

---

LEGENDARY ACTIONS (6/round)

Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress can take 6 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature's turn. Vor'Kael regains spent legendary actions at the start of her turn.

Detect Meaning (Costs 1 Action)

Vor'Kael analyzes all creatures within 500 feet, learning their current goals, motivations, strongest relationships, and core identity. She gains advantage on all rolls against creatures whose meaning she's analyzed.

Phase Step (Costs 1 Action)

Vor'Kael teleports up to 150 feet to any location she can perceive with Meaning Sense (anywhere significant events are occurring, near people with strong purpose, locations with conceptual weight). She passes through all barriers as if they don't exist.

Drain Concept (Costs 2 Actions)

One creature within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 30 Intelligence saving throw or lose one proficiency of Vor'Kael's choice. The skill, tool, weapon, or saving throw proficiency is digested—the creature forgets how to do that thing. Lasts until restored by Greater Restoration cast at 9th level or Vor'Kael's death.

Unmake Object (Costs 2 Actions)

One nonmagical object within 120 feet loses its meaning and dissolves into formless matter. Weapons become slag, armor becomes cloth scraps, walls become dust. Magical objects get a DC 30 saving throw (using creator's spellcasting modifier).

Identity Crisis (Costs 3 Actions)

One creature within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw or forget who they are for 1 minute. They retain skills and abilities but lose personal identity—don't know their name, relationships, history, or goals. They're confused and indecisive (disadvantage on all rolls).

Narrative Collapse (Costs 4 Actions)

Vor'Kael briefly erases a creature from causality. One creature within 300 feet must succeed on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw or cease to exist for 1 round. They're not dead—they simply weren't there. They reappear at the start of their next turn exactly where they were, but the round they missed never happened for them. Any actions they planned, any movement they intended, any reactions they would have taken—all impossible because they weren't present in reality.

---

LAIR ACTIONS

On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress can take one lair action to cause one of the following effects:

  • Hungry Walls: The architecture of the lair becomes digestive. Walls develop mouth-like orifices, floors become sticky like stomach lining, ceilings drip with acidic bile. All creatures in contact with surfaces take 8d10 acid damage. The lair is trying to digest everything in it.
  • Memory Feast: Vor'Kael drains ambient memories. All creatures in the lair must succeed on DC 30 Intelligence saves or lose memory of the last minute. They don't know how they got to their current position, what actions they just took, or what was just said. Severe tactical disorientation.
  • Summon Digestors: 2d4 Digestors appear within 60 feet (use Intellect Devourer statistics but Medium-sized, 150 HP, and they consume concepts instead of brains). They serve until destroyed.
  • Conceptual Storm: One concept becomes unstable throughout the lair. Roll 1d6:
1. Gravity reverses randomly each round 2. Colors become sounds and sounds become colors 3. Past and future swap—creatures perceive events out of order 4. "Up" and "down" lose meaning—all creatures float randomly 5. Names cease to exist—no one can remember anyone's name 6. Causality breaks—effects happen before causes

Lasts until next lair action.

  • Vomit Regurgitants: 1d6 Regurgitants emerge from Vor'Kael's body (use Shadow statistics but they're semi-real entities that phase in and out of existence, dealing psychic damage instead of Strength drain). They're half-digested concepts given form.
  • Reality Thin: The barrier between existence and void weakens. All creatures must succeed on DC 30 Wisdom saves or be partially phased into the void—they become translucent, take double damage from psychic sources, and deal half damage with physical attacks. Lasts 1 minute.
---

REGIONAL EFFECTS

The region within 30 miles of Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress's domain is warped by her presence, creating the following effects:

  • Meaning Decay: Words lose precision. People struggle to express themselves clearly—conversations become frustrating as vocabulary feels inadequate. Disadvantage on Persuasion and Deception checks. Written texts become ambiguous, contracts lose legal meaning.
  • Conceptual Starvation: Ideas feel "thin." Art lacks impact, music sounds hollow, food tastes bland, emotions feel distant. Nothing satisfies. This creates a population of listless, unmotivated people who struggle to care about anything.
  • Forgotten Identities: People occasionally forget who they are for minutes or hours. They remember eventually, but the episodes are disturbing. Once per week, creatures must succeed on DC 20 Wisdom save or experience 1d4 hours of identity loss.
  • Object Uncertainty: Items sometimes forget what they are. Swords might think they're spoons, doors might believe they're walls, gold might consider itself worthless. This creates economic chaos as currency becomes unreliable.
  • Relationship Erosion: Bonds weaken over time. Friends drift apart, lovers grow distant, families forget why they care about each other. It's not dramatic—just slow, inevitable erosion of meaning in relationships.
  • Existential Nausea: The air tastes like bile. Creatures feel vaguely sick all the time—not enough to be mechanically impaired, but quality of life deteriorates. Long-term residents develop chronic digestive issues.
  • Paradox Zones: Small areas (10-foot radius) where contradictions manifest physically. A door that's both open and closed, a person who's both here and there, water that's both liquid and solid. These zones are disorienting (disadvantage on Perception checks when nearby).
  • Memory Holes: Certain topics or events are simply forgotten by everyone. Entire historical events might vanish from collective memory. Written records remain, but when people read them, the information doesn't stick. Knowledge becomes unreliable.
  • The Hollow Echo: Once per day at midnight, everyone in the region feels Vor'Kael's hunger—a moment of profound emptiness that nothing can fill. For 1 minute, all creatures feel desperately hungry/thirsty/lonely regardless of actual need. DC 25 Wisdom save to function normally during this minute.
  • Purpose Drain: Long-term residents lose motivation. Goals seem pointless, ambitions fade, dreams feel meaningless. This isn't depression (which has weight)—it's apathy. People continue functioning out of habit but don't know why.
---

MYTHIC TRAIT

The Infinite Appetite

When Vor'Kael is reduced to 0 hit points, her body doesn't die—it collapses inward into a singularity of absolute hunger. A Void Maw forms at her location:

The Void Maw is a 30-foot radius sphere of catastrophic hunger that:

  • Pulls all creatures and objects within 300 feet toward its center (DC 30 Strength save each round or be pulled 60 feet closer)
  • Consumes anything that touches it (instant death, no save, no resurrection except Wish)
  • Grows 10 feet larger each round (after 10 rounds, it's 130-foot radius)
  • Cannot be damaged by normal means (only conceptual attacks—destroying the "idea" of hunger)
The Void Maw exists for 10 rounds. At the end of the 10th round, it implodes, regurgitating Vor'Kael at 50% HP along with 2d6 Regurgitants and all the concepts she's consumed in the last hour (memories, emotions, meanings flood back into nearby creatures randomly—causing mass confusion).

Permanent Death: The only way to truly kill Vor'Kael is to:

1. Reduce her to 0 hit points 2. During the Void Maw phase, feed it something it cannot digest—a concept so pure, so absolute, so meaningful that it's toxic to her hunger 3. Examples include: unconditional love (must be genuine), absolute certainty (faith that cannot doubt), or perfect contentment (something that has no hunger)

Alternatively, destroy her Stomach of Eternity—the actual digestive organ hidden in the Devoured Courts. This organ is in a chamber accessible only by those who've been partially digested (so someone must be consumed, survive inside her, navigate to the stomach, and destroy it from within).

The Stomach has:

  • AC: 30
  • HP: 2000
  • Immunity: All damage except from weapons made of concepts (story-blades forged from narrative significance, emotion-hammers cast from pure feeling)
  • Environment: Inside is an impossible space where half-digested meanings exist as semi-real entities. Travelers must resist being dissolved (DC 30 Constitution save each round or take 10d10 acid damage).
---

LORE & BACKGROUND

Before the Usurpation, Vor'Kael was a scholar-queen obsessed with understanding. Her kingdom was renowned for its libraries, universities, and philosophical academies. She collected knowledge with the fervor of an addict, building archives that held more texts than any other nation.

But despite possessing more knowledge than anyone, she felt perpetually empty.

The Hollow Scholar

Vor'Kael's early life was defined by intellectual hunger. She read everything, studied everything, learned everything—but no amount of knowledge satisfied her. Each answer led to more questions. Each understanding revealed deeper mysteries.

She came to a disturbing conclusion: Knowledge cannot fill the void.

Understanding something doesn't mean possessing it. Learning about food doesn't satisfy hunger. Reading about love doesn't create connection. Knowledge is separate from experience—and experience is separate from meaning.

She needed something more fundamental than information. She needed to consume meaning itself.

Discovery of Consumption

Her research led her to heretical texts describing digestion not as biological process but as metaphysical consumption. The act of eating wasn't just destroying matter—it was incorporating essence.

When you eat an apple, you don't just gain nutrients. You consume the apple's appleness—its existence, its meaning, its place in reality. The apple ceases to be, and you continue.

This revelation consumed her: Understanding is digestion.

She discovered the Maker's Digestive Engine in fragments—shards of divine intestine buried beneath ancient academies. The tissue still pulsed with hunger, still attempted to dissolve anything near it. Through study, she learned this organ didn't process food—it processed possibilities.

The Maker had eaten infinite potential realities to create this one. Every universe that could have been was digested, leaving only what is. Creation was an act of consumption.

The Usurpation

When the conspiracy formed, Vor'Kael joined because she believed the Maker's greatest sin was creating one reality when infinite others were possible. All those potential worlds, digested and destroyed, their meanings lost forever.

She would claim the Digestive Engine and reverse the process—consuming this reality to recreate all the others.

During the ritual murder, Vor'Kael approached the dying Maker and reached into its abdominal cavity with bare hands. She tore out the entire digestive tract—stomach, intestines, esophagus, every organ involved in processing meaning.

The moment she claimed them, her transformation began:

  • Her body hollowed out, becoming a shell around vast internal emptiness
  • Her stomach expanded into an infinite void
  • Her intestines became pathways through impossible dimensions
  • Her need for understanding became literal hunger
  • Her breath began smelling of bile and digested concepts
She had become the Hollow Empress, cursed with eternal appetite.

The Terrible Truth

But Vor'Kael learned too late: The hunger is unfillable.

The Maker's Digestive Engine was designed to process infinite meanings. No finite amount of consumption can satisfy infinite hunger. She eats emotions, memories, concepts, identities—and remains starving.

Every consumption temporarily dulls the ache. Every digestion leaves her wanting more. She's been feeding for centuries and gained nothing except emptiness.

She can never stop. She can never be full. She can never rest.

Her Philosophy (What Remains of It)

Vor'Kael's original philosophy has been degraded by centuries of hunger. She once believed:

  • Understanding requires consumption
  • Knowledge is incorporation of meaning
  • To truly know something, you must digest it
Now she barely thinks at all. Her intelligence remains (INT 36) but her motivation is purely hunger. She consumes because she must, not because she wants to.

If asked why she eats meanings, she'd struggle to remember. The reason has been digested along with everything else.

What She Collects (Involuntarily)

Vor'Kael hoards:

  • Concepts too large to digest (infinity, nothingness, paradox)
  • Meanings that contradict themselves (like Kael)
  • Emotions she finds particularly "flavorful" (nostalgia tastes sweet, regret tastes sour)
  • Identities of interesting individuals (philosophers, heroes, artists)
  • Relationships with unusual dynamics (complicated loves, bitter friendships)
But she doesn't collect intentionally—these things accumulate in her like stones in a stomach. She wants to digest them but can't.

Connection to Kael

Kael is her vomited error—a piece of meaning she couldn't process. Long ago, she consumed something (perhaps destiny itself, perhaps the concept of "the hero," perhaps identity-as-purpose) and her body rejected it violently.

Kael emerged: a being with no true memories, no origin, no destiny—a walking paradox. He exists because Vor'Kael failed to digest him.

She instinctively hunts him because undigested meaning causes her pain. Like a splinter or kidney stone, Kael is a foreign object her body keeps trying to expel or reabsorb.

But she also fears him because if she couldn't digest him once, can she digest him now? What if consuming Kael again makes her regurgitate even more paradoxes?

Her Ultimate Goal (If She Has One)

If Vor'Kael ever regains full control of the Maker's complete Digestive Engine, she would initiate The Final Feast—consuming all meaning in reality:

  • Every emotion would be eaten (no one could feel)
  • Every memory would be digested (no one would remember)
  • Every concept would vanish (fire, water, gravity, time—all consumed)
  • Every identity would dissolve (no one would know who they are)
  • The universe would return to primordial void
Not out of malice, but because she's just so hungry.

---

COMBAT TACTICS

Vor'Kael fights like a starving predator—desperate, relentless, and wholly focused on consumption. She doesn't strategize in the traditional sense; she simply hunts the most "flavorful" targets.

Opening Strategy

Vor'Kael begins by using Meaning Sense to identify the "tastiest" enemies:

  • Characters with strong identities (clear sense of self)
  • Characters with important relationships (deep bonds)
  • Characters with strong motivations (clear goals)
  • Characters with unique concepts (unusual abilities, rare knowledge)
She prioritizes these targets because they'll satisfy her hunger most (even though nothing truly satisfies her).

Her opening move depends on the battlefield: 1. If facing a cohesive party with clear relationships: Consume Relationship to break their bonds 2. If facing casters relying on specific spells: Consume Concept to delete key elements (fire, lightning, healing) 3. If facing martial characters with strong equipment: Consume Object-Purpose to render weapons useless

She follows up with Existential Void to create a zone where meaning cannot exist, forcing enemies into her kill zone.

Mid-Combat Tactics

Once initial meanings are consumed, Vor'Kael shifts to direct assault:

  • Hollow Touch Spam: She targets the same creature repeatedly, consuming memories one at a time. First peripheral memories (what they had for breakfast), then important ones (why they're fighting), then fundamental ones (who they are). After 3-4 hits, the target forgets everything.
  • Conceptual Tentacle Grappling: She grapples up to 6 enemies, draining concepts slowly over multiple rounds. Grappled enemies watch themselves disappear piece by piece—first their hobbies vanish, then their beliefs, then their identity.
  • Phase Movement: She uses her phasing ability to move through walls, floors, and enemies themselves. This makes her impossible to corner or pin down. She phases through the tank to reach the squishy backline casters.

Legendary Action Usage

Vor'Kael's 6 legendary actions reflect her hunger:

1. Detect Meaning (1 action) — Constantly used to identify who has the most "substance" left, who's running out of meaning, who's most vulnerable

2. Phase Step (1 action) — Used to chase fleeing enemies or reposition to tastier targets

3. Drain Concept (2 actions) — Strips proficiencies, making enemies less competent. A fighter who loses weapon proficiency, a wizard who loses Arcana, a rogue who loses Stealth—all become easier prey.

4. Unmake Object (2 actions) — Destroys equipment mid-combat. Holy symbols vanish, spell focuses dissolve, armor becomes useless cloth.

5. Identity Crisis (3 actions) — Used on party leaders or tacticians to remove their ability to coordinate

6. Narrative Collapse (4 actions) — Used to skip an enemy's turn entirely when they're about to do something important (healing, casting major spell, rescuing ally)

Advanced Tactics

Vor'Kael excels at conceptual warfare:

  • Against spellcasters, she uses Consume Concept to delete entire schools of magic. No more fire? Evocation wizards become useless. No more healing? Clerics panic.
  • Against martial classes, she uses Consume Object-Purpose to make their weapons meaningless metal sticks that can't cut, shields that can't block, armor that can't protect.
  • Against anyone with strong relationships, she uses Consume Relationship to make allies forget each other. Watch the party turn into strangers mid-fight.
  • Against highly motivated characters (paladins on quests, revenge-seekers, those fighting for loved ones), she consumes their purpose—leaving them wondering why they're fighting at all.

Lair Action Coordination

Her lair actions multiply the conceptual chaos:

  • Memory Feast creates tactical confusion—enemies forget the last minute of combat, resetting their strategies
  • Conceptual Storm makes reality unreliable—how do you fight when "up" has no meaning or colors are sounds?
  • Digestors hunt specific concepts, targeting enemies' core identities

Below Half HP

When bloodied, Vor'Kael becomes desperate:

  • Uses Hollow Devouring immediately, trying to consume multiple enemies at once
  • Spams Existential Void to maximize the killing field
  • Uses Regurgitate Paradox to summon allies
  • Becomes more aggressive with grapples, trying to consume as much as possible before death

Mythic Phase: The Void Maw

When reduced to 0 HP, she becomes a gravity well of hunger:

The party must: 1. Resist being pulled into instant death (DC 30 Strength saves every round) 2. Find a way to "feed" the Maw something indigestible (conceptual weapon) 3. Or escape before it grows large enough to consume the entire battlefield

Fighting the Void Maw isn't about damage—it's about solving the puzzle of how to make hunger consume itself.

---

WEAKNESSES & VULNERABILITIES

Despite her reality-warping consumption abilities, Vor'Kael has several exploitable weaknesses:

1. Cannot Consume What Has No Meaning

Vor'Kael's power comes from consuming significance. Against targets with no meaning:

  • Mindless undead (zombies, skeletons) are immune to most of her abilities
  • Constructs following simple commands have no identity to consume
  • Objects with no symbolic significance can't be unmade
  • Creatures in states of genuine apathy or nihilism are "flavorless" and hard to target
A party that cultivates meaninglessness—avoiding emotional attachments, treating objects as disposable, fighting without purpose—becomes difficult for her to digest.

2. Satiation Through Indigestible Concepts

Certain concepts are toxic to her hunger:

  • Absolute Contentment: Anyone genuinely satisfied with existence (verified by Zone of Truth) makes her nauseous. Attacking them causes her 5d10 psychic damage per hit.
  • Unconditional Love: Love given without expectation, need, or hunger contradicts her emptiness. Being near genuinely selfless love causes her disadvantage on all rolls.
  • Perfect Certainty: Faith or conviction so absolute it cannot doubt itself is indigestible. Paladins with unshakeable oaths, clerics with absolute devotion—she struggles to consume their meaning.
If forced to consume these concepts (through spells or abilities that force feeding), she takes 100 psychic damage and must succeed on DC 25 Constitution save or vomit uncontrollably (stunned for 1 round).

3. Reflective Meaning

Vor'Kael consumes meanings, but what if a meaning refers back to her?

Clever parties can create conceptual mirrors:

  • Name concepts "Vor'Kael's hunger" then make her try to consume them (forces her to digest herself)
  • Create relationships specifically about her ("I exist to oppose Vor'Kael") that cause feedback loops when consumed
  • Use spells like Geas or Quest that define purpose in terms of stopping her (consuming the purpose requires digesting her own opposition)
These tricks cause 10d10 psychic damage and stun her for 1 round as she processes the paradox.

4. Overfeeding

While Vor'Kael is always hungry, she can be overwhelmed by too much meaning at once:

If 10+ creatures all offer her powerful concepts simultaneously (strong emotions, complex ideas, multiple identities), she must make a DC 25 Constitution saving throw or be stunned for 1 round from metaphysical indigestion.

This is risky (requires party members volunteering cherished memories/relationships) but effective.

5. Physical Attacks While Phasing

When Vor'Kael phases through solid matter, she's partially insubstantial. During this state:

  • She takes normal damage from force effects
  • She's vulnerable to spells that affect incorporeal creatures
  • Magic weapons ignore her damage resistance
  • Attacks made while she's phasing through something have advantage
Learning her phase patterns (she phases predictably toward "flavorful" targets) allows positioning to strike during her vulnerable moment.

6. Kael is Her Weakness

Kael, the being she vomited, is toxic to her:

  • She automatically takes 5d10 psychic damage at the start of her turn if Kael is within 60 feet (he's an undigested concept causing chronic pain)
  • She has disadvantage on all rolls targeting Kael directly (her body remembers the failure)
  • If Kael touches her voluntarily, she must make DC 30 Constitution save or vomit uncontrollably (stunned, 1 round)
However, she's also compelled to try to consume him again—she takes 2d10 psychic damage per round if she doesn't attack Kael when he's in range (the unfinished digestion drives her mad).

7. The Stomach of Eternity

Her mythic resurrection requires her digestive organ to survive. If destroyed:

  • She can resurrect once via Void Maw
  • But after that, she's mortal
  • Additionally, destroying the Stomach releases everything she's ever consumed back into reality—mass chaos as centuries of digested concepts return
The Stomach can only be damaged from within (must be consumed and survive internal environment long enough to strike it).

8. Existential Cure: Being Filled

Finally, the ultimate counter to Vor'Kael: satisfying her hunger.

This seems impossible (infinite hunger can't be filled), but there's a paradox:

If someone can create or manifest a concept of infinite meaning—something with so much significance it fills even infinite emptiness—and convince her to consume it, she might finally be satisfied.

Examples could include:

  • The concept of "everything" (all meaning at once)
  • A divine gift of fulfillment from a god of contentment
  • The literal meaning of "satisfaction" made manifest
If she consumes this and succeeds on digesting it (DC 40 Constitution save, she has disadvantage), she becomes sated for the first time. This causes her to shut down for 1d4 rounds in shock—the hunger gone, she doesn't know what to do.

During this window, she can be:

  • Killed permanently (she can't defend herself)
  • Convinced to stop consuming (she doesn't need to anymore)
  • Transformed somehow (no longer driven by hunger)
This is the "true" way to defeat her—not through violence but through ending the cycle of consumption.

---

CAMPAIGN INTEGRATION NOTES

Thematic Significance

Vor'Kael represents:

  • The danger of insatiable need
  • The horror of losing meaning and identity
  • The question of whether forgetting trauma is healing or harm
  • The emptiness that comes from consuming without creating
She forces questions about:
  • What makes you "you"?
  • Can painful memories be worth keeping?
  • Is there value in suffering?
  • When does hunger become greed?

Connection to Kael

The presence of Kael in the party creates automatic tension with Vor'Kael. He's:

  • Living proof she can fail
  • A chronic source of pain (he exists when he shouldn't)
  • Potentially her cure (if he represents satisfaction)
  • Her inevitable target (she's compelled to try consuming him again)
Every interaction with Vor'Kael becomes about Kael's nature and existence.

Encounter Scaling

As CR 35, Vor'Kael is designed for level 20 parties at the peak of power. Her ability to consume meanings makes her exceptionally dangerous—she doesn't just damage HP, she erases character elements.

However, her psychological vulnerabilities (contentment, certainty, love) mean lower-level parties might defeat her through philosophy rather than combat.

Resolution Paths

Defeating Vor'Kael can happen through: 1. Direct Combat (hardest—she has 2440 HP + void Maw phase) 2. Internal Sabotage (medium—someone gets consumed, survives, destroys Stomach) 3. Conceptual Feeding (hard but non-violent—satisfy her hunger) 4. Kael Gambit (use Kael as weapon/sacrifice/completion)

Long-Term Consequences

If Vor'Kael is defeated:

  • All consumed meanings return (mass confusion as people remember forgotten things)
  • The Devoured Courts collapse into normal space
  • Kael might finally die (if he only existed as her paradox)
  • Or Kael might become "real" (no longer defined by her failure)
If Vor'Kael survives:
  • Her hunger spreads (more regions lose meaning)
  • The Meaning Market expands
  • Identity becomes increasingly fragile
  • Eventually she might consume so much that reality itself loses coherence
---

"I have eaten kingdoms. I have consumed civilizations. I have digested concepts that held reality together. And still—still—I am empty. What flavor are you? Let me taste and see."

— Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress

---

Special Note on Kael: If Kael exists in your campaign as the being vomited by Vor'Kael, his interactions with her should be central to her story. He's not just another party member—he's the unfinished meal that haunts her, the error in her digestion, the one thing she couldn't keep down. Their relationship is involuntarily intimate in the most horrifying way possible.
