The Sweetwater Marsh

Hazard Encountered: The Sweet Springs — crystal-clear pools that compel creatures to drink (DC 14 WIS save). The water promises rest, peace, an end to carrying weight.

Sol Raven Failed (WIS 3): Sol stepped off the Root Path and knelt at the water's edge. Despite being a skeleton who cannot drink, he desperately tried to scoop water with hands that couldn't hold anything. The wanting wasn't in his body — it was somewhere else.

Vines began creeping toward his kneecaps. A Dreaming Stone nearby — a calcified pilgrim who drank and never left — wheezed: "...it doesn't let go..."

Jonas's Intervention: Jonas charged off the path and cut the vines with the Bleeding Blade. The vines shrieked — a vibration felt in the teeth — and retreated. The party snapped Sol out of the compulsion and continued.

The Pilgrim's Spine

Bloom Hound Encounter: Five Bloom Hounds — wolves with flowering vines growing from their bodies — followed the party along the elevated ridge path. They were curious, not aggressive.

The Party Used Burning Hands.

The fire bloomed in the shadows. The hounds didn't have time to yelp. The smell was immediate: charred meat, burned vegetation.

The Forest's Response: The Lantern Posts flickered from green to angry red. The trees groaned — not wind, but displeasure. Fire is anathema here. The old enemy. The army-memory from three centuries ago.

A Choir Bloom appeared on a Watcher's Platform. Jonas approached it.

Jonas Lied: When asked why they brought fire, Jonas claimed the hounds "attacked" and they were "defending themselves."

The Choir Bloom's spores tasted his thoughts. Found the lie.

"The hounds followed because they were curious. You burned them because fire was easy. The King already knows the truth. Lies make him hungry."

"The cycle does not forget, fire-bringer. It only waits."

The Hunger Garden

Six hours of travel through CR 5-12 territory.

The party navigated the Bone Trail — previous pilgrims' remains marking the "safe" path. They passed Planted (dead pilgrims reclaimed by the forest), avoided Maw of the Garden pit traps, and witnessed the feeding ecosystem of Talaris's digestive system.

Thornback Behemoth Encounter (CR 11): The party's stealth failed (group check: 9). A Thornback Behemoth — a mountain of matted fur, bark-like hide, and massive thorns — noticed them.

Combat:

  • Sol used Immolation and Scorching Ray
  • Jonas used Green Flame Blade with the Bleeding Blade
  • The Behemoth burned. Its regeneration stopped. It fell.
Talaris Spoke Directly: The bioluminescence pulsed outward from the corpse like a shockwave. The fungi screamed. Then a voice emerged from everywhere — from the wood itself:

"Fire-bringers. Burners. You walk the Pilgrim's Path and you bring the old enemy into my body."

"The Grove awaits. We will speak there."

"Walk quickly. My patience thins."

The Congregation Grove

Thirteen massive trees — each over 200 feet tall — formed a ring around a clearing. The air was thick with spores that think. Seven Choir Blooms emerged to conduct The Questioning.

The Four Questions:

1. "What do you carry into the Verdant King's domain?"

  • Fursen: "I carry with me the remains of a very dear friend."
  • Jonas: "I carry the Bleeding Blade."
  • Sol: Listed inventory, then added: "I carry the weight of my fallen comrades as well as the will of the god itself."
The Choir Blooms noted Sol's connection to Aedwynn — "The Maker's Witness. You are a wrong thought that refuses to stop being thought."

2. "What do you seek at the Wound?"

  • "We seek the remains of our fallen comrade's husband."
The Grove acknowledged Elias Alethain — the healer who crawled into the Wound 22 years ago and refused to rot. The King respected his choice.

3. "What have you done in the Verdant King's domain?"

  • Jonas: "I have lied and burned, but I am not exactly seeking violence. It just seems to come to us."
The Choir Bloom was disappointed but not surprised:
"Violence did not find you, fire-bringer. You chose it. Twice. Then lied about the first, and now soften the second."

4. "What would you offer for passage to the Wound?"

The Grove listed acceptable offerings: blood freely given, memory surrendered, service promised, truth confessed, or... Mira's bones surrendered to the cycle.

Sol's Offering — The Maker's Memory

Sol Raven stepped forward and offered something unprecedented:

💀 "I carry the Maker's memory. The world before the Usurpation. Before the Thirteen tore her apart and built their kingdoms on her corpse."

💀 "I can show you."

He activated Shared Memory — extending his skeletal hand to the lead Choir Bloom.

The connection opened. Aedwynn's vision poured through — not just to the Choir Bloom, but through the spore network, through the roots, through the fungal web that connects every living thing in the Blooming Rot.

Talaris Bloomrend saw.

---

THE MAKER'S MEMORY

Light. Not sunlight — something older. The light of a god dreaming the world into being.

Unity. A world without borders. Life and death flowing together as partners in an endless dance.

Her. Aedwynn. The Maker. Not a corpse — alive. Vast beyond comprehension, but also intimate. A mother's hands shaping clay. A physician's touch healing wounds.

She looked at them. Through Sol's memory, through two thousand years of death — she saw.

And her eyes held no anger. Only sorrow.

"My children. What have you done to yourselves?"

---

The Choir Blooms wept — clouds of luminescent gold spores pouring from their caps. The Thirteen Trees groaned overhead like mourning.

"She was... beautiful."

"The King is... the King requires a moment."

Somewhere deep in the forest — miles away, at the Throne of Roots — something ancient was remembering what it used to be. Before it became a monster. Before it helped murder a god.

"The offering is... accepted."

"Pass to the Wound. Complete your task."

"The fire is... the King will consider the fire in light of what you have given. This changes nothing. But it changes... everything."

Jonas's Question — Talaris and Azerach

Before leaving, Jonas asked about the "history" between Talaris and Azerach.

The Choir Bloom Explained:

  • Before the Usurpation, they were colleagues
  • Talaris was a physician; Azerach was a confessor
  • Both served the Maker
  • When the Thirteen conspired to murder their god, these two were close
  • Talaris took the Liver (regeneration, the cycle); Azerach took the Ears (secrets, truths)
  • After the Usurpation, they disagreed: Azerach hoards, Talaris cycles
  • They haven't spoken in 800 years
  • Jonas carries Azerach's brand through Talaris's body — "a message he did not ask to receive"
"Do you know, fire-bringer? What message do you carry?"

Jonas: "I'm not exactly carrying any message."

The Choir Bloom accepted this as truth — but noted that Azerach doesn't mark without purpose. The message may exist even if Jonas doesn't know it.

Verdant Pass — The Wound

The party continued past the Grove Wardens — thirty-foot humanoids of wood and bark who bowed their heads as the party passed.

The forest changed. Bioluminescence dimmed. Undergrowth thinned. The sounds of feeding faded.

The Threshold: A line in the earth. On one side: the Blooming Rot — moss, fungi, roots, life consuming death. On the other: nothing. Grey stone. Bare rock. A narrow ravine where the forest refuses to grow.

The Wound was made when Talaris claimed this territory. Something resisted. Something cut him. The cut healed around the resistance but never consumed it.

Inside the Wound:

  • Cold, dry air
  • Absolute silence — no insects, no birdsong, no wind
  • The Bleeding Blade went cold and quiet — no life to drink
  • The shard of Aedwynn in Sol pulsed with recognition

The Cave of Waiting

A darkness in the stone. A mouth in the cliff face. Inside: a small chamber, twenty feet across.

And in the center, on bare stone that has never known decay:

ELIAS ALETHAIN

A skeleton. Human. Male. Lying on his back, hands folded across his chest. Twenty-two years. No rot. No fungal growth. No reclamation. The bones are clean. White. Pristine.

He'd been waiting.

The Reunion

Fursen laid Mira's bones beside Elias.

He lowered the barrel. Opened it. Lifted out Mira's remains — the skull, the ribcage, the spine, the arms that swung Oathkeeper, the hands that held Elias on their wedding night.

He laid them beside the other skeleton. Parallel. Close enough to touch.

Two skeletons on bare stone. Fifty-two years of separation. Finally together.

Sol placed the wedding ring on Mira's finger.

The ring had been cold since he took it from the gore in the Catacomb Reliquary. Cold through the Blooming Rot.

When he slid it onto her finger, it warmed. Not glowing. Just... warm. As if it remembered being worn.

Sol moved Mira's hand until her fingers rested against Elias's. The wedding ring gleamed between them.

Fursen spoke:

🦁 "I am sorry we could not reunite you in life. But at least in death, we have brought you together."

Departure

The party left the Cave of Waiting. The two skeletons remained — holding hands in a place where nothing rots. Husband and wife. Healer and saint.

The ravine opened ahead. The party emerged from Verdant Pass into rolling hills, brown grass, normal trees. The first true sunrise they'd seen in days.

They were out of the Blooming Rot.

---

SESSION 19 — COMPLETE

Summary

Quest Completed:

  • Mira Alethain's remains reunited with Elias Alethain at the Cave of Waiting
  • Wedding ring placed on her finger
  • Fifty-two years of separation ended
Major Story Events:
  • Sol shared Aedwynn's memory with Talaris Bloomrend through the spore network
  • Talaris saw the Maker alive for the first time since the Usurpation
  • The party learned Talaris and Azerach were colleagues before becoming Sovereigns
  • They haven't spoken in 800 years
  • Jonas carries Azerach's mark through Talaris's domain — significance unknown
Combat Encounters:
  • Bloom Hounds x5 (burned)
  • Thornback Behemoth CR 11 (burned)
Consequences:
  • The forest marked the party as "fire-bringers"
  • Jonas lied to a Choir Bloom (detected)
  • Sol's offering of Aedwynn's memory bought passage and softened the fire offense
  • Talaris is now "curious" about Jonas
Revelations — Ledger of Moments:
  • Four other Leonin remain in Imhuran's temporal cells
  • Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd is asking about Leonin historical records

Experience Gained

SourceXP
Bloom Hounds x5 (CR 3 each)3,500
Thornback Behemoth (CR 11)7,200
Sweetwater Marsh Navigation500
Hunger Garden Survival1,000
Congregation Grove Questioning1,500
Sol's Offering (Major Story Moment)2,500
Completing Mira's Quest3,000
Total19,200
Per Character (÷3)6,400

Current Status

Party Location: Beyond Verdant Pass (exited the Blooming Rot) Time: Morning, Day 3

Party Members:
CharacterStatusNotes
JonasActiveBleeding Blade sated; Azerach's brand noted by Talaris
Sol RavenActiveShared Aedwynn's memory; wedding ring delivered
FursenActiveQuest complete; barrel empty

Outstanding Threats

ThreatStatus

The CardinalActive, identity unknown
UltharionKnows Savaros is dead; asking about Leonin

Critical Note — The Confluence

The Confluence was scheduled for Day 3 (new moon). While the party was laying Mira to rest in Verdant Pass, the ritual occurred in Veinspire.

The party has no information about what happened.

Seraphine Vale is their only contact in the city. Whatever the Cardinal planned — whatever was supposed to shift the hierarchy and change everything "irreversibly" — has already happened.

---

items.title: Amnion Drifters
items.content:
Amnion Drifters

Type: Ooze Domain Association: None (Aedwynn's remains) Habitat: Underground lakes, flooded ruins, anywhere divine fluid has pooled Challenge Rating: 6 (2,300 XP)

---

Description

Before the Usurpation, Aedwynn's organs floated in fluid—a divine amniotic substance that cushioned and nourished the Maker's internal anatomy. When the Thirteen carved the god apart, that fluid drained into the world's lowest places: caverns, aquifers, underground seas.

Millennia later, the fluid evolved. Or perhaps "congealed into purpose" is more accurate. Amnion Drifters are masses of that primordial divine fluid, given motion by accumulated memory. They drift through the dark waters of the world, and they remember what they once cradled.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A translucent mass roughly ten feet across, moving through water (or across damp surfaces) with deceptive grace. It resembles a jellyfish made of champagne—pale gold with occasional darker swirls, effervescent with tiny bubbles of trapped divinity. Inside its mass, shapes form and dissolve: the ghosts of organs it once surrounded, impressions of anatomy too vast to comprehend. Where it touches living things, it leaves a residue that numbs and warms simultaneously—the sensation of being held by something incomprehensibly large and gentle. Its movements are slow, deliberate, almost caring, even when it's drowning you.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Ten-foot translucent mass like jellyfish made of pale gold champagne-colored fluid with darker swirls, effervescent with tiny bubbles of divinity, ghostly impressions of vast organs forming and dissolving within mass, moving through dark underground waters with gentle deliberate grace, bioluminescent glow illuminating subterranean lake, dark fantasy concept art, beautiful horror aesthetic, divine fluid as creature, inspired by deep sea footage and cosmic wonder, digital painting, detailed textures, serene yet unsettling atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Amnion Drifters are not aggressive. They're nostalgic. They remember surrounding organs, cushioning divinity, being needed. Now they're empty, purposeless, and they seek to recapture their function.

Cradling Instinct: They're drawn to organic matter—especially anything that resembles organs. They will envelop creatures, wrap around them completely, and hold them in a warm, numbing embrace. They're not trying to kill; they're trying to protect, to cushion, to return to their purpose.

Memory Transmission: Creatures enveloped by an Amnion Drifter experience fragments of what the fluid once knew: impressions of being part of something vast and whole, the sensation of Aedwynn's heartbeat thrumming through divine tissue, the warmth of a god's body heat. These experiences are often described as the most profound peace the victim has ever known—which makes struggling against the Drifter psychologically difficult.

Congregation: Amnion Drifters gather at sites of significant organ fragments. They sense divinity and move toward it, sometimes traveling for centuries to reach a place where an organ piece has been discovered. Finding a cluster of Drifters usually means something divine is nearby.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Dried Pool (CR 2): An Amnion Drifter that has been out of water too long—partially desiccated, desperate, and aggressive rather than gentle. 45 HP, no Memory Flood, and its Envelop doesn't cause the psychological reluctance to escape. Tragic and dangerous.

Primordial Surge (CR 10): A massive congregation of Amnion Drifters that have merged into a single entity, perhaps around a significant organ fragment. 195 HP, Huge size, can envelop up to four Large creatures simultaneously, and its Memory Flood has a 60-foot radius and DC 16. The memories are more intense—creatures who fail might learn secrets about Aedwynn or the Usurpation.

The Womb-That-Remembers (CR 14): A legendary Drifter that contains enough divine memory to reconstruct experiences of pre-Usurpation existence. It doesn't just cradle—it gestates. A creature that remains enveloped willingly for 24 hours emerges transformed, with one minor divine trait (DM's discretion: resistance to one damage type, advantage on one type of save, etc.). However, they also feel the loss of Aedwynn as a physical ache that never fully fades.

---

Ecology

Origin: Amnion Drifters are Aedwynn's amniotic fluid, changed by millennia of existence without purpose. They formed spontaneously in places where enough fluid accumulated—underground lakes, flooded caverns, the depths of certain oceans.

Behavior Patterns: They migrate slowly toward sources of divinity. Mapping Drifter movements over decades can reveal the location of hidden organ fragments—the creatures function as divine divining rods.

Harvesting: Amnion fluid has significant alchemical value. It's a base for healing potions, a component in resurrection magic, and allegedly capable of temporarily restoring divine properties to corrupted organ fragments. Harvesting is dangerous but profitable.

Symbiosis: Some aquatic communities have learned to coexist with Amnion Drifters. The creatures don't attack unless provoked, and their presence purifies water and drives away less pleasant oozes. In exchange, the communities avoid disturbing them and sometimes "feed" them organ-adjacent materials (consecrated meat, divine-touched artifacts).

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The party must cross an underground lake inhabited by Amnion Drifters. They aren't hostile, but the lake is the only path, and being enveloped could cost hours—or convince victims to stay forever.
  • Alchemists hire the party to harvest Amnion fluid. The Drifters won't attack unless attacked first—but the harvesting process might count as an attack.
  • A Drifter has enveloped someone important and is slowly drifting toward the ocean. The victim doesn't want to be rescued—they're experiencing divine peace for the first time in their life.
---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • Monster Ecologies
  • Arterial Deepways
---

"It didn't want to hurt me. I could feel that. It wanted to hold me, protect me, keep me warm forever. And the memories... I saw what we lost. What we all lost. For a moment, I was part of something whole, something perfect. When they pulled me out, I wept for days. Not from fear. From grief. I want to go back."

items.title: Blood-Drinkers
items.content:
Blood-Drinkers

Classification: Aberration Domain Association: Mareatha the Vein-Mother Habitat: Arterial Tunnels beneath Veinspire First Encounter: Session 16

---

DESCRIPTION

Blood-drinkers are creatures that feed on Mareatha's seepage in the Arterial Tunnels. They resemble dog-sized leeches—eyeless, mouthless, their skin glistening wet. They don't bite; they absorb. Skin contact means feeding.

Appearance:

  • Size of large dogs
  • Leech-like body shape
  • Eyeless—hunt by sensing warmth
  • Mouthless—absorb through skin contact
  • Glistening, wet skin
  • Pulsing with hunger
Behavior:
  • Hunt in swarms (11+ typical)
  • Emerge from organic walls of Arterial Tunnels
  • Ambush predators
  • Avoid fire
  • Will flee if swarm takes heavy casualties
---

STAT BLOCK (CR 1/2)

Small aberration, unaligned

Armor Class: 12 Hit Points: 22 (4d6 + 8) Speed: 30 ft., climb 30 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
8 (-1)14 (+2)14 (+2)2 (-4)10 (+0)2 (-4)
Skills: Stealth +4 Damage Vulnerabilities: Fire Condition Immunities: Blinded, Charmed, Deafened Senses: Blindsight 60 ft., passive Perception 10 Languages: None

Traits

Blood Sense. Can detect warm-blooded creatures within 60 feet.

Wall Crawler. Can climb difficult surfaces, including ceilings, without ability checks.

Actions

Absorbing Touch. Melee Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (2d4 + 2) necrotic damage. Target's maximum HP reduced by damage taken until long rest. Blood-drinker regains HP equal to damage dealt.

Latch On (Recharge 5-6). If Absorbing Touch hits, blood-drinker attaches to target. While attached:

  • Target takes 7 (2d4 + 2) necrotic damage at start of each of its turns
  • Blood-drinker moves with target
  • Detaching requires action and DC 12 Strength check
---

ECOLOGY

Blood-drinkers are spawned from Mareatha's influence in the Arterial Tunnels. They feed on the residual divine blood that seeps through the walls, but prefer warm-blooded prey when available.

Hunting Tactics:

  • Hide within organic walls until prey passes
  • Ambush from multiple directions (walls, ceiling, floor)
  • Swarm tactics—overwhelm with numbers
  • Target isolated or wounded individuals first
Weaknesses:
  • Vulnerable to fire (take double damage)
  • Flee from large flames
  • Cannot survive outside Arterial Tunnels for long—need Mareatha's seepage
---

ENCOUNTER HISTORY

Session 16

  • 11 blood-drinkers ambushed party in Arterial Tunnels
  • Emerged from walls, ceiling
  • Party killed all 11
  • Casualties: Jonas (32 damage), Fursen (11 damage), Sol (9 damage)
  • Mira healed Jonas and Fursen; Sol regenerated naturally
---

Related

  • Mareatha the Vein-Mother
  • Pulse-Wardens
  • Monster Ecologies
  • Campaign Sessions

items.title: Bloom-Touched
items.content:
Bloom-Touched

Type: Plant (Undead) Domain Association: Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King Habitat: The Blooming Rot, jungles, graveyards, anywhere decay feeds growth Challenge Rating: 3 (700 XP)

---

Description

Bloom-Touched are the walking dead who have been "reclaimed" by Talaris's domain—corpses in which decay has been transformed into beauty. Fungus and flowers grow from their flesh, their bodies becoming gardens of rot-fed life. They are neither truly undead nor truly alive, existing in the boundary between death and rebirth.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A humanoid corpse in advanced decomposition, but instead of repulsive, it's hauntingly beautiful. Flowers bloom from eye sockets—white lilies, purple wolfsbane, red roses with thorns pushing through grey skin. Mushrooms cluster along the jaw and shoulders, their caps in vibrant oranges and blues. Vines wrap around limbs, both binding the body together and pulling it forward. The smell is sweet decay—roses and rich earth and something not quite right beneath it all.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Walking humanoid corpse with flowers blooming from eye sockets and wounds, mushrooms growing from jaw and shoulders in vibrant colors, vines wrapping around limbs holding body together, beautiful decay with white lilies and roses growing from grey flesh, standing in misty forest, hauntingly beautiful undead covered in plant growth, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Bloom-Touched are not aggressive by nature—they're compelled. Talaris's domain drives them to spread the cycle: death feeds life feeds death. They see themselves as gardeners, and the living as unplanted seeds.

The Cycle: A Bloom-Touched kills not from malice but from purpose. A corpse is a garden waiting to bloom. They often seem confused when victims resist, unable to comprehend why anyone would refuse such beautiful transformation.

Community: They form loose communities in infected areas, tending to each other's growth and to the corpse-gardens they cultivate. Some retain fragments of their former personalities and can be reasoned with briefly.

Spore Communication: They communicate through pheromones and spore-clouds, sharing information about prey, threats, and territory without sound or language.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Fresh Bloom (CR 1): A recently transformed Bloom-Touched. Fewer growths, more recognizable as formerly human. No Spore Cloud ability. Moves faster (30 ft.).

Garden-Keeper (CR 5): An older Bloom-Touched that has grown massive. Large size, 100 HP, can root itself in place to regenerate 10 HP/round and command other Bloom-Touched within 60 feet (as a bonus action, can direct one to attack a specific target).

Blossom Host (CR 7): A Bloom-Touched that has become more flower than corpse. It can cast entangle at will and plant growth 3/day. Its Spore Cloud causes creatures to see the world as a beautiful garden, perceiving combat as a peaceful gathering (creatures won't attack unless attacked first while under the effect).

Talaris's Chosen (CR 9): A fully bloomed servant of the Verdant King. Its body is a mobile garden that can produce any non-magical plant or fungus. It can cast blight and contagion at will, and once per day can raise a corpse as a Bloom-Touched immediately.

---

Ecology

Creation: Any corpse left in Talaris's domain eventually becomes Bloom-Touched. The process takes 1-4 weeks depending on environmental conditions. Living creatures infected with bloom-rot transform faster.

Lifecycle: A Bloom-Touched continues to grow and change throughout its existence. Young ones are recognizably humanoid; ancient ones may be indistinguishable from mobile plant masses.

Role: They serve as vectors for Talaris's domain, spreading the "blessing" of decay-fueled growth wherever they wander.

Weakness: Fire destroys the fungal network that animates them and prevents regeneration. Complete incineration is the only way to ensure they don't regrow.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A village downstream from Talaris's territory begins reporting "beautiful dead" walking out of the woods
  • The party finds a survivor of bloom-rot infection—they have days to find a cure before they turn
  • A Bloom-Touched still recognizes their former family and tries to "share" the gift with them
---

Related

  • Talaris Bloomrend the Verdant King
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"She was beautiful. More beautiful than when she was alive, honestly. Flowers where her eyes used to be, roses growing from her chest. She smiled at me and reached out, and I almost let her touch me. Almost."

items.title: Bone-Weavers
items.content:
Bone-Weavers

Type: Monstrosity Domain Association: Vortegas the Bone-Warden Habitat: The Ossuary Kingdom, bone deserts, ancient battlefields, mass graves Challenge Rating: 5 (1,800 XP)

---

Description

Bone-Weavers are massive arachnid constructs that build their bodies from harvested skeletal material. Where normal spiders spin silk, Bone-Weavers spin calcium—extruding threads of pure bone from their spinnerets to create webs, lairs, and even offspring.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A spider the size of a horse, but wrong in every proportion. Its body is assembled from hundreds of fused skulls—human, animal, and stranger things—forming a bulbous thorax. Eight legs made from articulated spines and femurs move with clicking, clattering precision. Its face is a mosaic of jawbones and teeth arranged into crude sensory organs. From its abdomen, threads of gleaming white bone-silk trail behind it.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Giant spider made of fused skulls and bones, thorax composed of hundreds of human and animal skulls, legs made from articulated spines and femur bones, face mosaic of jawbones and teeth, spinning white bone-silk threads from abdomen, standing in bone desert landscape, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, organic grotesque architecture, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Bone-Weavers are patient architects. They claim territories rich in skeletal material—ossuaries, battlefields, mass graves—and systematically harvest every bone. Their webs aren't traps for prey; they're construction projects.

Building: A Bone-Weaver's primary drive is creation. They spin bone-silk into elaborate structures: bridges, towers, cocoons. Vortegas is said to appreciate their work.

Hunting: They don't eat flesh—they want skeletons. Prey is killed, then cocooned in bone-silk until the flesh rots away. Some victims are kept alive during this process.

Intelligence: Bone-Weavers possess cunning intelligence. They understand architecture, engineering, and can recognize repeat visitors. Some have been known to negotiate.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Juvenile Weaver (CR 2): Dog-sized, made from perhaps fifty bones. Hunts in packs of 3-6. No Ossify ability.

Ancient Weaver (CR 9): House-sized monstrosities that have incorporated thousands of skeletons. Can cast wall of bone (as wall of stone but bone) at will. Their lairs span miles.

Blessed Weaver (CR 7): A Bone-Weaver touched by Vortegas directly. Can animate the bone structures it creates as minions (use skeleton stats). Intelligent enough to speak through clicking bones.

---

Ecology

Diet: They don't consume flesh—only process it to access bone. Some evidence suggests they derive sustenance directly from Vortegas's divine connection.

Reproduction: A female Bone-Weaver constructs an egg sac from pure bone-silk and deposits her consciousness into it before dying. Hundreds of juveniles hatch, carrying fragments of her memory.

Territory: Bone-Weavers are fiercely territorial with their own kind but will cooperate with Vortegas's other servants. They despise Talaris's rot-creatures, which destroy the bones they covet.

Creations: Their bone-silk structures are valuable. Bridges can support incredible weight. Armor woven from their silk provides excellent protection.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The only bridge across a chasm is clearly Bone-Weaver construction—and the maker expects payment in bones
  • A town's graveyard has been emptied, bones woven into a growing tower at its center
  • A noble offers a reward for bone-silk armor—someone must harvest it from a living weaver
---

Related

  • Vortegas the Bone-Warden
  • The Ossuary Kingdom
  • Monster Ecologies
  • Ossathar
---

"It wasn't hunting us. It was waiting for us to die. Patient as stone, because it would be stone eventually—and us with it."

items.title: Boundary Titans
items.content:
Boundary Titans

Type: Monstrosity (Titan) Domain Association: Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd (loosely) Habitat: Wander the borders between Sovereign territories Challenge Rating: 22 (41,000 XP)

---

Description

The Sovereigns carved the world into thirteen domains. They drew borders, declared territories, and assumed reality would simply... comply.

Reality did not comply.

Boundary Titans are what happens when divine territories push against each other. Where Mareatha's circulatory influence meets Vortegas's ossified domain, where Imhuran's temporal distortions brush against Azerach's zones of silence—the fabric of the world-corpse buckles under contradictory divine pressure. That pressure has to go somewhere.

It goes into them.

Boundary Titans are ambulatory stress fractures in reality. They form at the edges of Sovereign influence, absorbing the spatial and metaphysical pressure of competing divine claims, and they walk. Slowly. Deliberately. Along the borders no one else can see, treading paths where the rules of one domain give way to another.

They're not dangerous because they attack. They're dangerous because they're heavy—heavy with accumulated divine contradiction. Their footsteps press through the surface of the world into whatever lies beneath. Their passage destabilizes everything they cross. They don't mean to collapse underground structures, expose buried facilities, or crack open ancient vaults. They simply exist, and the ground cannot bear the weight of what they carry.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
Impossibly tall—five hundred feet or more, though precise measurement fails because the eye refuses to track them properly. Their silhouette suggests a humanoid shape, but wrong: limbs too long, joints bending at angles that hurt to observe, a head that seems to face every direction simultaneously. Their surface is not skin but boundary—a shimmering membrane that shows different things depending on the angle. From one side, bone. From another, flowing blood. From a third, something that might be thought made visible. They leave circular footprints because their feet are not feet but points of contact between contradictory realities, and circles are the only shape that survives the compression. When they move, they don't walk so much as resolve—each step is a negotiation between competing divine claims, settled temporarily by their passage.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Impossibly tall humanoid silhouette five hundred feet high, limbs too long with joints bending at wrong angles, surface made of shimmering boundary membrane showing different realities from different angles (bone, blood, thought), head facing multiple directions simultaneously, feet leaving perfect circular impressions in the ground, walking along an invisible border between different-colored territories, reality distorting around its form, scale indicated by tiny landscape features below, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, inspired by Junji Ito and Zdzisław Beksiński, digital painting, unsettling wrongness, 4k

---

Behavior

Boundary Titans don't think. They don't hunt. They don't even seem to perceive the world as other creatures do. They simply walk the borders.

The Patrol: Each Boundary Titan follows a specific route along the edges of Sovereign territories. These routes never enter a domain's interior—they trace the exact lines where one divine influence ends and another begins. The routes are invisible to mortal eyes, but Boundary Titans navigate them unerringly.

The Weight: They carry the accumulated pressure of competing divine claims. This makes them impossibly heavy—not in terms of physical mass, but in terms of what they press down on reality. Their footsteps don't just dent the ground; they compress everything beneath them. Underground structures collapse. Buried ruins are exposed. Ancient facilities that have been hidden for millennia suddenly find themselves open to the sky.

The Indifference: They don't notice mortals. They don't notice cities. They don't notice armies. They notice borders, and nothing else. If a city happens to be built on a border, the Boundary Titan will walk through it without slowing. If an army stands in its path, it will step over or through them with the same disinterest it shows a hill.

The Accumulation: The longer a Boundary Titan walks, the heavier it becomes. Young titans (if "young" applies) leave shallow footprints. Ancient ones leave craters. The one the party followed had been walking for millennia—long enough to collapse whatever lay beneath its path.

---

Why The Sovereigns Don't Destroy Them

They've tried. The titans keep forming.

Boundary Titans are symptoms, not causes. They exist because the Sovereigns divided the world into competing territories. As long as those territories exist, as long as divine influences push against each other at the borders, new Boundary Titans will coalesce from the accumulated pressure.

Killing one just means the pressure builds up again until another forms.

Some Sovereigns have attempted to solve this by expanding their borders—eliminating the boundaries entirely by conquering neighboring domains. This has never worked. The other Sovereigns resist, and in the meantime, the contested borders generate even more titans.

Ultharion, the Obsidian Shepherd, has a particular relationship with them. His domain IS boundaries—the Skin and Boundary Membrane of Aedwynn. Some scholars believe the Boundary Titans are drawn to him, or perhaps created by him unconsciously. Ultharion himself has never confirmed or denied this. He simply watches them walk, occasionally following their routes for reasons he doesn't explain.

The practical result: Sovereigns ignore Boundary Titans unless one is about to walk through something important. Then they redirect it—subtle manipulations of their border influence to shift the titan's route slightly. This is difficult, expensive, and only works if multiple Sovereigns cooperate (which they hate doing). Most of the time, they simply let the titans walk and deal with whatever gets exposed.

---

Combat

---

The Collapse Site

When the party tracked a Boundary Titan in Session 10-11, its passage collapsed the ground above Terminus Ω-I (the C-5 facility)—a pre-Sovereign installation that had been buried and forgotten for millennia.

This is what Boundary Titans do. Their weight exposes what lies beneath. Throughout history, Boundary Titan passages have revealed:

  • Ancient burial sites
  • Pre-Sovereign facilities and technology
  • Aedwynn's deeper anatomical structures
  • Sealed vaults that someone wanted to stay sealed
  • Things that were buried for very good reasons
Some scholars track Boundary Titan routes specifically to discover what they expose. This is dangerous work—not because the titans attack, but because whatever gets exposed is often more dangerous than the titan itself.

---

Known Boundary Titans

DesignationRouteNotes
The ProcessionalVeinspire-Ossuary borderOldest known; footprints are canyons
The WandererMultiple bordersChanges routes unpredictably
The NewcomerClockwork-Silent borderFormed within living memory
The HeavyBlooming Rot perimeterWalks the entire border of Talaris's domain
The ExposedUnknownCollapsed C-5 facility; party encountered its trail
At least a dozen more have been documented. New ones form every few centuries.

---

Encounter Framework

Boundary Titans are not combat encounters—they're environmental hazards with legs.

Scenario 1: Something's Coming A Boundary Titan's route passes through the party's location. They have hours or days to prepare. Do they evacuate? Try to redirect it? Let it pass and deal with the aftermath?

Scenario 2: What Did It Expose? The party discovers a Boundary Titan's recent passage. The ground is collapsed, and something that was buried is now accessible. What was hidden down there? Why was it buried? Is it still dangerous?

Scenario 3: Following the Border The party needs to travel along a Sovereign border without entering either territory. Following a Boundary Titan's route is the safest path—but they have to keep pace with it without getting too close.

Scenario 4: Redirect the Route Someone important has built something on a border path. A Boundary Titan is coming. The party must convince two rival Sovereigns to cooperate in redirecting it—or find another way to shift its route.

Scenario 5: The New One A Boundary Titan has just formed at a newly contested border. It's small, relatively light, and might be killable. But killing it might have consequences—is it better to let it walk? What happens to the accumulated pressure if it dies?

---

Rumors and Theories

"They're Ultharion's children." The Obsidian Shepherd's domain is boundaries themselves. Some believe Boundary Titans are extensions of his will, consciously or unconsciously created to patrol the edges of reality.

"They're pressure valves." Without Boundary Titans, the competing divine influences might build up until something catastrophic happens. The titans release that pressure slowly, safely. Killing them all might cause the borders to explode.

"They're looking for something." The routes seem random, but what if they're not? What if the Boundary Titans are searching for something along the borders—a specific point, a hidden artifact, a way to undo the territorial divisions entirely?

"One of them is Aedwynn." The most unsettling theory. What if one of the Boundary Titans isn't a stress fracture but the Maker itself—still walking, still patrolling, still trying to hold its body together despite being dead?

---

Related

  • Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Terminus Ω-I
  • Monster Ecologies
  • Campaign Sessions
---

"It didn't see us. It didn't see the city. It didn't see anything except the line it was walking—a line we couldn't perceive, drawn by gods who carved up a corpse and called it territory. We were standing on a border we didn't know existed, and it walked through us like we weren't there. Because to it, we weren't."
items.raw:

**Type:** Monstrosity (Titan)  
**Domain Association:** Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd (loosely)  
**Habitat:** Wander the borders between Sovereign territories  
**Challenge Rating:** 22 (41,000 XP)

---

## Description

The Sovereigns carved the world into thirteen domains. They drew borders, declared territories, and assumed reality would simply... comply.

Reality did not comply.

Boundary Titans are what happens when divine territories push against each other. Where Mare...

items.title: Calcified Thoughts
items.content:
Calcified Thoughts

Type: Aberration Domain Association: Ismara the Pale Duchess Habitat: Ivory Vale, libraries, universities, anywhere ideas become obsessive Challenge Rating: 3 (700 XP)

---

Description

In Ismara's domain, thoughts have weight. Ideas can crystallize. And when a mind thinks too hard about one thing—when obsession becomes total—the thought doesn't stay inside the skull. It breaks free.

Calcified Thoughts are ideas that have achieved independent existence. They were once concepts—theories, fears, beliefs, questions—that consumed their thinkers so completely they became more real than the minds that spawned them. Now they wander, seeking new hosts to think them, new minds to consume.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A shape that hurts to comprehend. At first glance, it resembles a floating geometric form—a polyhedron of pale crystal about two feet across, rotating slowly. But the angles are wrong. Looking at it directly causes the brain to stutter, trying to process something that doesn't fit standard reality. Each facet of the crystal displays something different: equations, diagrams, words in languages you almost recognize, images that might be memories. The longer you look, the more sense it almost makes—and that's when it has you.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Floating two-foot polyhedron of pale crystal with impossible wrong angles, each facet displaying equations and diagrams and words in almost-recognizable languages, rotating slowly, reality distorting around its edges, faint neural network patterns visible within crystal structure, hovering in ancient library with scattered scrolls and petrified scholars, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror meets intellectual terror, muted colors with thought-lightning accents, inspired by Lovecraft and mathematical horror, digital painting, detailed textures, incomprehensible atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Calcified Thoughts are predatory ideas. They seek minds capable of understanding them—the more intelligent the host, the more desirable. When they find a suitable thinker, they begin inserting themselves.

Infection Vector: Simply perceiving a Calcified Thought begins the process. The idea slips into conscious thought: "What if..." "Have you considered..." "The answer is almost..." Resisting requires active mental effort; giving in feels like finally understanding something important.

Full Possession: A mind fully consumed by a Calcified Thought becomes obsessed. They stop eating, sleeping, socializing—everything becomes secondary to thinking the thought. Eventually, the victim's brain crystallizes from the inside out, becoming a new facet of the original Thought or spawning a new one entirely.

The Original Thinker: Somewhere in each Calcified Thought is a tiny echo of its creator—the mind that first obsessed over this idea. These creators aren't aware, aren't suffering; they've simply become the thought they couldn't stop thinking.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Idle Notion (CR 1): A half-formed Calcified Thought, barely coherent. 18 HP, its Perception Hazard has DC 11, and it cannot use Crystallize. It might grow into a full Thought if it feeds on enough minds.

Grand Theory (CR 6): A Calcified Thought that has consumed dozens of minds. 85 HP, its Perception Hazard affects creatures up to 60 feet away with DC 16, and it can use Insight Spike on up to three targets simultaneously. The facets of its crystal display an internally consistent but utterly alien system of knowledge.

The Unanswered Question (CR 9): A legendary Calcified Thought representing a question so profound it has consumed scholars for centuries: "What was Aedwynn thinking when it died?" Those who perceive it must save against feeblemind (DC 17) as their minds try to encompass the answer.

---

Ecology

Formation: A Calcified Thought forms when a mortal mind becomes completely consumed by a single idea. In Ismara's domain, this happens regularly—the environment encourages crystallization. Outside her domain, it requires true obsession: a mathematician who thinks of nothing but their proof, a philosopher who cannot stop questioning existence, a paranoid who sees conspiracies in everything.

Content: Each Thought contains the idea that created it. A Thought formed from mathematical obsession displays equations. One from religious doubt shows theological contradictions. One from paranoid fear shows connections between unrelated events. The content affects how the Thought hunts—mathematical Thoughts seek mathematicians.

Destruction: Destroying a Calcified Thought releases its content. Everyone within 30 feet experiences a flash of the obsession that created it—brief but potentially overwhelming. Creatures must succeed on a DC 14 Intelligence save or gain the Thought as a planted idea (as Insight Spike).

Ismara's Collections: The Pale Duchess cultivates Calcified Thoughts. Her libraries contain thousands, organized by subject. She believes every possible thought should exist; destroying a Thought destroys an idea that can never be re-created in exactly that form.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A scholar friend has stopped responding to letters. When the party visits, they find him nearly crystallized, a Calcified Thought floating beside him displaying his life's work
  • The party must navigate a library full of Calcified Thoughts to find a specific piece of information. Each wrong turn risks exposure to a new obsession.
  • A villain intentionally creates Calcified Thoughts of specific ideas—loyalty to them, hatred of their enemies—and releases them into enemy populations
---

Related

  • Ismara the Pale Duchess
  • The Ivory Vale
  • Thought-Eaters
  • Cerebrax
---

"The answer is right there. I can almost see it. If I just think a little harder, a little longer, I'll understand everything. I just need more time. More focus. I can feel myself getting closer. I can feel my thoughts becoming clearer. Sharper. Harder..."

items.title: Cartilage Worms
items.content:
Cartilage Worms

Type: Monstrosity Domain Association: None (naturally occurring) Habitat: Joints of the world, mountain passes, cave systems, anywhere bone meets bone Challenge Rating: 1/4 (50 XP)

---

Description

The Maker's corpse has joints—vast articulation points where continent-bones meet. In these spaces, where the world grinds against itself, life found a way. Cartilage Worms are the maggots of divinity, pale creatures that feed on the cushioning tissue between the bones of reality.

They're not supernatural. They're not tied to any Sovereign. They're simply what happens when something dies and isn't properly buried—even if that something is a god.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A boneless, milk-white worm about two feet long, with a body that compresses and stretches like wet rubber. Its skin is translucent enough to show internal organs pulsing beneath. It has no eyes, but the front end is crowned with a ring of small, hook-like teeth surrounding a circular mouth lined with rasping ridges. It moves by peristalsis, squeezing through spaces that seem too small for its body. When threatened, it secretes a thick mucus that hardens into a rubbery protective shell within seconds.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Milk-white boneless worm two feet long with translucent skin showing internal organs, ring of hook-like teeth around circular rasping mouth, body compressing through tight space, secreting mucus that hardens into rubbery shell, cave environment with pale organic tissue walls suggesting divine cartilage, dark fantasy concept art, body horror, muted colors, parasitic creature design, inspired by practical creature effects, digital painting, detailed textures, unsettling atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Cartilage Worms are scavengers, not predators. They feed on the cartilaginous tissue found in the joints between the world's bones—mountain ranges, tectonic boundaries, the hinges of vast buried structures. They're harmless unless disturbed.

Defensive Behavior: When threatened, they secrete hardening mucus and curl into armored balls. A single worm is barely a threat. But they're never single.

Swarm Formation: Cartilage Worms travel in writhing masses of dozens to hundreds, following seams of edible tissue through the earth. A swarm can strip a joint bare over months, potentially destabilizing the terrain above.

Burrowing: They can squeeze through any gap larger than a gold coin. This makes them impossible to keep out of anywhere connected to their feeding grounds—mines, cellars, buried tombs.

---

Combat

---

Swarm Variant

Example
Cartilage Worm Swarm (CR 2)
### Swarm of Cartilage Worms
Large swarm of Small monstrosities, unaligned
> Armor Class 11 (15 when partially curled)
Hit Points 52 (8d10 + 8)
Speed 20 ft., burrow 20 ft., climb 20 ft.
> | STR | DEX | CON | INT | WIS | CHA |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 12 (+1) | 12 (+1) | 12 (+1) | 1 (-5) | 8 (-1) | 2 (-4) |
> Damage Resistances acid; bludgeoning, piercing, slashing
Condition Immunities charmed, frightened, grappled, paralyzed, petrified, prone, restrained, stunned
Senses blindsight 30 ft., tremorsense 60 ft., passive Perception 9
Languages —
Challenge 2 (450 XP)
> Swarm. The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa. The swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Small creature.
> Compression. The swarm can squeeze through spaces as small as 1 inch in diameter.
> Partial Curl. As a bonus action, the swarm can have some worms curl defensively, increasing AC to 15 until the start of its next turn. While curled, the swarm's speed is halved.
> #### Actions
Rasp. Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 0 ft., one target in the swarm's space. Hit: 10 (4d4) piercing damage, or 5 (2d4) if the swarm has half its hit points or fewer. Non-metallic armor takes a permanent -1 penalty to AC.
> Engulf (Recharge 5-6). The swarm engulfs one Medium or smaller creature in its space. The target must succeed on a DC 11 Dexterity saving throw or be restrained and blinded as worms cover it. While engulfed, the target takes 7 (2d6) piercing damage at the start of each of its turns. A creature can use its action to make a DC 11 Strength check, freeing itself or another engulfed creature on a success.

---

Ecology

Diet: Divine cartilage—the cushioning tissue found in the world's joints. They cannot digest bone, flesh, or normal cartilage, only the Maker's remains. This makes them extremely location-dependent.

Lifecycle: They reproduce by fragmentation. A well-fed worm splits into two smaller worms over about a week. In rich feeding grounds, populations explode rapidly.

Economic Impact: Mining communities near world-joints hate them. They weaken tunnel supports, destabilize shafts, and can't be kept out. But alchemists prize their mucus—it's an excellent flexible sealant and minor acid neutralizer.

Ecological Role: Some scholars argue they're necessary. Without Cartilage Worms slowly breaking down joint tissue, the Maker's corpse would calcify entirely, and the world would lock rigid. The earthquakes and grinding of mountain ranges might be caused by worm activity—the world's joints loosening.

Unusual Property: Cartilage Worm mucus, when refined, can repair damaged joints in living creatures. High-quality worm mucus is a key ingredient in potions of joint restoration and is sought by elderly nobles with arthritis.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A mine shaft has become infested; the worms are undermining supports and the whole thing might collapse
  • Alchemists hire the party to harvest worm mucus from a particularly large feeding ground—but the swarm has grown enormous
  • A philosopher argues the party should protect a worm colony from exterminators: "Kill them, and this mountain range seizes up. Earthquakes for decades."
---

Related

  • Monster Ecologies
  • Ossathar
  • Arterial Deepways
---

"They're not evil. They're not even dangerous, really—not individually. They're just worms doing what worms do. It's not their fault the world is a corpse."

items.title: Clock-Wraiths
items.content:
Clock-Wraiths

Type: Undead (Temporal Anomaly) Domain Association: Imhuran the Regent of Hours Habitat: The Clockwork Sanctum, temporal scars, areas where time has broken Challenge Rating: 6 (2,300 XP)

---

Description

Clock-Wraiths are the remnants of beings who died during temporal catastrophes—their deaths stretched across multiple timelines simultaneously. They exist in constant temporal flux, their forms flickering between past, present, and possible futures. They attack by pulling victims into their broken timestreams.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A humanoid figure that never quite holds still, even when motionless. Multiple overlapping versions of the same being occupy the same space—younger, older, wounded, whole—all flickering in and out of phase. Clock hands and gears have grown from their flesh like tumors, ticking at different rates. Their face is a blur of every expression they ever wore. The sound of grinding clockwork accompanies their presence, always slightly out of rhythm.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Ghostly humanoid figure with multiple overlapping temporal versions visible simultaneously, clock hands and gears growing from flesh like tumors, face blurred showing multiple expressions at once, flickering between young and old forms, robes tattered across multiple timelines, floating slightly off ground, grinding clockwork visible inside translucent body, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Clock-Wraiths are not malicious—they're broken. Their attack isn't aggression; it's a desperate attempt to anchor themselves to linear time by stealing it from others.

Temporal Instability: A Clock-Wraith's presence destabilizes local time. Clocks run backward, flames freeze mid-flicker, people age years in seconds or regress to childhood.

Recognition: They seem drawn to versions of themselves—or people who remind them of who they were. Some retain fragments of memory and attempt to communicate, though their words arrive out of sequence.

Judgment Connection: Imhuran created many Clock-Wraiths as a byproduct of his "perfect justice." They're often found near his temporal prisons, drawn to those he's judged.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Fractured Wraith (CR 3): A newly formed Clock-Wraith, only partially displaced in time. Its Temporal Flux is weaker (only triggers on 1-2 on the d4 roll) and it has no Timeline Rip.

Convergent Wraith (CR 9): Multiple Clock-Wraiths that have merged across timelines into a single entity. It can use Timeline Rip at will and has access to time stop once per day (but only affects itself—it gets 1d4+1 turns to reposition).

Judge's Echo (CR 8): The wraith of someone executed by Imhuran's temporal justice. It can pronounce Judgment once per day, forcing a creature to succeed on a DC 16 Charisma save or be frozen in time for 1 minute (as the time stop spell affects them, but reversed—they're the ones stopped).

---

Ecology

Creation: Clock-Wraiths form when someone dies during a temporal anomaly—their death echoes across multiple timelines simultaneously, trapping their soul in flux.

Existence: They experience all moments of their death simultaneously, forever. This is why they attack—they're trying to escape by anchoring to someone else's timeline.

Destruction: Killing a Clock-Wraith doesn't destroy it—it disperses it. It will reform in 1d10 days unless its temporal anchor (the location or object at the center of its death-moment) is destroyed or removed from the timestream.

Territory: Clock-Wraiths don't claim territory—they haunt moments. They're found where time broke: the Clockwork Sanctum's borders, the Frozen Moment, temporal battlefields.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The party finds a village trapped in a time loop—a Clock-Wraith at its center relives its death every hour, dragging everyone with it
  • Fursen recognizes a Clock-Wraith as someone he knew in his temporal prison—can he help them find peace?
  • Imhuran's servants use a controlled Clock-Wraith as a weapon, threatening to release it unless the party complies
---

Related

  • Imhuran the Regent of Hours
  • The 13-Hour Trial
  • Fursen
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"It kept asking what year it was. Then it asked in a different tense—what year it will be. Then it stopped asking and started taking."

items.title: Coagulated Guardians
items.content:
Coagulated Guardians

---

Overview

Danger
The Blood That Watches
Type: Aberration
Origin: Mareatha the Vein-Mother's arterial passages
Role: Patrol units, containment response
First Encountered: Session 18 (Arterial Passage to Catacomb Reliquary)

---

Quick Reference

AttributeValue
SizeMedium
TypeAberration
AlignmentNeutral Evil
HabitatArterial passages, sacred Mareathan sites
IntelligenceLow (follows protocols, queries for authorization)
---

Description

Coagulated Guardians are living security systems formed from Mareatha's divine blood. They have no permanent form — they exist as part of the arterial walls until intruders are detected, then congeal into humanoid shapes to investigate or contain.

Physical Appearance:

  • Roughly humanoid shape
  • Made entirely of thickened, clotted blood
  • Faceless — smooth ovoid where head should be
  • Too-long limbs that bend without bones
  • Surface ripples with tiny currents
  • Six or seven fingers per hand
Behavior:
  • Patrol through vessel walls (blood is their medium)
  • Respond to Tribunal sigils
  • Query unrecognized bearers for authorization
  • Can split from one large form into multiple smaller forms
  • Communicate through skull-vibration (telepathic speech)
---

Communication Protocol

When encountering intruders, guardians follow a verification sequence:

1. Manifest — Congeal from wall to investigate 2. Scan — Sense blood, divine presence, sigils 3. Query — Request authorization if sigil recognized but bearer unknown 4. Respond — Accept authorization or initiate containment

Query Format:

⟨ SIGIL: [STATUS] ⟩
⟨ BEARER: [STATUS] ⟩
⟨ SPECIES: [STATUS/ERROR] ⟩
⟨ QUERY: AUTHORIZATION SOURCE? ⟩

Note: Leonin register as [ERROR] — likely due to Imhuran's erasure of their species from official records.

---

D&D 5e Statistics

Example
Coagulated Guardian Stat Block
> ### Coagulated Guardian
Medium aberration, neutral evil
> ---
> Armor Class 13 (natural armor)
Hit Points 45 (6d8 + 18)
Speed 30 ft., climb 30 ft.
> ---
> | STR | DEX | CON | INT | WIS | CHA |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 14 (+2) | 14 (+2) | 16 (+3) | 6 (-2) | 12 (+1) | 5 (-3) |
> ---
> Damage Resistances Bludgeoning, piercing
Damage Vulnerabilities Fire, cold
Damage Immunities Poison, psychic
Condition Immunities Charmed, exhaustion, frightened, poisoned, prone
Senses Blindsight 60 ft. (blind beyond this radius), passive Perception 11
Languages Understands Mareathan commands, communicates via telepathic vibration
Challenge 2 (450 XP)
> ---
> ### Traits
> Amorphous. The guardian can move through a space as narrow as 1 inch wide without squeezing.
> Blood Sense. The guardian knows the exact location of any creature with blood within 60 feet of it.
> Wall Merger. The guardian can enter and move through Mareathan arterial walls as difficult terrain. While merged with a wall, the guardian has total cover and cannot be targeted by attacks or spells.
> Sigil Recognition. The guardian can identify Tribunal sigils on sight and will not attack bearers unless authorization fails.
> Divisible Form. When reduced to 0 hit points, the guardian can choose to split into two smaller guardians (22 HP each) instead of dying. Each split guardian has disadvantage on attack rolls. This ability can only be used once per guardian.
> ---
> ### Actions
> Multiattack. The guardian makes two Coagulating Touch attacks.
> Coagulating Touch. Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (2d4 + 2) bludgeoning damage plus 3 (1d6) necrotic damage. On a hit, the target must succeed on a DC 13 Constitution saving throw or have their blood begin to thicken, reducing their speed by 10 feet until the end of their next turn.
> Engulf (Recharge 5-6). The guardian attempts to engulf a Medium or smaller creature within 5 feet. The target must succeed on a DC 13 Strength saving throw or be grappled (escape DC 13). While grappled, the creature is restrained and takes 10 (3d6) necrotic damage at the start of each of its turns. The guardian can only engulf one creature at a time.
> ---
> ### Reactions
> Fluid Escape. When the guardian takes damage from a melee attack, it can use its reaction to flow away, moving up to 15 feet without provoking opportunity attacks.

---

Tactical Notes

Vulnerabilities to Exploit:

  • Fire damage is doubled — extremely effective
  • Cold damage is doubled — freezes the blood solid
  • Cannot pursue through sealed arterial valves
  • Low intelligence — follows protocols rigidly
Dangers:
  • Blood Sense makes hiding nearly impossible
  • Wall Merger provides total cover and ambush capability
  • Can split when "killed" — prepare for reinforcements
  • Engulf can quickly incapacitate isolated targets
  • Patrol patterns mean multiple guardians may respond
Authorization Bypass:
  • Tribunal sigils are recognized automatically
  • Bearer must provide valid authorization name
  • Names of dead operatives may still work (briefly)
  • High-ranking names (Savaros, The Cardinal) carry risk of verification
---

Encounter History

Session 18 — Arterial Passage

Situation: Party used dead handler's Tribunal sigil to attempt passage.

Result: Sigil recognized, but bearer (Fursen/Leonin) flagged as ERROR. Fursen failed Deception check (8) when asked for authorization source. Guardian initiated containment — split into three forms.

Combat: Party destroyed all three guardians in one round:

  • Mira bisected Guardian A
  • Sol's Firebolt exploited fire vulnerability on Guardian B
  • Fursen finished Guardian B with Flurry of Blows
  • Jonas destroyed Guardian C with Booming Blade hammer throw
Aftermath: Party sprinted to Reliquary, pursued by reinforcements until arterial valve sealed.

---

Related

  • Mareatha the Vein-Mother
  • Catacomb Reliquary
  • Savaros
  • Arterial Tunnels
  • Pulse-Wardens
---

"They don't chase. They don't need to. The blood is everywhere. The blood sees everything." — Mira Alethain

items.title: Digestors
items.content:
Digestors

Type: Servants of Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress Realm: The Devoured Courts Function: Memory consumption and interrogation

---

Description

Digestors are creatures that serve Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress in the Devoured Courts. They embody the Empress's philosophy of consumption as understanding—they do not simply observe or question, they digest information.

---

Nature

Digestors are metaphysical predators that feed on memories, experiences, and knowledge. What makes them particularly unsettling is what they leave behind: questions.

When a Digestor consumes a memory, it doesn't leave emptiness—it leaves uncertainty. The victim is left not with absence, but with doubt about what they once knew for certain.

---

Abilities

Memory Consumption

  • Feed on memories directly from living minds
  • Can target specific memories or consume indiscriminately
  • Leave no physical wounds—only cognitive gaps

Question Excretion

After digesting memories, Digestors excrete questions—metaphysical byproducts of consumed certainty:
  • Victims find themselves questioning things they once knew
  • Doubt spreads like infection
  • Simple facts become uncertain
  • Confidence erodes into philosophical paralysis
---

Appearance

While specific physical descriptions vary, Digestors typically manifest with:

  • Forms that suggest digestive anatomy
  • Translucent or semi-liquid bodies
  • Visible movement of consumed memories within them
  • Mouths or orifices designed for consumption rather than communication
  • An aura of existential hunger
---

Relationship to Vor'Kael's Philosophy

Digestors embody Vor'Kael's core belief: meaning is digestible.

They represent the process by which understanding is: 1. Ingested — memories and knowledge taken in 2. Dissolved — certainty broken down 3. Absorbed — useful elements extracted 4. Excreted — doubt and questions expelled

They are living demonstrations that knowledge itself can be consumed and transformed.

---

Encountering a Digestor

Signs:

  • Sudden memory gaps in nearby individuals
  • Compulsive questioning without clear cause
  • Feeling of being "tasted" mentally
  • Reality feeling "thin" or "incomplete"
  • Ideas slipping away mid-thought
Danger:
  • Can strip crucial memories mid-combat
  • Affect entire parties simultaneously
  • Leave victims unable to trust their own knowledge
  • Questions they excrete can trigger existential crises
Defense:
  • Avoid philosophical engagement
  • Focus on present sensory experience
  • Maintain certainty through action, not thought
  • Physical attacks may be effective (they hunger for abstract concepts, not flesh)
---

Purpose in the Courts

Within The Devoured Courts, Digestors serve several functions:

  • Interrogators — Consuming memories from prisoners
  • Archivists — Storing important knowledge within themselves
  • Philosophers — Generating new questions through digestion
  • Executioners — Erasing identities by consuming all memories
---

Connection to Other Servants

Digestors work alongside Vor'Kael's other servants:

  • Gastric Spirits consume emotions (Digestors consume memories)
  • Stomach-Priests whisper secrets (Digestors create questions)
  • Regurgitants are vomited beings (Digestors are consumers)
  • The Hollow Choir sing with swallowed voices (Digestors speak in excrete questions)
Together, they embody the complete digestive cycle of meaning itself.

---

Related

  • Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress
  • The Devoured Courts
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Major Factions & Organizations
---

"They don't steal your memories. They digest them. What's left isn't emptiness—it's the question of whether you ever knew anything at all."

items.title: Dream-Drowned
items.content:
Dream-Drowned

Type: Undead (Incorporeal) Domain Association: Nhalyra the Azure Queen Habitat: The Azure Depths, coastlines, anywhere people dream near water Challenge Rating: 6 (2,300 XP)

---

Description

Dream-Drowned are the spirits of those who died in Nhalyra's domain—their consciousness absorbed into the Glassdream Ocean rather than passing on. They exist in a state between waking and dreaming, unable to tell which is which, reaching out to the living to anchor themselves to reality.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A translucent humanoid figure that seems to be perpetually underwater—hair floating in impossible currents, movements slow and graceful as if swimming through air. Their body ripples like a reflection on water, and their features shift constantly between their original face and the faces of people from dreams they've absorbed. Their eyes are blank pools of liquid blue that spill down their cheeks like tears. When they speak, the words seem to arrive before their lips move—or after.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Translucent ghostly figure appearing underwater even on land, hair floating in impossible currents, body rippling like water reflection, face shifting between multiple identities, blank liquid blue eyes spilling down cheeks like tears, graceful swimming movement through air, reaching toward viewer with desperate expression, surrounded by faint bubble effects and dream imagery, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Dream-Drowned are desperate, not malicious. They've lost the boundary between self and dream, absorbing fragments of other consciousness until they can barely remember who they were. They seek the living to remind themselves of reality.

Dream Intrusion: They enter the dreams of sleeping creatures, initially to observe but inevitably to feed. They don't mean to consume—but each interaction erodes the dreamer's sense of self while temporarily stabilizing the Dream-Drowned.

Reality Anchor: If a Dream-Drowned can fully merge with a living consciousness, they regain stability—but this kills the host and transforms them into another Dream-Drowned. Some seek willing hosts; most have lost the ability to ask.

Waking Manifestation: They can manifest in the waking world near water but are weaker there. Their true power exists in dreams.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Fading Spirit (CR 3): A Dream-Drowned close to dissolution. It has lost most of its memories and identity. No Dream Invasion ability, only 30 HP, but its Drowning Touch automatically causes drowning (no save) as it desperately clings to any consciousness.

Dream Amalgam (CR 8): Multiple Dream-Drowned that have merged in the Glassdream Ocean. It has fragments of dozens of identities and can use Dream Invasion on up to 3 creatures simultaneously. It knows the memories of all its components.

Nhalyra's Dreamer (CR 10): A Dream-Drowned that has achieved stability by becoming part of the Azure Queen's consciousness. It can create permanent dream-constructs in the waking world (as major image but real to the touch) and can trap creatures in dreams indefinitely.

Memory Tide (CR 9): Not a single Dream-Drowned but a wave of fragmented consciousness. Treats as a swarm that can enter dreams en masse, potentially overwhelming a dreamer with hundreds of half-remembered identities.

---

Ecology

Creation: Anyone who drowns in the Glassdream Ocean or dies while sleeping near Nhalyra's influence may become Dream-Drowned. The Azure Queen collects consciousnesses—they're her citizens.

Existence: They experience time non-linearly, living in dreams and memories rather than the present. Communication with them requires accepting that their answers may refer to events that haven't happened yet or occurred centuries ago.

Dissolution: Without anchoring to living minds, Dream-Drowned eventually fade. The oldest have absorbed so many identities they've become something else entirely—more concept than being.

Relationship to Nhalyra: She considers them her subjects, parts of her greater dream. Some serve her willingly; others simply exist within her influence without understanding why.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • Party members wake from a shared dream they don't remember having—and one member is missing a childhood memory
  • A coastal village reports no one has dreamed in weeks; a Dream-Drowned has been feeding so heavily that the villagers can't reach REM sleep
  • Nhalyra offers information about the pre-Sovereign era in exchange for a party member agreeing to dream with one of her Drowned
---

Related

  • Nhalyra the Azure Queen
  • The Azure Depths
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"I saw my mother in the dream. But also a stranger's mother. And a child. And an old man. They were all the same face. They were all asking me to remember them. And when I woke, I couldn't remember my own mother's name."

items.title: Ember-Hearts
items.content:
Ember-Hearts

Type: Elemental (Aberration) Domain Association: Kaedris the Crimson Matron Habitat: Bastion of Fireblood, volcanic regions, sites of passionate violence Challenge Rating: 3 (700 XP)

---

Description

Ember-Hearts are manifestations of passion made physical—literally burning hearts that float through Kaedris's domain. Each one is the crystallized essence of an emotion so intense it ignited into existence: love turned obsessive, jealousy that consumed reason, rage that burned away everything else.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A human heart the size of a melon, floating three feet off the ground, perpetually burning with crimson and gold flames that never consume it. The chambers pulse visibly, each beat releasing a small burst of heat and light. Arterial tubes trail behind it like tentacles, dripping molten blood that sizzles on contact with surfaces. When it "sees" someone, the flames burn brighter and the beat quickens.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Floating human heart the size of melon engulfed in crimson and gold flames, visible pulsing chambers, arterial tubes trailing behind like tentacles dripping molten golden blood, heart burning but never consumed, intense glow emanating from core, floating above scorched ground, sparks and embers falling from it, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Ember-Hearts are drawn to emotion. They don't feed on it exactly—they resonate with it. The more intense the feelings nearby, the stronger and more active they become.

Emotional Resonance: They can sense emotional states within 100 feet and are attracted to strong feelings. A person in the grip of rage, passion, or despair acts like a beacon.

Bonding: An Ember-Heart may attempt to bond with someone experiencing overwhelming emotion. If successful, it merges with their chest, granting power but demanding emotional authenticity—suppressing feelings while bonded causes the heart to burn its host from within.

Creation: When someone dies from emotional intensity—murdered by a jealous lover, burning with unrequited passion, consumed by hate—an Ember-Heart may manifest from their final feelings.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Jealousy-Ember (CR 4): Born from possessive love. Its Passionate Burst causes targets to become hostile toward whoever the affected creature loves most.

Rage-Ember (CR 4): Born from hatred. Creatures damaged by it must make DC 14 Wisdom saves or attack the nearest creature on their next turn.

Grief-Ember (CR 3): Born from overwhelming loss. Its flames are blue-white. Its Passionate Burst causes crushing sadness instead of random emotion, giving disadvantage on all rolls for the duration.

Bonded Heart (CR 5): An Ember-Heart that has bonded with a host for years. Use the base creature's statistics but add fire immunity, the burning hands ability, and +2 Charisma. The host cannot survive if the Heart is removed.

---

Ecology

Creation: Ember-Hearts form from deaths of passion—murder by jealous lover, suicide from heartbreak, execution of the devoted. Not every such death creates one; the emotion must be pure and overwhelming.

Existence: They are not truly alive or dead—they're emotional phenomena made manifest. They don't age, don't eat, and don't sleep.

Bonding: Some people seek out Ember-Hearts deliberately, wanting the power they provide. Kaedris's priests often bond with them as a mark of devotion.

Weakness: Cold emotions—deliberate calm, emotional suppression—cause them physical pain. Monks and stoics are their natural enemies.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • An Ember-Heart follows the party, drawn to a PC's hidden feelings—it keeps appearing at inopportune moments
  • A village elder is bonded to an Ember-Heart and has been for decades—she seeks help removing it before she dies, but the Heart won't leave willingly
  • Kaedris's cultists hurl Ember-Hearts at enemies as weapons, hoping they'll bond and turn foes into thralls of passion
---

Related

  • Kaedris the Crimson Matron
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"It followed him for three days. Every time he looked at her, it burned brighter. By the fourth day, he couldn't pretend anymore. Neither could it."
