items.title: Ember-Scorned
items.content:
Ember-Scorned

Type: Undead Domain Association: Kaedris the Crimson Matron Habitat: Bastion of Fireblood, sites of betrayed love, places where passion turned to violence Challenge Rating: 4 (1,100 XP)

---

Description

In the Bastion of Fireblood, love is not gentle. It is a binding oath carved into the soul and burned into the flesh. When that love is betrayed—when vows are broken, when passion curdles into hatred—the wound doesn't heal. It ignites.

Ember-Scorned are the remains of lovers betrayed so profoundly that their pain refused to die with them. They burn eternally with the passion that was turned against them, hunting not their betrayers specifically, but all who remind them of love's cruelty.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
Once human, now a walking cremation. The Ember-Scorned's body is blackened and cracked like cooling magma, with lines of molten orange-red glowing through the fissures. Their face retains just enough features to suggest who they were—the curve of a jaw, the hollow of an eye socket—but the expression is frozen in a rictus of anguished rage. They wear the remnants of whatever they died in: wedding clothes turned to ash that never falls, armor fused to flesh, a lover's gift melted into their chest. Where their heart was, there's only a cavity filled with white-hot flame that pulses like a heartbeat.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Humanoid figure with blackened cracked body like cooling magma, lines of molten orange-red glowing through fissures, face retaining just enough features to suggest former identity frozen in anguished rage, wearing ash-remnants of wedding clothes that never fall, heart cavity filled with white-hot pulsing flame, walking through volcanic landscape with rivers of molten blood, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, tragic romantic horror, inspired by Berserk and Dark Souls, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Ember-Scorned are driven by a single, terrible compulsion: to share their suffering. They're drawn to expressions of love—wedding celebrations, tender reunions, even simple displays of affection. When they find love, they destroy it.

Hunting Pattern: They don't attack immediately. First, they watch. They observe the love they've found, learning its shape, its vulnerabilities. Then they target the bond itself—killing one partner while forcing the other to watch, or corrupting the relationship until it self-destructs.

Memory Burns: Each Ember-Scorned carries specific memories of their betrayal. These memories leak out as heat—standing near one, you might suddenly feel a flash of jealousy that isn't yours, or hear whispered accusations in a dead lover's voice.

The Fire Within: They cannot be cooled. Water evaporates before touching them. Cold magic is absorbed and converted to heat. The only thing that quenches their flame, temporarily, is witnessing love truly destroy itself. A couple turning on each other satisfies them for days.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Scorned Whisper (CR 2): An Ember-Scorned whose fire has nearly burned out—little more than ash held together by spite. 27 HP, fire damage reduced to d4s, but its Memory Leak has a 60-foot radius and DC 14. It specializes in corruption, not combat.

Pyre of the Devoted (CR 7): An Ember-Scorned formed from someone whose love was absolute—a paladin betrayed by their god, a parent murdered by their child, a spouse killed by their beloved. 112 HP, AC 16, immune to radiant damage as well. Its Jealous Flame automatically affects creatures in loving relationships (no save, only half damage on save).

The Last Vow (CR 9): A legendary Ember-Scorned formed when two lovers betrayed each other simultaneously—each becoming Scorned, then fusing into a single entity of mutual destruction. Two fused figures, burning, forever reaching for each other's throats. Treats as two creatures sharing a space; when one is destroyed, the other gains its hit points and becomes frenzied.

---

Ecology

Creation: An Ember-Scorned forms when someone dies from betrayed love in an area touched by Kaedris's influence—or when the betrayal is so profound it creates that influence. The process takes 1d4 days after death; the corpse slowly chars from within, then rises.

Existence: They remember everything about their love and its destruction. This memory is their fuel—they literally burn their own history for power. An Ember-Scorned that "forgets" (through magic or time) simply crumbles to cold ash.

Prevention: In Kaedris's domain, complex rituals exist to "release" the Scorned peacefully—essentially recreating their death and allowing proper grief. Outside her domain, speak with dead followed by remove curse during the rising period can sometimes prevent the transformation.

Society's Response: The Bastion of Fireblood considers Ember-Scorned a natural consequence of oath-breaking. Some communities force betrothed couples to face captured Scorned as a warning. "Remember what awaits if you forsake your vows."

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A wedding celebration is interrupted by an Ember-Scorned—the bride's first husband, who died "accidentally" a year ago
  • The party must escort two lovers through territory haunted by Scorned. The lovers' relationship is rocky; arguments might draw attention...
  • An Ember-Scorned recognizes one party member as the reincarnation of their betrayer. It will follow them forever unless they can somehow make amends for a crime committed in a past life
---

Related

  • Kaedris the Crimson Matron
  • Bastion of Fireblood
  • Ember-Hearts
---

"She burned for three days before she rose. We could hear her crying the whole time—not from pain. From the memory. When she finally stood, she walked straight to his house. They found them together in the ashes. He was still holding her hand."

items.title: Fleshwrights
items.content:
Fleshwrights

Type: Servants of Aral-Vyn the Bladed Saint Realm: The Crucible of Flesh Function: Body sculpting and anatomical artisanry

---

Description

Fleshwrights are artisans who sculpt living bodies into new forms. They serve Aral-Vyn the Bladed Saint and embody his philosophy that flesh is an unfinished medium requiring constant refinement and perfection through removal.

---

Purpose

Fleshwrights are living sculptors who reshape the human form according to Aral-Vyn's doctrine that perfection requires excision. They do not merely heal or augment—they transform bodies into art through surgical precision and anatomical mastery.

---

Abilities

Flesh Sculpting

  • Reshape living tissue without anesthesia or hesitation
  • Remove "imperfections" (scars, asymmetry, unnecessary organs)
  • Graft muscle and sinew into functional artworks
  • Create "optimal configurations" for specific tasks

Anatomical Mastery

  • Perfect understanding of human anatomy
  • Can identify structural weaknesses at a glance
  • Reshape bones through pressure and time
  • Redirect nerve pathways

Living Art

  • Bodies they sculpt become exhibits
  • Each "improvement" is a masterpiece
  • Preservation of their work through continued maintenance
---

Process

The Correction

Fleshwrights perform annual "Corrections" on citizens of the Crucible: 1. Assessment — Identify what holds the subject back 2. Incision — Remove the imperfection 3. Refinement — Reshape remaining tissue 4. Completion — The subject is reborn, "improved"

What is removed might be:

  • Physical flaws
  • Emotional attachments
  • Limiting beliefs (carved from neural tissue)
  • Memories that cause stagnation
---

Philosophy

Fleshwrights believe:

  • Pain is enlightenment — Suffering reveals what must be cut away
  • Flesh must not stagnate — Bodies must evolve or decay
  • Identity lies beneath the skin — True self emerges through removal
  • Perfection requires sacrifice — What is unnecessary must go
They see themselves not as torturers but as liberators, freeing people from the flesh that imprisons them.

---

Appearance

Fleshwrights typically appear as:

  • Surgeons with impossibly steady hands
  • Bodies bearing countless surgical scars (their own corrections)
  • Tools integrated into their fingers and palms
  • Minimal clothing to display their own "perfected" anatomy
  • Eyes that assess structural integrity constantly
---

Cultural Impact

Within the Crucible

  • Respected as master artists
  • Sought out for voluntary corrections
  • Run living clinics where imperfection is excised
  • Train apprentices in the art of surgical perfection

Outside the Crucible

  • Feared as mutilators
  • Sought by those desperate for physical transformation
  • Hunted in regions where body modification is taboo
  • Sometimes hired as assassins (removal as art)
---

Relationship to Other Servants

  • Sinew-Priests provide the muscle; Fleshwrights shape it
  • Nerve-Bearers control sensation; Fleshwrights redirect it
  • Pain-Acolytes endure; Fleshwrights inflict with purpose
  • The Exsanguined Choir bleed musically; Fleshwrights perfect the flow
---

Notable Practices

The Gallery Walk

Annual exhibition where Fleshwrights display their "completed" subjects—living people reshaped into walking art.

The Skinless Saints

Fleshwrights who have removed their own skin to better understand muscle and nerve, walking as anatomical diagrams.

The Bone Garden

A meditation space where Fleshwrights contemplate skeletal perfection and plan future corrections.

---

Encountering a Fleshwright

Signs:

  • Surgical precision in all movements
  • Constant assessment of bodies nearby
  • Tools concealed in their own flesh
  • The faint smell of antiseptic and exposed tissue
Danger:
  • They see imperfection as an invitation
  • May attempt "corrections" without consent
  • Pain is their primary language
  • Consider resistance itself a flaw to be removed
Survival:
  • Don't show weakness or imperfection
  • Move with confidence and purpose
  • Demonstrate you are "already perfected"
  • Flee if they offer to "help"
---

Related

  • Aral-Vyn the Bladed Saint
  • The Crucible of Flesh
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns

items.title: Grave Wardens
items.content:
Grave Wardens

Classification: Undead Domain Association: Pre-Sovereign (neutral) Habitat: The Bone Galleries beneath Veinspire First Encounter: Session 16

---

DESCRIPTION

Grave Wardens are ancient guardians of the catacombs that predate the Sovereigns. They protect sacred burial grounds from desecration—by anyone, including the gods themselves.

Appearance:

  • Desiccated humanoid husks
  • Wrapped in rotted burial cloth
  • Eyes replaced by flickering green flame
  • Ancient weapons clutched in skeletal hands
  • Move with creaking, deliberate motion
  • Dust falls from joints when they move
Speech:
  • Speak in unison when multiple present
  • Hollow, rattling voice
  • Words spaced with pauses: "This... is... sacred... ground..."
  • Can detect lies through their green flame sight
---

BEHAVIOR

Purpose:

  • Guard sacred burial grounds
  • Remember the names of the dead
  • Challenge trespassers
  • Destroy those who lie on sacred ground
Neutrality: The Grave Wardens serve no Sovereign. They remember when the Sovereigns were mortal. They call them "usurpers" and do not respect their authority.

The Question: When encountering trespassers, Grave Wardens pose a single question. Answer truthfully and passage is granted. Lie, and combat begins.

What They Detect:

  • Sovereign brands and marks
  • Divine connections
  • Lies spoken on sacred ground
  • The "weight of divine attention"
---

STAT BLOCK (Estimated CR 4)

Medium undead, lawful neutral

Armor Class: 14 (natural armor) Hit Points: 52 (8d8 + 16) Speed: 25 ft.

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
16 (+3)10 (+0)14 (+2)12 (+1)16 (+3)14 (+2)
Saving Throws: Wis +5, Cha +4 Skills: Insight +7, Perception +5 Damage Resistances: Necrotic; bludgeoning, piercing, slashing from nonmagical attacks Damage Immunities: Poison Condition Immunities: Charmed, exhaustion, frightened, poisoned Senses: Darkvision 120 ft., passive Perception 15 Languages: All languages known in life, telepathy 60 ft.

Traits

Lie Detection. Grave Wardens automatically know when a creature within 30 feet speaks a deliberate falsehood. Their green flames flare when detecting lies.

Collective Voice. When multiple Grave Wardens are present, they speak in perfect unison.

Sacred Ground. While in burial grounds they protect, Grave Wardens have advantage on all attacks and saving throws.

Turn Resistance. Advantage on saving throws against turn undead effects.

Actions

Multiattack. Two attacks with ancient weapon.

Ancient Weapon. Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) slashing/bludgeoning/piercing damage (weapon dependent) plus 7 (2d6) necrotic damage.

Judgment Gaze (Recharge 5-6). One creature within 30 feet that has lied to the Warden: DC 13 Wisdom save or 22 (4d10) psychic damage and frightened for 1 minute. Half damage on success, not frightened.

---

ENCOUNTER HISTORY

Session 16 — The Bone Galleries

  • Three Grave Wardens challenged the party
  • Asked Jonas if he served or defied the usurpers
  • Jonas answered "I don't serve anyone" (Persuasion: 2)
  • Wardens detected the lie—he wears Azerach's brand
  • Nearly attacked before Mira intervened
  • Mira vouched for Jonas, explaining brand was forced (Persuasion: 26)
  • Wardens accepted Mira's word: "The old one vouches for the branded warrior"
  • Allowed passage with warning about deeper galleries
Key Dialogue:
  • "This... is... sacred... ground..."
  • "You... carry... marks... of... the... usurpers..."
  • "Do... you... serve... the... usurpers...? Or... do... you... defy... them...?"
  • "The... blood... church... hunts... you..."
  • "We... remember... the... blood... church... They... filled... these... galleries..."
  • "The... old... one... vouches... for... the... branded... warrior..."
---

NOTES

Potential Allies: The Grave Wardens hate the Mareathan Church. They remember the purges, the mass graves, the desecration. If approached correctly, they could provide:

  • Safe passage through the Galleries
  • Information about the old catacombs
  • Warnings about deeper dangers
  • Possibly even aid against the Church
Weakness: They cannot leave sacred ground. Their power is tied to the burial sites they protect.

---

Related

  • Mareatha the Vein-Mother
  • Monster Ecologies
  • Campaign Sessions

items.title: Grief-Moths
items.content:
Grief-Moths

Type: Aberration (Fey-touched) Domain Association: Eredain the Choirless Monarch Habitat: Floating Archipelago of Elystra, areas of intense emotion, mass mourning sites Challenge Rating: 2 (450 XP)

---

Description

Grief-Moths are ethereal insects drawn to emotional resonance—specifically sorrow. They appear as luminous moths with wingspans of two to three feet, their wings displaying patterns that shift to reflect the grief they've consumed. Where they gather, despair deepens.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A moth with gossamer wings that shimmer between pale blue and bruise-purple. The wing patterns aren't static—they display flickering images: faces of the dead, moments of loss, symbols of mourning that differ for each viewer. The body is translucent, revealing a core of condensed grey light that pulses like a slow heartbeat. Its antennae are unnaturally long, constantly tasting the emotional currents of the air.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Ethereal moth with large gossamer wings showing ghostly faces and mourning symbols in shifting patterns, translucent body with grey pulsing light core, extremely long feathery antennae, wingspan three feet, floating in dim atmosphere with emotional resonance waves visible, pale blue and purple coloring, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Grief-Moths don't cause sorrow—they amplify it. They're drawn to funerals, battlefields, places where people weep. Their presence intensifies existing grief until it becomes debilitating.

Feeding: They consume emotional resonance. A Grief-Moth that feeds on profound loss grows brighter, its wings displaying more detailed imagery. Starving moths are nearly invisible.

Attraction: The moths communicate grief across distances. When one finds a source of sorrow, others arrive within hours. Swarms of hundreds can form around mass tragedies.

Symbiosis: Some Eredain cultists cultivate them, believing concentrated grief creates pure emotional resonance. They're used in rituals to strip away emotional defenses.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Faded Moth (CR 1/2): A starving grief-moth, nearly transparent. Its aura is weak (DC 10) but it attacks more desperately.

Grief Swarm (CR 5): A swarm of grief-moths acting as one (use swarm rules). The combined Grief Aura has DC 15 and deals 2d6 psychic damage to creatures that fail.

Resonant Moth (CR 4): A moth that has fed on a truly profound loss—the death of a child, genocide survivors, etc. Its Wing Display can cause long-term effects (DC 15 Wisdom save or suffer from persistent grief for 1d4 days, functioning as the slow spell emotionally).

---

Ecology

Diet: Pure emotional resonance. They prefer grief but can subsist on any intense negative emotion.

Reproduction: When a grief-moth feeds on enough sorrow, it splits into two smaller moths. Neither is the "original"—both carry fragments of accumulated grief.

Lifespan: Theoretically immortal if fed. Unfed moths fade into nothing within weeks.

Natural Role: Some scholars argue they serve an ecological purpose—processing emotional residue that would otherwise poison an area. Haunted locations often lack grief-moths because they've already been "cleaned."

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A funeral procession is interrupted by a descending cloud of grief-moths—someone must protect the mourners while they complete the rites
  • The party finds a village in catatonic despair, dozens of grief-moths having turned a single death into community-wide paralysis
  • A noble offers payment to harvest grief-moth wings for a ritual—they claim it will help them "let go" of their dead spouse
---

Related

  • Eredain the Choirless Monarch
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"They don't make you sad. They show you what you've already lost. They just make sure you can't look away."

## Description

Grief-Moths are ethereal insects drawn to emotional resonance—specifically sorrow. They appear as luminous moths with wingspans of two to three feet, their wings displaying patterns that shift to reflect the grief they've consumed. Where t...

items.title: Guilt-Eaters
items.content:
Guilt-Eaters

Type: Fiend (Lesser) Domain Association: Imhuran the Regent of Hours Habitat: The Clockwork Sanctum, courtrooms, places of judgment, sites of unpunished crimes Challenge Rating: 2 (450 XP)

---

Description

Imhuran believes all things must be judged. When judgment is delayed, when guilt festers unacknowledged, when crimes go unpunished—the weight of that injustice eventually manifests. Guilt-Eaters are born from the accumulated shame of the unjudged, drawn to those carrying secrets they've never confessed.

They don't punish. They feed. And the feeding is worse than punishment.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A creature the size of a large cat but shaped like nothing natural. Its body is a mass of overlapping scales, each scale inscribed with a tiny crime—lies, thefts, betrayals, murders, all written in languages the viewer somehow understands. It walks on six legs that bend at wrong angles, each ending in a single hooked claw. Its head is a featureless dome of polished bronze, like a judge's gavel made flesh, with a single vertical seam that splits open to reveal a tooth-lined cavity when it feeds. The seam weeps black fluid that smells like old regret.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Cat-sized creature with body of overlapping scales each inscribed with tiny crimes, six legs bending at wrong angles with single hooked claws, featureless polished bronze dome head with vertical seam that splits open revealing tooth-lined cavity, black fluid weeping from seam, walking through gothic courthouse interior with crumbling architecture, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Guilt-Eaters are drawn to unconfessed guilt like sharks to blood. They can sense the weight of unacknowledged wrongs—not the crimes themselves, but the shame the perpetrator carries. The greater the guilt, the more irresistible the scent.

Feeding Method: They don't attack immediately. First, they follow, always at the edge of vision. Their presence intensifies guilt—victims become obsessed with their past wrongs, unable to sleep, snapping at loved ones, seeing accusation in every face. When the guilt becomes unbearable, the Guilt-Eater feeds—literally consuming the shame from its victim.

The Aftermath: Victims who survive being fed upon feel nothing. Not relief—emptiness. The guilt is gone, but so is any sense of wrong. They can remember their crimes but feel no remorse, no connection to the harm they caused. Many become sociopaths. Some become monsters.

Pack Behavior: They travel alone but congregate around sites of mass guilt—prisons, courtrooms, inquisition chambers. Imhuran's priests sometimes use them to "cleanse" prisoners before execution, ensuring they face death without the comfort of repentance.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Shame-Gnat (CR 1/4): A tiny, newly-formed Guilt-Eater that feeds on minor guilts—white lies, petty thefts, unkind words. Annoying rather than dangerous, but swarms of them can drive people to madness. 7 HP, Amplify Shame has 30-foot range.

Verdict Stalker (CR 5): An ancient Guilt-Eater that has consumed hundreds of guilty souls. Its scales are covered in crimes from every era. 68 HP, its Consume Guilt targets all creatures within 10 feet, and it can cast zone of truth at will. Its presence causes guilty creatures to confess compulsively (DC 15 Wisdom save to resist).

The Unjudged (CR 8): A legendary Guilt-Eater spawned from a truly monstrous unpunished crime—genocide, deicide, or worse. It's the size of a horse, and its scales tell a single, horrifying story. It hunts only those guilty of crimes against entire peoples. When it finally feeds, the victim doesn't just lose guilt—they lose all memory of the crime and everyone affected by it.

---

Ecology

Origin: Guilt-Eaters spawn spontaneously in areas where justice has been delayed or denied—courtrooms that rendered false verdicts, prisons holding the wrongly accused, mass graves of unavenged victims. Imhuran doesn't create them, but he considers them a natural consequence of improper judgment.

Diet: Pure guilt. They cannot eat flesh, cannot harm the innocent, and will starve without shame to consume. This makes them strange weapons—they ignore the virtuous entirely.

Relationship to Justice: Imhuran's priests debate whether Guilt-Eaters serve justice or undermine it. Some argue removing guilt is the ultimate punishment—stripping away the capacity for redemption. Others see them as abominations that prevent genuine repentance.

Destruction: Killing a Guilt-Eater releases the accumulated guilt it has consumed in a psychic burst. Every creature within 30 feet experiences a flash of every crime the creature has fed upon—momentary but overwhelming. Creatures must succeed on a DC 13 Wisdom saving throw or be stunned until the end of their next turn.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A party member with a dark secret realizes something is following them—always at the edge of vision, waiting
  • A prison has been "cleansed" by Guilt-Eaters before a mass execution. The prisoners face death calmly, but something is deeply wrong
  • The party must protect a war criminal they despise from a Verdict Stalker—if it feeds, he'll forget his crimes entirely, and with them, the locations of mass graves his victims' families need to find
---

Related

  • Imhuran the Regent of Hours
  • The Clockwork Sanctum
  • Clock-Wraiths
  • Paradox Beasts
---

"I did terrible things in the war. I know I did. I can describe them in detail. But when the creature finished with me... I don't feel them anymore. I remember holding the knife. I don't remember why it should matter."

items.title: Hollow-Born
items.content:
Hollow-Born

Type: Aberration (Conceptual Entity) Domain Association: Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress Habitat: The Devoured Courts, areas of existential crisis, places where meaning has failed Challenge Rating: 7 (2,900 XP)

---

Description

Hollow-Born are beings that emerge when Vor'Kael fails to fully digest a concept. They are half-real entities—partially abstract, partially physical—that embody whatever idea the Empress couldn't consume. Each Hollow-Born represents a different failed digestion: abandoned purposes, unconsummated hungers, questions without answers.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A humanoid figure that seems incomplete—not damaged, but fundamentally unfinished. Parts of its body are simply absent, replaced by visual static or swirling grey void. Its features shift depending on what concept it embodies: a Hollow-Born of "forgotten promises" might have a mouth but no voice, while one of "abandoned hope" might have eyes that show only darkness. They move with purpose but leave no footprints, as if the ground refuses to acknowledge their weight.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Humanoid figure with parts of body replaced by visual static and grey void, incomplete features suggesting unfinished existence, some body parts simply absent or fading into nothingness, face partially formed showing existential incompleteness, standing in dissolving landscape, reality warping around it, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Hollow-Born don't attack in the traditional sense—they complete themselves. They're drawn to people who embody the concept they're missing. A Hollow-Born of "lost love" seeks those in love to consume the feeling. One of "abandoned duty" hunts those with strong obligations.

Consumption Drive: Each Hollow-Born has a conceptual hunger specific to its nature. They can sense when the relevant concept is nearby and will pursue it relentlessly.

Reality Distortion: Their incompleteness affects reality around them. In their presence, related concepts become unstable. Near a Hollow-Born of "broken trust," allies begin doubting each other.

Communication: They speak, but only in fragments of the concept they embody. A Hollow-Born of "forgotten names" speaks only in the names of people who've been forgotten. They cannot understand anything unrelated to their nature.

---

Combat

---

Variants (By Concept)

Hollow-Born of Forgotten Promises (CR 7): Hunts those who've made commitments. Its Conceptual Erosion causes creatures to forget promises they've made.

Hollow-Born of Abandoned Hope (CR 8): Hunts optimists and the faithful. In its presence, healing magic is less effective (healing spells restore only half normal hit points within 60 feet).

Hollow-Born of Lost Love (CR 7): Hunts those in love. Its Concept Drain can permanently sever romantic bonds if the target fails by 5 or more.

Hollow-Born of Broken Purpose (CR 9): Hunts those with clear goals. Its Conceptual Erosion can cause affected creatures to lose their class abilities for 1 round (as they forget why they developed those skills).

Hollow-Born of Starved Hunger (CR 6): Hunts the satisfied. Near it, food provides no nourishment. It represents the hunger Vor'Kael couldn't satisfy.

---

Ecology

Creation: Vor'Kael constantly attempts to consume and understand abstract concepts. When she fails—when a concept is too alien, too pure, or too contradictory—she vomits the half-digested remainder into existence. Hollow-Born are not born; they're excreted.

Existence: They are driven entirely by the need to complete themselves. A Hollow-Born that fully consumes enough of its concept becomes a complete entity and either transcends into pure abstraction or collapses into nothing—scholars debate which.

Relationship to Kael: Kael is technically a type of Hollow-Born—a concept Vor'Kael failed to digest—but one that achieved independent existence without losing itself. This makes Kael both kin to and hunted by other Hollow-Born.

Destruction: A Hollow-Born can be temporarily dispersed through combat, but will reform near its originating concept within 1d4 weeks. To truly destroy one, someone must either fully embody the concept it hungers for (satisfying it) or completely remove that concept from existence.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A Hollow-Born of "broken oaths" stalks Jonas specifically—his divine brands represent promises made to Sovereigns
  • The party discovers a village where no one can remember their loved ones—a Hollow-Born of "forgotten family" feeds nearby
  • Kael recognizes a Hollow-Born as "sibling"—can they communicate? Bargain? Or is conflict inevitable?
---

Related

  • Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress
  • Kael
  • The Devoured Courts
  • Digestors
  • The Misremembered
---

"It wasn't trying to kill me. It was trying to take what I believed in. My faith, my duty, my love—it wanted to eat those and leave me hollow too. Like it was."

items.title: Marrow-Stalkers
items.content:
Marrow-Stalkers

Type: Undead Domain Association: Vortegas the Bone-Warden Habitat: Ossuary Kingdom, bone deserts, crypts, places of mass death Challenge Rating: 4 (1,100 XP)

---

Description

Marrow-Stalkers are predatory undead created when Vortegas's influence saturates a burial ground too thoroughly. The bones become hungry—not for flesh, but for more bone. They hunt the living to steal their skeletons, leaving behind only deflated skins.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A skeletal humanoid that moves wrong—joints bend in directions they shouldn't, the spine undulates like a serpent, fingers extend with too many knuckles. Its bones are polished white and seamless, as if carved rather than grown. Where a skull should have eyes, there are only pits that drink in light. When it moves, you can hear the grinding of bone on bone, like stones rubbing together. The jaw unhinges far wider than any human's could.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Skeletal humanoid with wrong proportions, joints bending in impossible directions, serpentine undulating spine, fingers with too many knuckles, polished white seamless bones, empty eye sockets that absorb light, unhinging jaw opened impossibly wide, predatory pose, moving through darkness, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Marrow-Stalkers are patient hunters. They can remain motionless for years, indistinguishable from ordinary skeletons, until living prey enters their territory. Then they strike.

Hunting Method: They don't kill—they extract. A Marrow-Stalker pins its victim and uses specialized bone growths to crack open skin and muscle, then systematically pulls the skeleton free. This process takes about ten minutes and is invariably fatal, but victims remain conscious for most of it.

Incorporation: Stolen bones are added to the Stalker's body. Old Marrow-Stalkers have dozens of excess arms, legs, and skulls fused to their frames.

Pack Behavior: Though usually solitary, Marrow-Stalkers will cooperate to bring down larger prey. They communicate through bone-clicks inaudible to most creatures.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Juvenile Stalker (CR 2): A recently animated Stalker with only its original skeleton. No Bone Rattle ability, and Bone Extraction deals only 2d8+3 damage.

Elder Stalker (CR 7): A Marrow-Stalker that has incorporated dozens of skeletons. It has 120 HP, +7 to attacks, and its Bone Rattle has a 30-foot radius with DC 15. It can make three claw attacks due to extra arms.

Stalker Amalgam (CR 10): Multiple Stalkers that have fused into a single massive entity the size of an elephant. Uses the statistics of a bone devil but with the Marrow-Stalker's abilities. Nightmare fuel.

---

Ecology

Creation: Marrow-Stalkers arise spontaneously in areas saturated with Vortegas's influence—ossuaries, catacombs, mass graves. They are not created deliberately; they simply emerge when enough bone accumulates in one place.

Diet: They don't need to consume bones to survive—they want to. Their drive is compulsive collection, not sustenance.

Reproduction: A Marrow-Stalker that incorporates enough bones eventually splits, calving off a new Stalker like an amoeba dividing. The "offspring" has no memory of its parent.

Territory: They claim burial sites and defend them against all intruders. Some scholars theorize they're protecting the bones, not hoarding them.

Weaknesses: Bludgeoning weapons are most effective—shattering their bones beyond repair. They're also vulnerable to turn undead effects, though powerful ones may resist.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The party seeks shelter in an ancient crypt, not realizing the scattered bones around them are a waiting Marrow-Stalker
  • A noble's ancestral tomb has been "cleaned out"—only deflated skin-bags remain of the honored dead
  • Vortegas's cultists use Marrow-Stalkers as guardians, feeding them prisoners to keep them sated
---

Related

  • Vortegas the Bone-Warden
  • Bone-Weavers
  • The Ossuary Kingdom
  • Ossathar
---

"We found her in the morning. Just... skin. Like someone had taken off a glove. Whatever did it took its time."

items.title: Monster Ecologies
items.content:
Monster Ecologies

The death of Aedwynn warped the natural world, creating ecosystems where anatomy and apocalypse intersect. Creatures adapt to Sovereign influence, regional anomalies, and the remnants of divine tissue scattered across the land.

---

MAJOR REGIONAL ECOLOGIES

1. Pallid Plains — The Dimming Fields

Location: Northern Ossathar Environment: A region where light drains color and predators stalk silence

The Dimming Fields are vast, colorless grasslands where sunlight arrives drained of warmth and pigment. Sound is absorbed by the ground, creating an oppressive quiet.

Apex Predators:

  • Pale-Stags: Majestic deer-like creatures with antlers made of frozen breath. They exhale clouds of numbing cold and feed on the warmth of living things. Their hooves make no sound.
  • Gallery Hounds: Skinless quadrupeds that track prey by scent alone. Their exposed musculature ripples as they run, and they hunt in coordinated silence. Seeing them is deeply unsettling—watching anatomy in motion.
  • Hollow Shepherds: Headless torso-walkers that lumber on leg-like appendages. They're drawn to sound and will follow any noise for miles. When they catch prey, they absorb it into their hollow interior.
Lesser Creatures:
  • Colorless moths that feed on pigment
  • Silent grass that wraps around ankles
  • Mist that steals voice
Survival Tips:
  • Move slowly
  • Avoid making sound
  • Wear gray or white clothing
  • Never whistle
---

2. The Floating Archipelago of Elystra

Location: Sky-bound islands drifting in resonance currents Environment: Massive landmasses suspended by Eredain the Choirless Monarch's gravitational distortions

Megafauna:

  • Cloud Eels: Serpentine creatures hundreds of feet long that swim through air currents. They filter-feed on floating plankton and pose little threat unless provoked. Their organs contain levitation bladders harvested by Sky-Leviathan Poachers.
  • Sky-Reef Leviathans: Continent-sized flying creatures whose backs are covered in entire ecosystems. Villages exist on their spines. They move slowly, feeding on atmospheric resonance.
  • Feathered Gargants: Bird-like titans with metallic feathers that chime when they fly. Their songs can be heard from miles away. Highly territorial but majestic.
Lesser Creatures:
  • Gravity-minnows (small floating fish)
  • Wind-spiders (create webs between floating rocks)
  • Echo-birds (repeat sounds they hear)
Hazards:
  • Sudden gravity shifts
  • Falling between islands
  • Predatory wind currents
---

3. The Inverted Forests of Eredain

Location: Scattered gravity-anomaly zones Environment: Trees root into the sky; gravity collapses upward

These forests grow inverted, with roots reaching toward clouds and branches buried in soil. "Down" is relative, making navigation disorienting.

Predators:

  • Upside Stalkers: Humanoid predators that walk on ceilings and walls as if they were floors. They drop onto prey from "above" (which might be below).
  • Gravity Bats: Massive chiropterans that manipulate local gravity with echolocation. They can reverse gravity in small areas, causing prey to "fall" into their waiting jaws.
  • Song-Bound Treants: Trees given consciousness by Eredain's resonance. They "walk" by shifting their gravitational anchor. They're curious but dangerous—curious enough to crush travelers while examining them.
Lesser Creatures:
  • Inverted squirrels
  • Gravity-shifted insects
  • Floating seeds that root mid-air
Survival Tips:
  • Tie yourself to something stable
  • Don't trust your inner ear
  • Follow the flow of water (it still obeys its own gravity)
---

4. The Glassdream Ocean (Nhalyra)

Location: The Azure Depths Environment: A liquid-crystal sea that stores memories of everything drowned within

The ocean is partially solid, partially liquid, and always conscious. Swimming through it means swimming through thoughts.

Creatures:

  • Soul-Kelp: Underwater forests made from drowned consciousness. They tangle around swimmers, absorbing memories. Harvested for memory-storage.
  • Dream-Fins: Fish that swim through emotional currents rather than water. They're translucent and show swirling patterns of color. Eating one causes vivid dreams.
  • Reflective Sirens: Aquatic humanoids whose songs are actually stolen memories played back. They lure travelers with echoes of lost loved ones. Not malicious—simply repeating what they've absorbed.
Lesser Creatures:
  • Memory-shrimp (store single thoughts)
  • Glass-jellies (transparent and nearly invisible)
  • Thought-currents (living streams of consciousness)
Hazards:
  • Losing sense of self
  • Drowning in someone else's memories
  • Psychic whirlpools
---

5. The Ossuary Badlands (Vortegas)

Location: Ossathar Environment: Bone deserts filled with marrow-storms

Endless dunes of powdered bone stretch beneath a white sky. Storms of ossified dust whisper the memories of the dead.

Creatures:

  • Ribworms: Massive segmented creatures that burrow through bone-sand. Each segment is a fused ribcage. They sense vibration and erupt beneath prey.
  • Spine Serpents: Made from articulated vertebrae, these serpents undulate across dunes with unsettling fluidity. Their bite injects marrow-poison that crystallizes bones from within.
  • Ossuary Golems: Constructs assembled from thousands of bones, held together by Vortegas the Bone-Warden's will. They patrol ruins and attack those who disturb the dead.
Lesser Creatures:
  • Marrow-wisps (glowing spirits)
  • Bone-scarabs (feed on calcium)
  • Skull-crabs (hermit crabs using skulls as shells)
Hazards:
  • Marrowstorms (bone-dust storms)
  • Quicksand made of powdered skeleton
  • Haunted bones that remember their deaths
---

6. The Blooming Rot (Talaris)

Location: Talar'Noth Environment: A biome where fungus and necrosis coexist beautifully

The jungle is alive with death. Corpses bloom into gardens. Decay feeds new growth. Everything smells of earth and sweetness.

Creatures:

  • Blossom Fiends: Humanoid shapes formed from decomposing flesh, covered in beautiful flowers. They spread spores that cause euphoric rot. Victims die smiling, becoming gardens.
  • Fungal Ogres: Giants whose bodies are 90% mushroom. They lumber through the jungle, scattering spores with each step. Not aggressive unless threatened, but their touch causes instant fungal growth.
  • Sproutborn Spirits: Ghosts of those who died and "bloomed" into new life. They're partially tangible, partially plant. They remember fragments of their past lives and often help travelers—or lead them into beautiful death.
Lesser Creatures:
  • Rot-butterflies (feed on decomposition)
  • Mycelium mats (sentient fungal networks)
  • Corpse-fruit trees
Hazards:
  • Spore clouds causing hallucinations
  • Ground collapsing into compost pits
  • "Healing rot" that preserves corpses
---

7. The Devoured Courts (Vor'Kael)

Location: Vor'Khalem Environment: Digestive landscapes where meaning decays

Acid marshes, bile rivers, and fields where purpose dissolves. Reality here is soft, interpretable, and hungry.

Creatures:

  • Hunger Wraiths: Vaporous entities that embody raw need. They don't eat flesh—they consume meaning, leaving victims feeling hollow and purposeless.
  • Digestive Idols: Statue-like beings made from concepts Vor'Kael attempted to digest. They're partially real, partially abstract. Attacking them requires philosophical arguments as much as weapons.
  • Abstract Leeches: Parasites that feed on understanding. After feeding, victims forget how to do things they once knew—reading, walking, speaking.
Lesser Creatures:
  • Metaphor-flies (buzz with fragmented ideas)
  • Bile-slugs (leave trails of dissolved meaning)
  • Hunger-moss (grows on abandoned purposes)
Hazards:
  • Existential nausea
  • Ideas losing coherence mid-thought
  • Landscapes shifting based on perception
---

MID-TIER ECOLOGIES

8. Mirror Marshes

Swamps where reflections attack before their originals. Creatures include mirror-doubles, reflection-predators, and surface-walkers.

9. Valve-Lakes

Bodies of water with heartbeat tidal cycles. Home to pulse-fish, rhythm-crabs, and synchronized predators.

10. Soulflow Channels

Rivers of liquid memory. Creatures: memory-eels, thought-currents, and consciousness-parasites.

11. The Crimson Orchards

Trees fed by arterial soil, producing blood-fruit. Creatures: vein-birds, crimson apes, and sap-vampires.

12. The Sandglass Wastes

Dunes where time skips forward or backward. Creatures: temporal-scorpions, frozen predators, and future-echoes.

---

MINOR ANOMALIES

13. The Still-Light Pillars

Columns of light frozen mid-air. Touching them causes temporary blindness.

14. Whisper Graves

Graveyards that echo the last sounds of the dead. Approached by mourners and necromancers alike.

15. Spore-Mist Hollows

Fog-filled valleys where hallucination spores cause vivid visions.

16. Vein-Worm Burrows

Living tunnels that pulse and contract. Used for fast travel by those brave enough.

17. Boundary Tears

Rogue mirror-portals that open randomly. Lead to The Blackfold or stranger places.

---

Creature Categories by Threat Level

Deadly (Avoid):

  • Sky-Reef Leviathans
  • Ossuary Golems
  • Blossom Fiends
  • Hunger Wraiths
Dangerous (Fight With Caution):
  • Pale-Stags
  • Gallery Hounds
  • Ribworms
  • Fungal Ogres
Neutral (Avoid Provoking):
  • Cloud Eels
  • Soul-Kelp
  • Sproutborn Spirits
  • Song-Bound Treants
Beneficial (Use Carefully):
  • Dream-Fins (edible, cause dreams)
  • Memory-shrimp (store information)
  • Corpse-fruit (nutritious but unsettling)
---

Related

  • Locations & Realms
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Criminal Underworld

items.title: Nerve Shepherds
items.content:
Nerve Shepherds

Type: Aberration Domain Association: None (Aedwynn's remains) Habitat: Anywhere neural tissue remains—Cerebrax, Arterial Deepways, ancient ruins Challenge Rating: 12 (8,400 XP)

---

Description

Aedwynn's nervous system didn't die with the rest of it. Divine nerves don't decay—they just stop receiving signals. But after millennia of silence, some nerve clusters have begun generating their own impulses, desperate to fulfill their original purpose: carrying messages through a god's body.

Nerve Shepherds are ambulatory clusters of divine neural tissue, still trying to deliver signals that no longer have a destination. They intercept thoughts, memories, and commands from any mind they encounter, absorbing them into their endless, purposeless relay.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A mass of pale, glistening fibers roughly twelve feet across, pulsing with bioluminescent light that travels along its strands in waves. It moves by extending and retracting tendrils, pulling itself across surfaces like a massive neural jellyfish. Thousands of thin filaments trail behind it, occasionally sparking with captured thoughts—brief images, words, sensations flickering along their length. At its core, a dense knot of tissue throbs rhythmically, and those who look too long swear they can see faces forming and dissolving in its depths: everyone whose thoughts it has ever carried.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Twelve-foot mass of pale glistening neural fibers pulsing with bioluminescent waves of light, moving by extending and retracting tendrils like neural jellyfish, thousands of trailing filaments sparking with captured thoughts showing brief images and words, dense throbbing core with faces forming and dissolving within, moving through ancient divine architecture, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, bio-luminescent lighting, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Nerve Shepherds are not intelligent in any conventional sense. They're reflexes—automatic processes still executing without oversight. Their behavior follows simple rules burned into divine tissue billions of years ago.

Signal Interception: They detect thoughts within range and compulsively absorb them into their network. This isn't malicious; it's their purpose. They were meant to carry signals. Now they carry whatever they can find.

Message Delivery: Periodically, a Shepherd will attempt to "deliver" accumulated signals—discharging stored thoughts into the nearest receptive mind. This is overwhelming at best, fatal at worst. Centuries of intercepted thoughts, memories, and dying screams, all at once.

The Eternal Route: Shepherds follow paths. Not roads—neural pathways burned into the world's anatomy. They trace the routes they once carried signals along, even though those routes now cross oceans, mountains, and Sovereign domains. Nothing deters them from their route.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Frayed Tendril (CR 5): A small fragment of a Nerve Shepherd, perhaps thirty filaments operating semi-independently. 68 HP, no Signal Discharge, and its Thought Absorption only works within 30 feet. Common in areas where Shepherds have been damaged.

Ganglion Nexus (CR 16): A Nerve Shepherd that has absorbed so many thoughts it's achieved a kind of gestalt consciousness—not truly intelligent, but able to form sentences from stolen words, make predictions from absorbed memories, and even bargain using knowledge it has intercepted. 250 HP, can cast detect thoughts at will, and its Signal Discharge can target a single creature to deliver specific absorbed memories (potentially including secrets, passwords, or hidden knowledge).

The Spinal Procession (CR 20): A legendary Nerve Shepherd that follows the entire length of Aedwynn's fossilized spine—a creature miles long, mostly dormant, but devastating when disturbed. Uses the statistics of a purple worm combined with a Nerve Shepherd, plus lair actions that cause earthquakes and mass psychic damage along the spine's length.

---

Ecology

Origin: Nerve Shepherds are what remains of Aedwynn's peripheral nervous system. When the god died, signals stopped flowing, but the nerves themselves remained functional—just purposeless. Over millennia, some clusters developed enough autonomous activity to move, to seek, to continue their endless work.

Diet: They don't need sustenance. They're powered by the residual divine energy in their tissue. However, absorbing thoughts seems to sustain their activity—a Shepherd that goes too long without intercepting signals becomes dormant.

The Routes: Each Shepherd follows a specific neural pathway—a route it was designed to carry signals along. These routes are burned into the world's geography. A Shepherd's path might cross through a mountain, under an ocean, directly through a city. It will not deviate. It cannot.

Harvesting: Nerve Shepherd tissue is incredibly valuable. Alchemists use it for telepathic potions. Artificers weave it into communication devices. Ismara's scholars study it for insights into divine cognition. But harvesting is dangerous—damaged Shepherds discharge violently.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A Nerve Shepherd's route passes directly through a major city every few decades. It's due to arrive in three days. The city needs to either divert it (impossible?), endure it (devastating), or destroy it (the discharge alone could kill thousands).
  • A Shepherd has absorbed a critical piece of information—the location of a hidden artifact, a Sovereign's secret, a loved one's last words. The party must find a way to extract that specific signal without triggering a full discharge.
  • Two enemy factions are both hunting the same Shepherd. Each believes it carries evidence of the other's crimes. The party is caught in the middle.
---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • Cerebrax
  • Calcified Thoughts
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"It wasn't thinking at us. It was thinking through us. Every mind it had ever touched, every thought it had ever carried—for a moment, I was all of them. Thousands of years of prayers, confessions, dying words, love letters. All trying to reach someone who died before my ancestors were born."
items.raw:

**Challenge Rating:** 12 (8,400 XP)

---

## Description

Aedwynn's nervous system didn't die with the rest of it. Divine nerves don't decay—they just stop receiving signals. But after millennia of silence, some nerve clusters have begun generating their own impulses, desperate to fulfill their original purpose: carr...

items.title: Paradox Beasts
items.content:
Paradox Beasts

Type: Aberration (Cosmic) Domain Association: Karthayne the Hollow Crown Habitat: The Absent Throne, reality tears, places where contradictions manifest Challenge Rating: 9 (5,000 XP)

---

Description

Paradox Beasts are living contradictions—creatures that exist and don't exist simultaneously. They emerge from Karthayne's domain, where the Heart of Creation and Destruction coexist, spawned from impossible concepts given flesh: the weight of nothing, the color of silence, the shape of unasked questions.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
Describing a Paradox Beast causes headaches because it actively resists description. It is simultaneously present and absent—you see a beast and an empty space occupying the same location. It has a form that shifts between states: solid and smoke, animal and geometric, singular and multiple. Observers disagree on basic facts about it (how many legs, what color, how large) because all observations are simultaneously true. The only constant: looking at it feels wrong, like reading a sentence that contradicts itself.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Impossible creature that seems to exist and not exist at once, form shifting between solid flesh and empty space, multiple contradictory shapes overlapping, animal features and geometric forms simultaneously, glitching reality effect around it, observers would see different things, visual paradox made flesh, standing in fractured reality landscape, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Paradox Beasts don't think in ways that can be understood—their minds are as contradictory as their bodies. They may attack and flee, hunt and hide, exist and vanish, all simultaneously.

Reality Interference: Their presence destabilizes local reality. Cause and effect become unreliable. Objects may fall up, words may precede speech, attacks may heal.

Impossible Hunger: They consume contradictions—things that both are and aren't. A promise that was kept and broken. A door that is open and closed. A person who is alive and dead.

Communication: Some scholars believe Paradox Beasts are intelligent and attempting communication, but their messages are always self-contradicting. "I am not here" spoken while clearly present. "This is false" which is true only if false.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Minor Paradox (CR 4): A smaller, weaker paradox—perhaps the contradiction of a lie told truthfully or a gift given selfishly. No Reality Fracture Aura, simplified Paradoxical Existence (50% chance of being targetable each round).

Concept Paradox (CR 12): A paradox beast that has consumed a major contradiction, like "the war that was won and lost" or "the king who ruled but never reigned." It can cast confusion at will and its Contradiction affects all creatures in a 30-foot radius.

Karthayne's Emissary (CR 15): A paradox beast that has been claimed by the Hollow Crown. It is both her servant and not—following orders while disobeying them. It can create localized antimagic fields that also enhance magic (spells in the field either fail or are maximized, 50% chance).

The Unborn (CR 11): A paradox beast that represents a creature that should have existed but didn't. It has the memories and abilities of a being that was never born. It desperately wants to "become real" and will try to replace a living creature by consuming their existence.

---

Ecology

Creation: Paradox Beasts emerge spontaneously when contradictions become too heavy for reality to contain—broken oaths that were kept, deaths that were survived, creations that were unmade. Karthayne may also deliberately create them.

Existence: They don't experience time or space normally. A Paradox Beast may attack you yesterday and tomorrow but not today. Tracking them is nearly impossible.

Destruction: They can't truly be killed—only "resolved." Forcing a Paradox Beast into a single consistent state (through magic, logic, or sheer willpower) causes it to collapse into reality and cease existing.

Danger: The longer they exist, the more reality warps around them. Areas long inhabited by Paradox Beasts become logic-free zones where anything can happen.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A wizard's experiment created a localized paradox—a door that is both open and closed—and the Paradox Beast that emerged from it now guards the tower
  • Karthayne sends a Paradox Beast to deliver a message: the message is both an offer and a threat, both true and false
  • A Paradox Beast claims to be a future version of one of the party members—and also their past self—and wants to "rejoin" them
---

Related

  • Karthayne the Hollow Crown
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"It asked me for directions to the place it had already been. It thanked me for the help I refused to give. It left before it arrived. And somehow, I understood all of it, and none of it, perfectly."

items.title: Reflex Beasts
items.content:
Reflex Beasts

Type: Monstrosity (Divine Remnant) Domain Association: None (Aedwynn's autonomic systems) Habitat: Anywhere on the Maker's corpse, especially near nerve tissue or joints Challenge Rating: 10 (5,900 XP)

---

Description

Even a dead body retains some automatic functions for a time. Muscles twitch. Nerves fire. Reflexes persist long after conscious control has ended.

Aedwynn's body has been dead for millennia, but it was a god's body. Some reflexes never stopped. They accumulated, condensed, and eventually achieved a kind of independent existence. Reflex Beasts are automatic responses that forgot they needed a stimulus—predatory functions executing endlessly, hunting motions without a hunt, defensive reactions without a threat.

They are not intelligent. They are not even instinctive. They are mechanical—muscle memory given form, attack patterns without purpose, the ghost of a god's fight-or-flight response made flesh.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A nightmare of anatomy in motion. Imagine a creature assembled from twitching muscle fibers, exposed tendons, and raw nerve clusters, roughly humanoid but constantly shifting as different reflex patterns compete for dominance. One moment it crouches like a predator preparing to spring; the next, it raises limbs defensively as if warding off an unseen blow; the next, it makes gripping motions in the air, catching prey that isn't there. Its "face" is simply a concentration of sensory nerves, flickering with electrical activity, reacting to stimuli faster than any conscious creature could. It makes no sound except the wet snap of tendons and the crackle of nerve-fire.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Creature assembled from twitching muscle fibers exposed tendons and raw nerve clusters, roughly humanoid but constantly shifting between predator crouch and defensive posture and gripping motions, face of concentrated sensory nerves flickering with electrical activity, constant motion as different reflex patterns compete, wet organic surfaces with visible muscle striations and nerve bundles, no skin only raw anatomy in motion, dark fantasy concept art, anatomical horror, inspired by medical illustration made nightmare, digital painting, detailed textures, primal violence atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Reflex Beasts don't think. They execute. They're running ancient programs burned into divine tissue, responding to triggers that may or may not actually be present.

Trigger Response: Different stimuli activate different reflex patterns. Sudden movement triggers pursuit. Loud sounds trigger defensive postures. The presence of blood triggers... something predatory and terrible. A Reflex Beast in a quiet, empty room might stand motionless for years. A Reflex Beast in a combat zone is a whirlwind of lethal automatic responses.

Pattern Cycling: They cycle through reflex patterns semi-randomly, shifting from predator to defender to something unclassifiable. This makes them unpredictable—you never know which response you'll trigger next.

No Self-Preservation: They have no survival instinct because survival instinct is a conscious function. Reflex Beasts will attack until destroyed, retreat only if "flee" reflexes are triggered, and never prioritize their own safety. This makes them terrifyingly committed.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Minor Reflex (CR 4): A smaller accumulation of reflex tissue, perhaps the remnant of a specific automatic function. 60 HP, Medium size, no Reflex Pattern (stuck in one pattern permanently), no Nerve Strike. Dangerous but predictable.

Compound Reflex (CR 14): A massive Reflex Beast incorporating multiple reflex systems that have merged. 230 HP, Huge size, rolls 1d6 for Reflex Pattern (add two new patterns: "5 - Grapple: can grapple two creatures simultaneously with advantage" and "6 - All: applies all patterns simultaneously for one round"), and its Nerve Strike recharges on 4-6.

The First Flinch (CR 18): The original reflex—Aedwynn's first automatic response to the pain of the Usurpation. It carries the memory of divine agony, and its attacks inflict psychic damage from shared trauma. 300 HP, its attacks deal an additional 2d10 psychic damage, and any creature it damages must save against confusion (DC 18) as they experience flashes of being murdered by the Thirteen.

---

Ecology

Origin: Reflex Beasts coalesce from concentrated autonomic activity in Aedwynn's tissues. They form near major nerve clusters, joint articulation points, and especially near the spine. The Ossathar region has the highest concentration.

Behavior Patterns: Without stimuli, they're dormant. Travelers who learn to move silently, avoid bleeding, and minimize vibration can sometimes pass through Reflex Beast territory unnoticed. Combat in their territory, however, is almost guaranteed to attract them.

Reproduction: They don't reproduce—they accumulate. A Reflex Beast grows larger as more automatic responses join its mass. Very old Reflex Beasts are terrifyingly large and complex, cycling through dozens of different reflex patterns.

Exploitation: Some cultures have learned to use Reflex Beasts as guardians. If you control the stimuli in an area, you control the Beast's behavior. Elaborate trap systems can channel a Reflex Beast's violence toward intruders while leaving residents safe—as long as no one makes a wrong move.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The party must cross territory infested with dormant Reflex Beasts. Any combat, loud noise, or sudden movement could wake them. This becomes a tense stealth sequence.
  • A Reflex Beast has wandered into a city and is responding to the constant stimuli with constant violence. The party must lure it out without triggering a massacre.
  • Someone has learned to control Reflex Beasts using specific patterns of stimuli. They're using them as assassins, and the beasts leave no evidence of intelligent direction.
---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • Nerve Shepherds
  • Ossathar
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"It didn't decide to kill us. It didn't decide anything. We moved, and it moved. We attacked, and it attacked. We ran, and it chased. It was like fighting a machine made of meat—no malice, no fear, no hesitation. Just response after response after response, until we stopped moving and it stopped caring."

items.title: Scab-Hounds
items.content:
Scab-Hounds

Type: Monstrosity Domain Association: Mareatha the Vein-Mother Habitat: Borders of the Sangreal Dominion, abandoned battlefields, sites of mass bloodshed Challenge Rating: 1 (200 XP)

---

Description

Scab-Hounds are the inevitable consequence of divine blood spilling onto mortal soil. When enough of Mareatha's essence seeps into the earth—through arterial rivers, battlefield slaughter, or cathedral runoff—the ground itself clots. What rises is hungry, loyal to nothing, and built entirely for pursuit.

They are not undead. They are not constructs. They are coagulated instinct given form.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A quadruped the size of a large dog, but wrong in every proportion. Its body appears formed from layers of dried blood and scab tissue—deep crimson-brown, cracked like drought-stricken earth, weeping fresh red from the fissures when it moves. It has no eyes, just smooth scab-plating across its skull, but its nostrils are enormous, constantly flaring. Its legs are too long, joints bending backward like a hare's, and its claws are crystallized blood—sharp as obsidian. When it opens its mouth, there are no teeth—just a lamprey-like spiral of hardened platelets that spin inward.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Quadruped creature made of layered dried blood and scab tissue, crimson-brown cracked surface weeping fresh blood from fissures, eyeless head with smooth scab-plating and enormous flaring nostrils, backward-bending hare-like legs, crystallized blood claws sharp as obsidian, lamprey-like spiral mouth of hardened platelets, hunting posture in blood-red grasslands, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Scab-Hounds hunt by scent—specifically, they track the iron signature of blood. Fresh wounds drive them into frenzy. They can smell an open cut from two miles away and will pursue relentlessly until they feed or are destroyed.

Pack Hunters: They always form in groups of 3-7, emerging from the same blood-saturated soil. A pack shares a kind of rudimentary hive-sense—when one scents prey, all know.

Feeding: They don't eat flesh. They lap blood directly from wounds, their spiral mouths creating a suction seal. Victims report a disturbing numbness as they feed—the scab-matter contains natural coagulants that prevent the wound from bleeding out. They want you alive. They want to feed again tomorrow.

Territorial Marking: They urinate crystallized blood-stones to mark their territory. These stones hum faintly and will attract other Scab-Hounds if not removed.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Weeping Pup (CR 1/4): A newly-formed Scab-Hound, small and weak. Only 11 HP, no Blood Lap ability. Often ignored by adventurers—a mistake, as where there's one, there are always more forming.

Clotfather (CR 3): An ancient Scab-Hound that has fed for decades. Its body is massive and armored with crystallized blood. 45 HP, AC 15, and its bite deals an additional 1d6 necrotic damage. It can command nearby Scab-Hounds telepathically.

Arterial Stalker (CR 5): A Scab-Hound that has drunk directly from an arterial river. It moves through blood like water, can meld into pools of blood, and regenerates 5 HP per round while in contact with blood.

---

Ecology

Formation: Scab-Hounds emerge from any soil saturated with enough blood over time. Battlefields, execution grounds, slaughterhouses—anywhere death bleeds into earth. In Mareatha's domain, they're common as wolves. Elsewhere, they mark sites of historical atrocity.

Lifecycle: They don't reproduce. They simply form from blood-soil when conditions are right. A particularly bloody battle might spawn dozens over the following weeks.

Relationship to Mareatha: The Vein-Mother doesn't control them, but she permits them. They serve as a kind of immune response—attacking those whose blood hasn't been sanctified by the Church. Mareathan faithful can walk among them safely; their blood smells "wrong" to the hounds.

Destruction: Fire destroys them permanently. Otherwise, their bodies simply re-coagulate over 1d4 days unless the remains are scattered or purified.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A merchant caravan is found dead on the road—every body pale and drained, the earth around them crimson and soft. The pack will return tonight to check for more.
  • The party must cross an old battlefield where Scab-Hounds roam freely. Any wound sustained attracts the pack.
  • A Mareathan priest uses trained Scab-Hounds to hunt heretics—they can scent "unsanctified" blood.
---

Related

  • Mareatha the Vein-Mother
  • Blood-Drinkers
  • Vein-Crawlers
  • Sangreal Dominion
---

"We bandaged the cut immediately. Thought we were clever. Then we heard them howling—they'd already tasted the air."

items.title: Silence-Ticks
items.content:
Silence-Ticks

Type: Aberration (Vermin) Domain Association: Azerach the Whispered King Habitat: Dominion of Still Air, areas of enforced silence, near Azerach's followers Challenge Rating: 1/4 (50 XP)

---

Description

Silence-Ticks are thumb-sized parasites native to Azerach's domain. They appear as pale, fleshy ticks with tiny malformed ears covering their bodies instead of eyes. Where they feed, sound dies.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A bulbous, cream-colored tick roughly two inches across. Its body is covered in dozens of tiny ears of varying sizes—human, animal, and shapes that don't correspond to any known creature. It has no visible eyes or mouth. When it feeds, its body pulses silently, growing more translucent until you can see the stolen sounds churning inside as grey vapor.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Fleshy pale tick covered in tiny malformed ears instead of eyes, translucent body showing grey vapor inside, no mouth or eyes visible, sitting on stone surface, disturbing body horror creature, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Silence-Ticks don't drink blood—they consume sound. They attach to victims and create zones of absolute silence around them. The tick grows fat on stolen noise: conversations, screams, footsteps, even heartbeats.

Feeding: A single tick can drain all sound within a 10-foot radius. Victims often don't notice until they try to speak and hear nothing.

Colony Behavior: Silence-Ticks cluster together. A large enough colony can silence an entire building. Azerach's temples are infested with them intentionally.

Communication: Ticks communicate through vibration patterns undetectable to most creatures. They coordinate feeding and warn each other of threats through the ground.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Gorged Tick (CR 1/2): A tick that has fed for weeks. Its silence zone extends to 30 feet. When killed, the sound release deals 2d6 thunder damage.

Queen Tick (CR 2): Massive breeding females the size of a dog. Their silence zone extends 60 feet and they can birth 2d4 ticks per day. Killing one releases a cacophony that stuns creatures within 30 feet (DC 14 Con save or stunned for 1 round).

---

Ecology

Diet: Sound. Starving ticks become desperate and swarm anything making noise.

Reproduction: Queens lay eggs in absolute silence. The eggs absorb ambient sound to gestate—a reason Azerach's followers deliberately speak near tick nests.

Natural Enemies: Creatures from Eredain's domain hunt them—the Choirless Monarch's servants despise anything that consumes sound.

Habitat: Anywhere Azerach's influence touches. Wild populations exist in caves and ruins where silence is natural.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • Party enters an area and realizes they can't hear each other speak—ticks are attached to their gear
  • A village is slowly going silent as a tick colony spreads through the walls
  • Azerach's cultists use tick-infested cloaks to move without sound
---

Related

  • Azerach the Whispered King
  • Monster Ecologies
  • Sibilants
---

"The worst part isn't the silence. It's when you kill one and hear everything it ate—every scream, every plea, every prayer—all at once."

items.title: Skin-Walkers
items.content:
Skin-Walkers

Type: Aberration (Shapechanger) Domain Association: Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd Habitat: The Blackfold, mirrors, shadowed places, anywhere identity can be stolen Challenge Rating: 8 (3,900 XP)

---

Description

Skin-Walkers are servants of Ultharion who have transcended their original forms entirely. They no longer possess a true appearance—only the stolen identities of their victims. They exist as living voids that wear other people's skin, faces, and memories like clothing.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
In their "true" form (rarely seen), a Skin-Walker is a void in the shape of a person—absolute darkness given humanoid outline, with no features except a faint shimmer where a face should be. When wearing a stolen skin, they appear perfectly identical to their victim, down to scars, mannerisms, and voice. The only tell: their reflection is always delayed by a fraction of a second, as if the mirror needs time to process what it's seeing.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Humanoid figure of pure void and darkness in the shape of a person, no features visible except faint shimmer where face should be, standing next to a mirror showing a delayed reflection of a normal human, tendrils of shadow peeling skin from a body at its feet, absolute darkness against dim background, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Skin-Walkers don't simply shapeshift—they steal. When they take a form, they remove it from the original owner, leaving behind a featureless, identity-less husk.

Identity Theft: The process requires physical contact and takes about one minute. The victim must be helpless or willing. The Skin-Walker gains their appearance, voice, some surface memories, and personality traits. The victim loses all of these—becoming a blank, faceless creature that eventually dies of existential dissolution.

Impersonation: A Skin-Walker wearing stolen identity is nearly impossible to detect through normal means. They have the victim's mannerisms, knowledge of common facts, and even emotional responses. Only prolonged interaction, specific questions about obscure memories, or magical detection reveals them.

Collection: Most Skin-Walkers maintain multiple stolen identities, switching between them as needed. Some collect skins obsessively, building vast wardrobes of stolen selves.

---

Combat

---

Related

  • Ultharion the Obsidian Shepherd
  • The Blackfold
---

"It wore my brother's face. But when I looked in the mirror behind it, I saw only darkness."
