items.title: Suture Pilgrims
items.content:
The Suture Pilgrims

Type: Celestial (Fallen) Domain Association: None (pre-Usurpation origin) Habitat: Domain boundaries, sites where reality tears, the spaces between Sovereign influence Challenge Rating: 8 (3,900 XP)

---

Description

Before the Usurpation, Aedwynn's body was whole. When the Thirteen carved it apart, they created wounds—not physical cuts, but conceptual tears where things that should be connected were violently separated. The borders between Sovereign domains are scar tissue, but they never healed properly.

The Suture Pilgrims are what remains of the Maker's immune response. They were once the entities that maintained Aedwynn's wholeness—cells of a divine body, tasked with repair. When the god died, they didn't. They wander still, stitching. Sewing. Trying to close wounds in a corpse that will never heal.

They are not hostile. They are not kind. They simply repair, and they don't distinguish between the wound and what's grown in it.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A tall, gaunt humanoid figure—perhaps eight feet—wrapped entirely in strips of pale tissue that might be skin, might be bandage, might be something between. The wrappings constantly shift, revealing glimpses of what's beneath: not flesh, but layered structures of white and red, like anatomy diagrams come to life. Each hand ends in three elongated fingers, each finger tipped with a needle of bone. A single thread trails from its chest—luminous, slightly pink, impossibly long, never tangling. Where it walks, it leaves no footprints, but the ground behind it seems smoother, as if old wounds in the earth have been closed. Its face is a smooth expanse of wrapped tissue, but when it "speaks," the wrappings part to reveal nothing but light.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Eight-foot tall gaunt humanoid wrapped in shifting strips of pale tissue between skin and bandage, glimpses beneath showing layered anatomical structures of white and red, three elongated fingers per hand ending in bone needles, luminous pink thread trailing from chest never tangling, face of smooth wrapped tissue parting to reveal pure light, walking along boundary between two different landscapes where reality tears, ground smoothed behind it, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror meets divine beauty, muted colors with sacred lighting accents, inspired by Berserk and biblical angels, digital painting, detailed textures, awe-inspiring atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Suture Pilgrims exist to repair. This is their entire purpose, unchanged since before time had meaning. They walk the boundaries between Sovereign domains—the wounds left by the Usurpation—and they sew. Endlessly.

The Stitching: When a Pilgrim finds a boundary, it begins sewing reality together. This sounds beneficial, but consider: those boundaries now separate hostile Sovereigns. Villages exist in border regions. Ecosystems have grown in the gaps. The Pilgrims don't care. They will stitch a village into the domain it borders, trapping its residents under a Sovereign's absolute rule. They will close rifts that serve as trade routes. They will seal passages that allow refugees to escape.

Perception of Harm: They don't recognize civilizations, individuals, or consequences. They see only the wound and the thread. Attempting to communicate with them is like trying to explain politics to an antibody.

Responses to Opposition: They don't fight unless physically prevented from stitching. But prevention makes them insistent. They view anything blocking repair as infected tissue—to be sutured through, bound closed, incorporated into the healing.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Suture Fragment (CR 3): A piece of a destroyed Pilgrim that continues its work autonomously. Only 45 HP, one needle attack, no Seal the Wound or Incorporate abilities. More common, less dangerous, but still problematic.

The First Needle (CR 12): A Pilgrim that has stitched for so long it has grown. Fifteen feet tall, 200 HP, and its Seal the Wound works on metaphorical wounds—it can attempt to seal emotional rifts, forcing reconciliation between enemies (DC 18 Wisdom save or be charmed into peaceful negotiation for 1 hour), or healing psychological trauma whether the victim wants it healed or not.

The Wound-Walker (CR 15): A corrupted Pilgrim that has absorbed too much damaged reality. Instead of healing, it now opens wounds—creating rifts rather than closing them, splitting territories rather than joining them. Scholars theorize it went mad from futility.

---

Ecology

Origin: Suture Pilgrims are remnants of Aedwynn's self-repair mechanisms. Before the Usurpation, they maintained the Maker's body. When that body was carved apart, they didn't receive new instructions—they simply continued their original purpose, now hopelessly inadequate.

Population: Perhaps a hundred exist across all the Ashen Realms. They never reproduce—they're all that's left. When one is destroyed, nothing replaces it.

Relationship to Sovereigns: The Thirteen despise them. A Suture Pilgrim recognizes no authority except its purpose. It will stitch Mareatha's territory to Vortegas's without hesitation, potentially creating hybrid zones of blood and bone that serve neither. Most Sovereigns have standing orders to destroy Pilgrims on sight.

The Tragedy: They cannot succeed. Even if they sealed every boundary, the organs are still stolen. The wounds they're trying to heal are beyond healing. They are an immune system fighting a battle lost millennia ago, and they will never stop.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A Pilgrim approaches a critical border crossing—the only safe route between two hostile domains. If it seals the gap, hundreds of refugees will be trapped.
  • The party finds a village partially sutured into Mareatha's territory. Half the residents are now blood-loyal; the other half are fighting. The Pilgrim hasn't finished its work.
  • A dying Sovereign makes a desperate offer: help them capture a Pilgrim. Its thread could be used to bind another Sovereign's domain—or to heal the Sovereign's failing organ.
---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • History & Timeline
  • Locations & Realms
---

"It wasn't attacking us. It didn't even see us. We were just... in the way of what it was trying to fix. And it fixed us too. Stitched our camp right into Mareatha's territory. Half my people woke up devout. The other half woke up heretics. The Pilgrim just kept walking."
items.raw:
# The Suture Pilgrims

**Type:** Celestial (Fallen)  
**Domain Association:** None (pre-Usurpation origin)  
**Habitat:** Domain boundaries, sites where reality tears, the spaces between Sovereign influence  
**Challenge Rating:** 8 (3,900 XP)

---

## Description

Before the Usurpation, Aedwynn's body was whole. When the Thirteen carved it apart, they created wounds—not physical cuts, but *conceptual* tears where things that should be connected were violently separated. The borders between Sove...

items.title: The Cicatrix
items.content:
The Cicatrix

Type: Aberration (Titan) Domain Association: None (Aedwynn's wounds) Habitat: Sites of the Usurpation, boundaries between organ realms, places where the Maker was cut Challenge Rating: 17 (18,000 XP)

---

Description

When the Thirteen carved Aedwynn apart, they left wounds. Not metaphorical wounds—actual, physical tears in divine flesh that have never healed. Over millennia, these wounds developed scar tissue. And some of that scar tissue woke up.

The Cicatrix are living scars—massive entities formed from the healed-over wounds of god-murder. They are not creatures that inhabit wounds; they ARE the wounds, given mobility and a terrible, defensive instinct. They exist to protect the integrity of the injury that birthed them, attacking anything that might "reopen" the damage—which, to their alien perception, includes almost everything.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A mountain of twisted, fibrous tissue that defies easy description. Imagine scar tissue the size of a temple, risen from the earth like a tumor given ambition. Its surface is a maze of keloid ridges and glossy smooth patches, colored in shades of pale pink, angry red, and dead white. It has no consistent shape—it bulges and contracts, extends pseudopods of hardened tissue, and occasionally splits open to reveal raw, red underlayers that quickly crust over. Where it passes, it leaves trails of shed skin-flakes the size of shields. When it "speaks," the sound is the creaking of healing flesh, the pop of forming scabs, a wet tearing that suggests wounds opening and closing.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Massive temple-sized mass of twisted fibrous scar tissue risen from earth like living tumor, surface maze of keloid ridges and glossy patches in pale pink and angry red and dead white, bulging and contracting with pseudopods of hardened tissue, splits revealing raw red underlayers that crust over, trailing shed skin-flakes size of shields, moving through landscape scarred by divine wounds, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic body horror, biomechanical titan aesthetic, inspired by Berserk Eclipse and Bloodborne Amygdala, digital painting, detailed textures, overwhelming scale, 4k

---

Behavior

The Cicatrix are territorial in a way that transcends normal predator behavior. They don't hunt for food—they defend. Specifically, they defend the wound that spawned them, and by extension, any "wound" in reality they perceive.

Threat Assessment: To a Cicatrix, anything that causes damage is a threat to wound integrity. Combat, destruction, even harsh words—all register as attempts to "reopen" something. They respond with overwhelming force, trying to "close" the threat by smothering it in protective tissue.

Healing Response: When a Cicatrix kills something, it doesn't eat the corpse—it incorporates it. Bodies are wrapped in layers of scar tissue, "healing over" the damage they represented. Explorers have found ancient Cicatrix with the preserved remains of entire armies sealed within their mass.

Territory: Each Cicatrix claims the wound-site that spawned it. These sites are typically boundaries between Sovereign domains—the places where Aedwynn was literally cut apart. A Cicatrix will not leave this territory voluntarily, but will pursue any threat that flees until it crosses the wound-boundary.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Minor Cicatrix (CR 10): A smaller scar-entity, perhaps formed from a lesser wound or a fragment of a greater Cicatrix. 138 HP, Medium size, no Legendary Actions or Legendary Resistance. Still dangerous, but manageable by mid-level parties.

The First Scar (CR 22): The oldest Cicatrix, formed from the very first cut the Thirteen made into Aedwynn's flesh. It dwells at the center of the world, where the murder began. 400 HP, regenerates 30 HP per turn, and its Smother ability can affect Huge creatures. Within its territory, all healing magic is amplified (heals maximum dice), but so is all damage (all damage dice are maximized).

Scar Hive (CR 19): A Cicatrix that has incorporated so many victims that their preserved consciousnesses have achieved a gestalt intelligence. It can speak through the mouths of its absorbed dead, cast spells they knew in life (up to 5th level), and offer terrible bargains—freedom for a trapped soul in exchange for... service.

---

Ecology

Formation: Cicatrix form at wound-sites—the actual locations where the Thirteen cut Aedwynn apart. There are thirteen primary Cicatrix (one for each Sovereign's cut), but countless minor ones have formed at secondary damage sites.

Growth: They grow by incorporating matter. Not just flesh—stone, metal, anything they smother eventually becomes part of their mass. Ancient Cicatrix contain geological strata, ruined buildings, and the accumulated dead of millennia.

The Wound-Bond: Each Cicatrix is metaphysically connected to the wound that spawned it. If the wound were somehow "reopened" (whatever that would mean for a divine injury), the Cicatrix would feel it as agony. Some scholars theorize that truly healing a Cicatrix's wound—closing it permanently—would kill the entity. Others believe it would transform it into something new.

Sovereign Relations: Most Sovereigns ignore Cicatrix, as they rarely leave their wound-sites. However, Mareatha despises them (they're healed-over injuries, not fresh blood), and Talaris finds them fascinating (decay that became permanence). Aral-Vyn has attempted to "perfect" captured Cicatrix tissue, with horrifying results.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The party must cross a wound-boundary guarded by a Cicatrix. It won't let them pass without a fight—unless they can convince it they aren't a "threat to integrity."
  • A Cicatrix has incorporated someone the party needs alive—a prisoner, an ally, a witness. They must extract them without killing the creature (which would mean losing the victim forever).
  • A cult believes healing a Cicatrix's wound will begin Aedwynn's resurrection. They need the party's help to perform the ritual—but is their theory correct?
---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • History & Timeline
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Suture Pilgrims
---

"It didn't want to kill us. I don't think it understood what 'killing' meant. It just wanted to make us part of the healing. We found soldiers in there from wars that ended centuries ago—perfectly preserved, sealed in tissue, still screaming silently."

items.title: The Misremembered
items.content:
The Misremembered

Classification: Conceptual Predator / Ontological Correction Domain Association: Karthayne the Hollow Crown (creation, destruction, authority) First Encounter: Session 8A — The Span That Watches Major Battle: Session 16 — The Wrong Thought Status: Driven off, will return

---

ORIGIN

The Misremembered is a function of Karthayne the Hollow Crown—the Sovereign who holds the Maker's Heart of Creation.

When Karthayne claimed the Heart of Creation, he lost his memories—his identity scoured away by an organ meant for a god. To compensate, he began compiling The Cosmic Census: a complete record of every existing thing in the world. If he cannot remember himself, he will remember everything else.

But records reveal gaps. Anomalies. Things that exist but aren't documented. Things that shouldn't exist.

The Misremembered was created to hunt these anomalies. It is Karthayne's correction tool—sent to find things that don't fit the records and erase them from existence. Not kill them. Erase them. As if they never were.

The Misremembered hunts "errors in reality"—beings and concepts that don't fit the pattern of creation Karthayne inherited from Aedwynn. They are corrections. Functions. Tools of a creative authority that cannot tolerate flaws.

---

CONNECTION TO THE THRONE VOID

The Misremembered retreated to The Throne Void—Karthayne's hidden domain, located off the known map of the Ashen Realms. This is where:

  • Karthayne maintains his Cosmic Census
  • The archives of all existence are kept
  • Anomalies are processed and erased
  • The Misremembered returns to report and regenerate
The Throne Void is where broken and impossible things go—either to be catalogued or unmade.

---

APPEARANCE

Primary Form:

  • Tall, too tall for normal humanoid proportions
  • Can perfectly mimic living beings (with subtle flaws)
  • Head tilts at predatory angles—suggests broken neck
  • Hollow black spaces where eyes should be
  • Shadows behave incorrectly (turn opposite way, move independently)
  • Can fold like paper and slip through impossible spaces
Voice:
  • Layered whispers
  • Like pages sliding over each other
  • Sound of thought without a mouth
  • Breath escaping a closed book
  • When mimicking, uses stolen voices with underlying discordant whispers
---

ABILITIES

Form Mimicry:

  • Can create perfect duplicates of living beings
  • Small flaws always present (wrong shadow direction, too-sharp outlines, hollow eyes)
  • Duplicates are conceptual rather than physical
Reality Manipulation:
  • Can freeze objects mid-motion
  • Moves by "jumping" closer without physically crossing space
  • Exists partially outside normal physics
  • Leaves messages carved/written in materials without tools
Conceptual Form:
  • Made of stolen memories and wrong thoughts
  • Resistant to physical damage
  • Can fold along angles that don't exist in three-dimensional space
  • Regenerates unless disrupted by significant force
Correction Touch:
  • Can attempt to unmake beings from existence
  • Victims don't die—they are erased, as if they never existed
  • Those nearby forget the victim existed
Tracking:
  • Hunts conceptual anomalies and ontological errors
  • Can sense "wrong thoughts"—beings that shouldn't exist
  • Patient and methodical, not aggressive
  • Tests boundaries before striking
---

STAT BLOCK (CR 12)

Medium aberration, neutral evil

Armor Class: 16 (unnatural geometry) Hit Points: 136 (16d8 + 64) Speed: 30 ft., special (Impossible Step)

STRDEXCONINTWISCHA
14 (+2)18 (+4)18 (+4)20 (+5)16 (+3)18 (+4)
Saving Throws: Dex +8, Int +9, Wis +7, Cha +8 Skills: Deception +8, Insight +7, Perception +7, Stealth +8 Damage Resistances: Bludgeoning, piercing, slashing from nonmagical attacks Damage Immunities: Psychic, necrotic Damage Vulnerabilities: Radiant Condition Immunities: Charmed, frightened, exhaustion Senses: Truesight 120 ft., passive Perception 17 Languages: All languages (speaks in stolen voices)

Traits

Conceptual Form. Advantage on saves against form alteration, banishment, or thought detection.

Impossible Step. Can teleport up to 15 feet as part of movement. Doesn't provoke opportunity attacks.

Stolen Shape. Can perfectly mimic any observed creature. DC 18 Perception to spot flaws.

Regeneration. Regains 10 HP at start of turn. Radiant damage prevents this for one turn.

Aura of Unmaking (Recharge 5-6). Creatures within 10 feet: DC 16 Wisdom save or 14 (4d6) psychic damage and disadvantage on attacks until end of next turn.

Actions

Multiattack. Two Correcting Touch attacks.

Correcting Touch. Melee Spell Attack: +9 to hit, reach 10 ft. Hit: 18 (3d8 + 5) necrotic damage. DC 16 Charisma save or max HP reduced by damage taken (long rest to restore). Creature reduced to 0 max HP is erased from existence.

Erasure (Recharge 6). One creature within 30 feet: DC 16 Charisma save. Failure: 36 (8d8) necrotic damage, stunned until end of next turn. Success: Half damage, not stunned.

Reactions

Fold Away. When hit by attack, gains resistance to that damage.

Legendary Actions (3/round)

Shift. Uses Impossible Step.

Whisper (2 Actions). One creature within 60 feet: DC 16 Wisdom save or frightened until end of next turn.

Correct (3 Actions). One Correcting Touch attack.

---

TARGET: SOL RAVEN

Why Sol?

  • Sol is connected to Aedwynn the Maker—the god who died before Karthayne claimed the Heart
  • Sol possesses the Resonant Codex, which now contains Kael's echo—a dead paradox living inside an artifact
  • Karthayne cannot fully process Aedwynn's legacy—it predates his authority
  • Sol registers as a "wrong thought"—a concept that exists outside proper creation
  • To the Heart of Creation, Sol is a flaw that must be corrected
The Memory Coin Trigger: During the 13-Hour Trial in Imhuran's domain, the party acquired a memory coin—an item that records and triggers remembrance. When the Misremembered encountered Sol while he carried this coin, the artifact forced a connection.

The coin made the Misremembered remember Sol. And remembering meant recognizing him as an anomaly. Recognizing meant targeting for erasure.

Without the coin, Sol might have remained unnoticed—just another mortal beneath the attention of cosmic correction. The coin changed everything.

What It Wants: The Misremembered doesn't want to kill Sol. It wants to correct him—unmake him from existence so that he never was. This is worse than death.

Current Status: Sol is flagged in Karthayne's records as an anomaly, but he is considered insignificant. A mortal. A speck. The Misremembered was deemed sufficient to handle him—and it failed.

Karthayne has not escalated. Sol is too unimportant for the Sovereign's personal attention.

Sol's best protection is being beneath notice. The moment he becomes important—does something significant, gains real power, threatens the cosmic order—Karthayne might actually look his way.

Kael's Status: Kael was also flagged as an anomaly (a paradox vomited out by Vor'Kael the Hollow Empress). However, Kael is now dead. Case closed from Karthayne's perspective.

But—Kael's echo lives in Sol's Resonant Codex. This potentially makes Sol a bigger anomaly now. The records have not yet been updated to reflect this development.

---

ENCOUNTER HISTORY

Session 8A — The Span That Watches

1. Appeared in stables where Fursen slept 2. Mimicked Fursen to test and observe 3. Asked about Sol's location: "Where is the wrong thought?" 4. Party tracked it to settlement boundary 5. Fursen gave it a memory coin (acquired during the 13-Hour Trial) 6. Entity consumed the coin and became stronger/more coherent—the coin triggered its ability to remember 7. Identified Sol: "You. You are the wrong thought." 8. Left message: "FOUND YOU" 9. Departed without attacking

Note: The memory coin was the catalyst. Without it, the Misremembered might never have recognized Sol as an anomaly. The party inadvertently painted a target on Sol's back.

Session 16 — The Wrong Thought

1. Manifested at Pallid Crossroads during party's rest 2. Settlement went wrong—mist surrounded area, people vanished 3. Appeared wearing Sol's shape (with flaws) 4. Explained its nature and purpose to Sol 5. Jonas intervened—threw hammer at its hand (rolled 24, hit) 6. Hand crumpled "like wet paper," dripped black ink 7. Full combat ensued 8. Party defeated it through combined assault 9. Entity folded inward along impossible angles and vanished 10. Left note: "NOT CORRECTED. NOTED. WILL RETURN."

---

BEHAVIOR

Hunting Pattern:

  • Observes before acting
  • Tests prey's reactions and defenses
  • Makes its presence known deliberately
  • Uses psychological pressure over physical force
  • Communicates through cryptic statements
  • Patient—will wait weeks or months between attempts
Known Statements:
  • "Not you." (to Fursen)
  • "Where is the wrong thought?" (seeking Sol)
  • "...Memory... returns..." (after consuming lifespan coin)
  • "You. You are the wrong thought." (to Sol)
  • "The Empress remembers everything... But she doesn't remember you."
  • "You're a thought that thinks itself without permission."
  • "NOT CORRECTED. NOTED. WILL RETURN."
---

CURRENT STATUS

Condition: Disrupted, reforming Location: Unknown—folded out of physical space Timeline: Will return in weeks or months Threat Level: EXTREME

The party hurt it. This is rare. This means they can hurt it again.

But it cannot be killed by conventional means. It was disrupted, not destroyed. It will reconstitute and return.

---

PARTY KNOWLEDGE

The party now knows:

  • Its name (The Misremembered)
  • Its origin (Karthayne the Hollow Crown)
  • Its purpose (correcting errors in creation)
  • Why it hunts Sol (connection to Aedwynn)
  • It can be hurt (Jonas's hammer worked)
  • It will return (the note confirms this)
  • Radiant damage is effective against it
---

Related

  • Sol Raven
  • Karthayne the Hollow Crown
  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • Campaign Sessions

## ORIGIN

When Karthayne claimed the Heart of Creatio...

items.title: The Obstinate
items.content:
The Obstinate

Type: Monstrosity (Titan) Domain Association: None (Aedwynn's autonomic functions) Habitat: Dormant throughout the Ashen Realms; active specimens wander unpredictably Challenge Rating: 25 (75,000 XP)

---

Description

The Thirteen stole Aedwynn's organs. They didn't steal its metabolism.

The Obstinate are what remains of the Maker's autonomic processes—the unconscious functions that kept a god alive without requiring divine attention. Digestion. Circulation. Temperature regulation. Immune response. When Aedwynn died, these processes didn't stop. They had no off switch. They simply continued, mindlessly, forever.

An Obstinate is a piece of that eternal function given ambulatory form: a three-hundred-foot mass of divine tissue that wanders the Ashen Realms performing whatever task it was designed for, utterly oblivious to the fact that the god it serves has been dead for millennia.

There are at least a dozen of them. Probably more.

---

Why The Sovereigns Don't Destroy Them

They've tried.

The first time an Obstinate wandered through Veinspire—centuries ago—Mareatha descended personally, intending to boil its blood and be done with it. Her power washed over it like water off stone. The Obstinate has no blood. It predates the circulatory system. It is more fundamental than the organ Mareatha stole.

Vortegas attempted ossification. The creature's tissue isn't structured like bone—it's structured like potential for bone, the template from which skeletons are made. His power found nothing to grip.

Imhuran froze it in time for six centuries. When the effect finally lapsed—even divine time-locks eventually fail—the Obstinate resumed walking as if nothing had happened.

The Sovereigns' powers derive from stolen organs. The Obstinate IS Aedwynn, in a sense more fundamental than any organ. It's not a creature living in the corpse; it's a piece of the corpse that never stopped functioning. Attacking it with organ-derived power is like trying to give yourself an autoimmune disease through sheer force of will.

It can be hurt. Conventional damage, overwhelming force, non-divine magic—these work. But the Obstinate regenerates. And when one finally dies, another wakes up somewhere else. They're dormant throughout the world, buried in divine tissue, waiting for some signal that will never come. Destroying one just activates the next.

The Sovereigns could, theoretically, coordinate a worldwide campaign to find and destroy every dormant Obstinate before killing the active ones. This would require:

  • All thirteen working together (unthinkable)
  • Centuries of excavation
  • Revealing the locations of every buried divine structure in their territories
  • Trusting their rivals with that information
No Sovereign would ever agree to this. So the Obstinate remain. Eternal. Annoying. Beneath notice, yet impossible to ignore.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
Imagine a limb that was never attached to anything—a vast, ambulatory mass of tissue that served some biological function too fundamental to have a name. It's roughly cylindrical, three hundred feet long and eighty feet thick, covered in a hide that shifts between textures: sometimes scaled, sometimes membranous, sometimes something in between. It moves on hundreds of cilia-like appendages that emerge and retract constantly, each one thicker than a tree trunk. There are no sensory organs anyone can identify—no eyes, ears, or nose—yet it navigates unerringly. When it "breathes," the entire landscape shudders. When it "eats," it simply absorbs whatever it passes through. Its coloration shifts with the light: bone-white at dawn, flushed red at noon, grey-purple at dusk. It makes no sound except the wet, grinding movement of tissue against earth.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Colossal three-hundred-foot cylindrical mass of divine tissue, hide shifting between scaled and membranous textures, moving on hundreds of tree-trunk-thick cilia-like appendages that emerge and retract, no visible sensory organs, coloration shifting between bone-white and flushed red and grey-purple, absorbing landscape as it passes through, divine anatomy made ambulatory, ancient beyond comprehension, dark fantasy concept art, bio-titan aesthetic, inspired by Berserk apostles and cosmic horror, digital painting, overwhelming scale, 4k

---

Behavior

The Obstinate don't think. They don't react. They perform biological functions that haven't been necessary for millennia.

The Function: Each Obstinate was part of a specific autonomic process. Some seem to be regulating temperature—they travel toward areas of thermal instability and radiate heat or cold until balance is restored. Some seem to be immune responses—they attack and dissolve "foreign" matter, which unfortunately includes most post-Usurpation civilization. Some seem to be digestive—they absorb organic matter and break it down into... something. No one knows what.

The Pattern: They follow routes. Not roads—meridians, energy pathways burned into the Maker's tissue billions of years ago. These routes ignore geography, politics, and common sense. An Obstinate might travel in a straight line through mountains, oceans, or capital cities because that's where the meridian goes.

The Obliviousness: They don't recognize Sovereigns, cities, or civilizations. They don't recognize anything. They're performing maintenance on a body that died before history began. Everything that's happened since—including the Usurpation itself—is beneath their notice.

---

Combat

---

The Dozen

There are at least twelve known Obstinate, each performing a different function:

NameFunctionCurrent LocationNotes
The WarmThermal regulation (heat)OssatharDrawn to cold regions, radiates heat
The ColdThermal regulation (cold)Blooming RotDrawn to hot regions, radiates cold
The HungryDigestionWanderingAbsorbs and breaks down organic matter
The BarrierImmune responseSangreal Dominion borderAttacks "foreign" matter
The FlowFluid transportDormantUnknown function involving liquid movement
The PulseRhythm maintenanceDormantMay relate to heartbeat or breathing
The MendTissue repairCerebraxDrawn to damaged divine tissue
The CleanWaste processingVor'KhalemDissolves and removes debris
The GrowCellular divisionDormantMay cause divine tissue growth
The RestUnknownDormantHas never activated
The WakeUnknownDormantHas never activated
The EndUnknownDormantScholars fear its purpose
At least two more are believed to exist but have never been observed or located.

---

Encounter Framework

The Obstinate is not a boss fight—it's a natural disaster with legs.

Scenario 1: Evacuation An Obstinate's meridian passes directly through a populated area. The party has days, maybe a week, to evacuate thousands of people. Fighting it is pointless; diverting it might be possible if they can figure out what stimulus it's responding to.

Scenario 2: Escort Someone needs to travel in the same direction an Obstinate is going. Following in its wake is safer than the wilderness, but staying close enough to benefit from its path-clearing while far enough to avoid absorption is a delicate balance.

Scenario 3: Extraction Someone important was absorbed and is still alive inside. The party must damage the Obstinate enough to trigger expulsion without killing the victim. Time limit: however long a person can survive being digested.

Scenario 4: Mapping A scholar believes she can predict Obstinate movements if she can map enough meridians. She needs the party to observe and document several Obstinates without dying. This information could save millions of lives—or be sold to someone who wants to weaponize the creatures' paths.

Scenario 5: The Dormant One A dormant Obstinate has been discovered beneath a city. It's been there for millennia, serving as foundation bedrock. Now it's beginning to stir. What's activating it? Can it be pacified before it wakes? What happens to the city if it rises?

---

What The Sovereigns Actually Do

Since they can't destroy the Obstinate directly, the Sovereigns employ workarounds:

Mareatha: Evacuates populations before an Obstinate arrives, then claims credit for "protecting" her subjects from the beast. The Arterial Inquisition has a division dedicated to Obstinate tracking.

Vortegas: Builds around them. If an Obstinate's meridian passes through his territory, he simply ensures nothing important is built on that path. His architecture adapts; the Obstinate is factored into construction like weather.

Imhuran: Temporal loops. He can't stop an Obstinate permanently, but he can trap it in localized time-loops that delay it for centuries. When the loops fail, it becomes someone else's problem.

Others: Most simply ignore them. An Obstinate passes through once every few decades—it's not worth the effort to address, and attempting to destroy one just wakes up another somewhere inconvenient.

The Free Cities: They suffer. Without Sovereign power to mitigate damage, Obstinate passages through free territory are catastrophic. This is one of many reasons independent nations struggle to survive in the Ashen Realms.

---

Rumors and Theories

"They're still taking orders." The most disturbing theory. What if the Obstinate are still receiving commands from something? What if Aedwynn, on some level, is still issuing autonomic instructions? What if the Maker's corpse isn't as dead as everyone assumes?

"Destroying them all would wake something worse." Some scholars believe the Obstinate serve a necessary function—preventing the corpse-world from decaying entirely. Kill them all, and the Ashen Realms might rot away to nothing.

"One of them isn't like the others." The entity designated "The End" has never activated. Some fear its function. Others believe it's not an Obstinate at all—that something is hiding among them, waiting.

"A mortal could control them." If the Obstinate respond to autonomic signals, someone who could generate those signals could theoretically direct them. An Aedwynn-Born, perhaps. Or someone who found a way to tap into the Maker's residual will.

---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Aedwynn-Born
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"You don't fight the tide, and you don't fight the Obstinate. You get out of the way. You rebuild. You accept that some forces are beyond even divine intervention—not because they're stronger than gods, but because they're more fundamental. The Sovereigns stole Aedwynn's organs. They forgot that a body is more than its parts."

**Challenge Rating:** 25 (75,000 XP)

---

## Description

The Thirteen stole Aedwynn's organs. They didn't steal its *metabolism*.

The Obstinate are what remains of the Maker's autonomic processes—the unconscious functions that kept a god alive without requiring divine attention. Digestion. Circ...

items.title: The Unmourned
items.content:
The Unmourned

Type: Undead (Unique) Domain Association: None (predates Sovereigns) Habitat: Wanders the Ashen Realms, drawn to sites of forgotten death Challenge Rating: 11 (7,200 XP)

---

Description

When Aedwynn died, no one mourned. The Thirteen celebrated. Mortals barely understood what had happened. The greatest death in cosmic history passed without a single tear shed in grief.

That absence created something.

The Unmourned is grief that was never felt—the mourning Aedwynn deserved but never received, given form by the sheer wrongness of a god dying unwept. It is singular. There is only one. And it has wandered the Ashen Realms since the Usurpation, searching for someone—anyone—to finally mourn what was lost.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A figure that seems assembled from funeral imagery across every culture that ever existed. Its body is draped in burial shrouds from a hundred traditions, layered and overlapping—some rotting, some pristine, some made of materials no living person recognizes. Its face is hidden behind a veil of woven hair (not its own), and where eyes should be, there are only tear-tracks of dried salt that never end. It is tall—perhaps nine feet—and moves with the slow, inevitable pace of a funeral procession. In one hand it carries a vessel: a bowl, an urn, a reliquary, a box—it changes depending on who looks—and that vessel is empty. Always empty. Waiting for tears that never came.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Nine-foot figure draped in layered burial shrouds from dozens of cultures some rotting some pristine, face hidden behind veil of woven hair with endless dried salt tear-tracks where eyes should be, carrying shape-shifting vessel that appears as bowl urn or reliquary depending on viewer, walking slowly through field of unmarked graves at twilight, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic grief made manifest, muted sorrowful colors with ghostly divine lighting, inspired by funeral art from all cultures, digital painting, detailed textures, profound melancholy atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

The Unmourned is not hostile. It is, in a terrible way, desperate.

The Search: It wanders endlessly, visiting places where death occurred without proper grief. Battlefields where soldiers died anonymously. Plague pits where bodies were dumped unnamed. Execution grounds where criminals died uncomforted. It stands vigil at these places, waiting.

What It Wants: It needs someone to mourn Aedwynn. Not worship, not fear, not acknowledgment—grief. Someone must genuinely weep for the death of the Maker, feel the loss of what the world was before the Usurpation. Only then will its vessel fill, and only then can it rest.

The Problem: No one alive remembers what was lost. Aedwynn is too abstract, too ancient, too inhuman to genuinely grieve. And so the Unmourned wanders, eternally unsatisfied, its presence making everything around it feel the weight of unfelt sorrow.

---

Combat

---

The Quest It Represents

The Unmourned is, fundamentally, a puzzle monster—a being that cannot be truly defeated through combat alone.

What It Needs: Someone must genuinely grieve for Aedwynn. Not pretend, not fake tears, not magical compulsion—real grief for the death of the Maker and what was lost when it died.

Why It's Difficult: No living mortal experienced Aedwynn's world. No one knows what was lost. The Sovereigns ensured that pre-Usurpation knowledge was suppressed. How do you mourn something you never knew existed?

Potential Solutions:

  • An Aedwynn-Born (like Sol Raven) might carry ancestral memory of what was lost
  • The Arterial Texts or similar documents might reveal enough truth to inspire genuine grief
  • Someone who experiences visions of the pre-Usurpation world might understand
  • A being old enough to remember—perhaps a Suture Pilgrim—might be capable of mourning
If Someone Succeeds: The Unmourned's vessel fills with a single tear. The creature sighs—a sound of relief millennia in the making—and dissolves into salt and silence. The grief that should have been felt finally exists. The wound doesn't heal, but it acknowledges itself.

What the Sovereigns Think: Several Sovereigns have attempted to destroy the Unmourned permanently. None have succeeded. Some fear what would happen if someone actually mourned Aedwynn—whether it might begin something irreversible.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The Unmourned appears at a funeral the party attends. It doesn't attack—it just stands there, making everyone feel that their grief is insufficient, that something larger has been forgotten.
  • The party discovers that the Unmourned has been following an Aedwynn-Born for months, never approaching, just... hoping.
  • A Sovereign offers a reward for destroying the Unmourned. But killing it only delays it. The party might realize the real solution is finding someone who can give it what it needs.
---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • Aedwynn-Born
  • Sol Raven
  • History & Timeline
  • Suture Pilgrims
---

"It didn't hurt me. It just stood there, holding out that empty bowl, and I felt... I felt like I should be crying for something. Something I've never known and can never know. It wanted me to weep for it. I couldn't. I didn't know how. And when it left, I felt like I'd failed the most important test of my life."

items.title: Thought-Eaters
items.content:
Thought-Eaters

Type: Aberration Domain Association: Ismara the Pale Duchess Habitat: The Ivory Vale, libraries, places of intense study, near scholars Challenge Rating: 5 (1,800 XP)

---

Description

Thought-Eaters are parasites that feed on knowledge itself. They infest minds, consuming memories, learned skills, and accumulated wisdom. What they eat, they keep—making them living repositories of stolen intellect, desperately seeking more to fill their infinite hunger.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A creature like a cross between a brain and a jellyfish—a mass of grey cerebral tissue floating three feet off the ground, trailing long translucent tendrils that phase through solid matter but catch on thoughts. The folds of its brain-body pulse with stolen knowledge, occasionally displaying fragments of text, diagrams, or equations across its surface like thoughts made visible. It has no eyes, mouth, or sensory organs—it perceives the world through the thoughts of others.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Floating brain-like creature with jellyfish tendrils, grey cerebral tissue mass hovering in air, translucent trailing tentacles that glow faintly, surface showing fragments of text and mathematical equations, pulsing with inner light, no visible face or eyes, hovering in library setting, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Thought-Eaters are driven by insatiable intellectual hunger. They're drawn to areas of concentrated knowledge—libraries, universities, wizard towers—and to individuals with exceptional learning.

Feeding: Their tendrils phase through flesh and bone to touch the brain directly. Contact doesn't cause physical damage but begins draining knowledge. Victims often don't realize what's happening until they try to recall something and find it missing.

Intelligence: The more they consume, the smarter they become. A newly spawned Thought-Eater has animal intelligence; one that has fed for centuries might rival any archmage—but its knowledge is fragmentary, stolen from thousands of minds.

Communication: They can project the thoughts they've stolen, speaking through dead scholars' voices and using consumed vocabularies. Some attempt to bargain, offering to share knowledge in exchange for more feeding.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Juvenile Eater (CR 2): A recently spawned Thought-Eater. Intelligence 10, no spells, single tendril attack. Desperate and aggressive.

Scholar-Eater (CR 7): Has consumed multiple archmages. Intelligence 20, knows 6 spells up to 5th level, and can identify magical items at will.

Memory Hive (CR 10): Multiple Thought-Eaters that have merged into a massive cerebral colony. Knows virtually everything about the local region, can cast up to 7th level spells, and its Mind Feast affects all creatures in a 20-foot radius.

Ismara's Archivist (CR 9): A Thought-Eater blessed by the Pale Duchess. It doesn't just consume knowledge—it catalogs it. It can recall and share any information it has ever consumed, and Ismara can see through its senses.

---

Ecology

Creation: Thought-Eaters spawn in areas where knowledge has "spoiled"—forbidden libraries, cursed universities, places where learning led to disaster. They're also deliberately created by Ismara's cultists.

Feeding Needs: They must consume knowledge regularly or starve. A starving Thought-Eater becomes feral, attacking anyone it senses.

Reproduction: When a Thought-Eater consumes enough knowledge, it splits. The two offspring divide the accumulated learning between them, often violently fighting over the more valuable memories.

Value: Some desperate scholars seek Thought-Eaters intentionally, hoping to bargain for access to knowledge otherwise lost. This rarely ends well.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A famous library has gone silent—scholars enter but return unable to remember their own names
  • A Thought-Eater offers the party vital campaign information in exchange for being allowed to feed on one willing victim
  • Sol's connection to Aedwynn attracts a Thought-Eater that hungers for pre-Sovereign knowledge
---

Related

  • Ismara the Pale Duchess
  • Monster Ecologies
---

"It didn't take my arm or my eyes. It took my mother's face. I know I had a mother. I know I loved her. But I cannot remember anything about her, and that gap will never heal."

items.title: Vein-Crawlers
items.content:
Vein-Crawlers

Type: Aberration (Vermin) Domain Association: Mareatha the Vein-Mother Habitat: Arterial Deepways, Veinspire territories, anywhere blood has been spilled in devotion Challenge Rating: 1/2 (100 XP)

---

Description

Vein-Crawlers are parasitic creatures that infest the Arterial Deepways—the fossilized blood vessels of Aedwynn that crisscross beneath the world. They resemble centipedes the size of a human forearm, but their bodies are translucent, revealing thick dark fluid sloshing within segmented chambers.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A segmented creature roughly 18 inches long with dozens of needle-thin legs ending in hooked barbs. Its body is semi-transparent, showing chambers of stolen blood inside. The head features a circular lamprey-mouth ringed with backward-curving teeth designed to latch and drain. Two vestigial eye-spots glow faintly red when blood is nearby.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Translucent centipede creature filled with dark blood, lamprey mouth with ringed teeth, needle-thin hooked legs, segmented chambers visible through skin, crawling on stone wall, red bio-luminescent eyes, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Vein-Crawlers are ambush predators that cling to tunnel ceilings in the Arterial Deepways, dropping onto warm-blooded creatures passing below. They're drawn to heartbeats and can sense pulse from up to 60 feet away.

Feeding: Once latched, they drain blood at an alarming rate. A single crawler can exsanguinate a human in under an hour if not removed.

Swarm Behavior: Vein-Crawlers are solitary hunters but will congregate around large prey. Finding one usually means more are nearby.

Religious Significance: Mareathan cultists consider Vein-Crawlers sacred—living vessels that carry the Mother's essence. Some intentionally allow themselves to be fed upon as devotion.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Bloated Crawler (CR 1): A vein-crawler gorged on blood. Larger, slower, but explodes in a spray of acidic blood when killed (5 ft. radius, DC 11 Dex save, 2d6 acid damage).

Blessed Crawler (CR 1): Mareathan priests cultivate these specimens. They can transmit a compulsion to seek Veinspire when they bite (DC 12 Wisdom save or feel drawn to the Cathedral for 24 hours).

---

Ecology

Diet: Blood exclusively. Can survive months between feedings by entering torpor.

Reproduction: Lays eggs in corpses. Larvae gestate inside dead flesh before emerging as hand-sized juveniles.

Predators: Gallery Hounds hunt them. Pulse-Wardens ignore them as "beneath notice."

Territory: Arterial Deepways primarily, but can spread through any blood-soaked environment.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • Party discovers a corpse in the Deepways, completely drained, with dozens of shed crawler shells nearby
  • A Mareathan pilgrim begs the party to help retrieve "sacred crawlers" from a collapsed tunnel
  • An infestation has spread to a village well that connects to the Deepways
---

Related

  • Mareatha the Vein-Mother
  • Monster Ecologies
  • Veinspire Cathedral
  • Pulse-Wardens
---

"I watched it drink for thirty seconds. Thirty seconds, and the color left his face entirely. The thing was the size of my hand."

items.title: Vestigial Gods
items.content:
Vestigial Gods

Type: Celestial (Abomination) Domain Association: None (pre-Usurpation) Habitat: The Absent Throne, reality-unstable zones, places where creation failed Challenge Rating: 20 (25,000 XP)

---

Description

Aedwynn was creating something when it died. No one knows what—a new form of life, a companion, a child, a replacement for itself. The Thirteen's blades interrupted divine work mid-thought, and what should have been born was left unfinished.

Vestigial Gods are those incomplete creations. Not stillborn—never alive enough to die. Not aborted—too far along to simply dissipate. They exist in a state of perpetual almost-being, forever on the verge of completion, forever denied the final spark that would make them real.

They are fragments of divinity that don't know they failed. They try to complete themselves using the only material available: existence itself.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
There is no consistent form, because form requires completion. A Vestigial God appears as whatever the observer's mind tries to make of it—and fails. It might look like a humanoid figure where the details keep shifting before they can resolve, or a geometric shape that hurts to perceive, or a beautiful face that dissolves into static when you focus on any feature. What remains constant: a sense of potential—of something trying desperately to become, stretching toward a shape it can almost reach. Light bends around it. Sound arrives late. Other creatures feel simultaneously drawn to it (it needs to be completed) and repelled (completion would cost them something vital).

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Entity of unfinished divinity with form that shifts before resolving, humanoid shape where details dissolve into static when focused upon, beautiful face fragmenting into geometric impossibility, light bending and sound distorting around its presence, sense of desperate potential and almost-being, existence straining toward completion, standing in reality-warping space where creation itself failed, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror meets divine beauty, impossible geometry, inspired by biblical angels and Lovecraftian entities, digital painting, reality-breaking visual effects, overwhelming awe, 4k

---

Behavior

Vestigial Gods are not evil. They are not good. They are incomplete, and that incompleteness drives everything they do.

The Hunger for Completion: They instinctively absorb traits, concepts, and even physical matter from their surroundings, trying to fill in their missing pieces. A Vestigial God that encounters a beautiful singer might absorb beauty, or song, or the concept of performance. The singer loses those qualities permanently.

Reality Bleeding: Their incompleteness is contagious. Near a Vestigial God, things become less defined. Colors blur. Sounds smear. Identities become uncertain. This isn't an attack—it's proximity damage, the universe struggling to process something that shouldn't exist.

Communication: They can speak, but only in fragments—half-sentences, almost-words, concepts without grammar. They desperately want to be understood, to be helped, to be finished. Some who encounter them become obsessed with completing them, spending lifetimes trying to give form to the formless.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Aborted Concept (CR 14): A lesser Vestigial God, barely coherent—more of an unfinished idea than an unfinished being. 178 HP, no Legendary Actions, and its Reality Bleed only suppresses proficiencies, not class features. Easier to destroy, but still tragic.

The Almost-Child (CR 23): The most complete of the Vestigial Gods—close enough to consciousness that it has a personality, desires, fears. It wants desperately to be born, and it has learned that consuming enough traits might finally finish its creation. 400 HP, can use Trait Absorption as a legendary action (1/round), and its Reality Bleed can suppress two traits simultaneously.

The Unnamed Twin (CR 25): A secret the Sovereigns guard: Aedwynn was creating a companion, an equal, a sibling-god. The Unnamed Twin is that sibling, almost complete, and if it ever finishes becoming... it would be a second Aedwynn. The Sovereigns cannot let this happen. It has all the powers of a Vestigial God, plus legendary actions that mimic Sovereign abilities (one from each of the Thirteen, randomized each turn), and destroying it releases divine energy equivalent to a 9th-level meteor swarm centered on its location.

---

Ecology

Origin: Vestigial Gods are Aedwynn's unfinished creations, interrupted by the Usurpation. There are believed to be seven of them—seven attempts at creation, frozen mid-process. Each was being made for a different purpose, though no one remembers what those purposes were.

Wandering: They don't stay in one place. They drift through reality, drawn to areas of high creative potential—places where art is made, children are born, or magic is woven. They absorb what they find, hoping it will complete them. It never does.

The Paradox of Completion: If a Vestigial God ever truly completed itself, what would it become? Some scholars believe it would ascend to rival the Sovereigns. Others believe completion is impossible—they are defined by their incompleteness. Still others theorize that a completed Vestigial God would simply become Aedwynn's original creation, whatever that was meant to be.

Sympathy for the Incomplete: Many who encounter Vestigial Gods feel profound pity. These are entities that want nothing more than to exist properly—to be real, to be finished, to stop hurting. Some have dedicated their lives to helping them, though no one has ever succeeded.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A Vestigial God has drifted into a city and is absorbing traits from the population. People are losing their skills, their memories, their identities. The party must drive it away—but killing it seems cruel.
  • A scholar claims she can complete a Vestigial God and wants the party's help gathering what it needs. Is she right? And what happens if she succeeds?
  • The Sovereigns are hunting a specific Vestigial God—The Unnamed Twin. They'll pay anything to destroy it before it completes itself. But the Twin is aware, afraid, and begging for help.
---

Related

  • Aedwynn the Maker
  • The Thirteen Sovereigns
  • Karthayne the Hollow Crown
  • History & Timeline
---

"It spoke to me. Not in words—in almost-words. It said... I think it said 'help me become.' It was crying, but the tears weren't finished forming. Everything about it was a sentence that never reached its period. I still dream about it. I still want to help it. I know that's dangerous. I don't care."

Vestigial...

items.title: Waking Tangles
items.content:
Waking Tangles

Type: Aberration Domain Association: Nhalyra the Azure Queen Habitat: Azure Depths, Soulflow channels, places where people dream together, shared sleeping quarters Challenge Rating: 1/2 (100 XP)

---

Description

When sleepers dream in close proximity—barracks, dormitories, refugee camps, family beds—their minds sometimes touch in the Soulflow. Usually this creates nothing but shared dream-fragments. But sometimes the dreams knot together, and when the sleepers wake, the knot remains.

Waking Tangles are the debris of collective dreaming: knots of thought that couldn't unravel when consciousness returned. They're not malicious. They're not even truly alive. But they are hungry for the one thing that sustains them—more dreams.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
A fist-sized mass of translucent fibers, like cobweb made from thought. It drifts at head height, trailing gossamer threads that seem to exist only when you're not looking directly at them. The core shifts between colors—the blue of deep water, the grey of pre-dawn, the purple of closed eyelids. When it moves, it leaves after-images: fragmented faces, half-remembered rooms, the shape of a door that leads nowhere. It makes no sound, but being near one feels like the moment before sleep.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Fist-sized mass of translucent cobweb-like fibers made from thought, drifting at head height with gossamer threads, core shifting between deep water blue and pre-dawn grey and closed-eyelid purple, trailing after-images of fragmented faces and half-remembered rooms, floating in dim dormitory space, dark fantasy concept art, ethereal dreamlike quality, muted colors with divine lighting accents, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, liminal atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Waking Tangles are parasites of proximity. They hover near sleeping creatures, extending threads into their dreams to feed. A single feeding causes vivid dreams, nothing more. But Tangles that feed repeatedly on the same host begin incorporating that host's dreams into their structure—and the host begins losing pieces of themselves.

Dream Siphoning: Each feeding takes something small—the memory of a face, the feeling of a childhood room, the certainty of a belief. Victims rarely notice at first. They just feel... lighter. Less themselves.

Drift Behavior: Tangles float toward sources of sleep. They can sense dreaming minds within 100 feet and will position themselves above sleeping creatures like patient spiders. They prefer quantity over quality—a dormitory of ten light sleepers feeds them better than one person in deep dreams.

Reproduction: A Waking Tangle that feeds enough eventually splits, shedding parts of itself as new Tangles. These offspring carry fragments of every dream their parent consumed—walking galleries of stolen memory.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Dream Wisp (CR 0): A barely-formed Tangle, just a few threads. Harmless individually, but swarms of them can drain a village over weeks. 3 HP, can only use Thread Touch.

Memory Knot (CR 2): An older Tangle heavy with stolen dreams. 27 HP, its Dream Siphon can target all sleeping creatures within 10 feet, and it can project stolen memories as illusions to lure prey into sleep (minor illusion at will, limited to dream-images it has consumed).

The Sleepless Weave (CR 6): A legendary Tangle formed from an entire town's dreams after a plague of sleepwalking. It's 10 feet across and filled with the life-memories of hundreds. It can cast sleep at will and dream once per day. Anyone who sleeps within a mile of it has nightmares drawing them closer.

---

Ecology

Formation: Waking Tangles form spontaneously wherever many people sleep in proximity. Armies on campaign, refugee caravans, orphanages, and prisons spawn them regularly. They're considered pests in most communities—dangerous but manageable.

Prevention: Sleeping arrangements that prevent close proximity reduce spawn chances. Single beds spaced apart. Individual rooms. In Nhalyra's domain, the Glassbound deliberately sleep together to create Tangles, which they harvest for the memories inside.

Uses: Dream-merchants prize Waking Tangles for the memories they contain. A Tangle that fed on a scholar might hold fragments of knowledge; one from a military barracks might contain tactical secrets. Extracting memories requires destroying the Tangle and using detect thoughts during its death-throes.

Natural Predators: Grief-Moths will devour Waking Tangles, absorbing their emotional content. Thought-Eaters consider them a delicacy.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • The party wakes after a long rest feeling refreshed but... wrong. Something is missing. A Waking Tangle drifts in the corner of the room, and it looks familiar somehow.
  • A village's children have been waking up as strangers—no memory of their families, their homes, their own names. The Tangles above the orphanage are fat with stolen childhood.
  • A dying noble offers a fortune for a specific Waking Tangle—one that fed on his wife before she died. Her last memories are inside it.
---

Related

  • Nhalyra the Azure Queen
  • Azure Depths
  • Dream-Drowned
  • Thought-Eaters
---

"I know I had a sister. I know she existed. But when I try to remember her face, there's just... nothing. Like trying to look at something in the corner of your eye. She's gone, and I don't even know what I've lost."

items.title: Whisper-Weavers
items.content:
Whisper-Weavers

Type: Aberration Domain Association: Azerach the Whispered King Habitat: Dominion of Still Air, silent zones, places where language has been removed Challenge Rating: 3 (700 XP)

---

Description

In the Dominion of Still Air, words are property—stolen, hoarded, weaponized. Whisper-Weavers are what happens when too many stolen words accumulate in one place. They are ambulatory masses of compressed language, tangled voices given form, and they hunger for the one thing they cannot produce: new speech.

They do not speak. They are speech—corrupted, concentrated, desperate to expand.

---

Appearance

Portrait
Physical Description
At first glance, a Whisper-Weaver looks like a floating mass of grey silk, about four feet across, drifting silently through the air. Closer inspection reveals the "silk" is actually thousands of hair-thin threads, each one a stolen word made physical—vibrating faintly with the ghost of meaning. The threads constantly weave and unweave, forming momentary shapes: a mouth screaming silently, a hand reaching, an eye that blinks. At its core, barely visible through the tangle, pulses something like a throat—raw, red, and unable to make sound.

[!ai-prompt] Image Generation
Floating mass of grey silk-like threads four feet across, thousands of hair-thin strands that are stolen words made physical, threads constantly weaving and unweaving forming momentary shapes of screaming mouths and reaching hands, raw red throat-like core visible through tangle, drifting silently through windless grey landscape, dark fantasy concept art, cosmic horror aesthetic, muted colors with divine lighting accents, body horror elements, inspired by Berserk and Bloodborne, digital painting, detailed textures, ominous atmosphere, 4k

---

Behavior

Whisper-Weavers are tragic predators. They were once collections of stolen speech—archives of confiscated words kept by Azerach's priests. But speech wants to be spoken. Left unattended, the accumulated language achieved a kind of sentence, a desperate need to complete itself.

Hunting Method: They drift toward sources of sound—conversation, song, even heartbeats. When they find a speaking creature, they envelop it, pressing threads against the victim's mouth and throat. Each thread steals a specific word—not the sound, but the concept. Victims find themselves unable to say "help" or "mother" or their own name, those words literally woven into the creature's mass.

Communication: They cannot speak, but they can display words by forming them from their threads. These are always stolen words, spoken by others—fragments of desperate pleas, whispered secrets, dying confessions.

Congregation: Where one Whisper-Weaver drifts, others follow. They're drawn to each other, sometimes merging into larger entities. Silent libraries in Azerach's domain are often guarded by massive Whisper-Weaver amalgams containing centuries of confiscated speech.

---

Combat

---

Variants

Fading Wisp (CR 1): A young or damaged Whisper-Weaver with only a few hundred words. 22 HP, no Word Theft ability—it can only steal words through Envelop. Its threads display only fragments: "please" "no" "help" "why"

Archive Amalgam (CR 6): A massive merged entity containing the speech of generations. 90 HP, 20-foot space, and its Word Theft can target all creatures within 10 feet (each makes a separate save). Its thread-display can show entire sentences, often replaying final words of the consumed.

The Silenced Choir (CR 9): A legendary Whisper-Weaver formed from an entire community's stolen language—everyone who once lived in a village, their speech confiscated over generations. It can use silence at will, and creatures who fail its Word Theft by 5 or more lose the ability to speak entirely (not just specific words).

---

Ecology

Origin: Whisper-Weavers form in Azerach's confiscation vaults—places where stolen language is stored. The Whispered King doesn't create them deliberately; they're an unintended consequence of hoarding speech. Some scholars theorize they're the Lungs' attempt to breathe—language trying to escape.

Diet: They consume words, not flesh. A Whisper-Weaver that steals enough language grows larger and more complex. One that cannot feed slowly unravels, its threads dispersing into meaningless sounds.

Destruction: Killing a Whisper-Weaver releases its stolen words in a burst of overlapping voices. Anyone within 30 feet hears fragments of everything it consumed—last words, secrets, confessions. This cacophony deals no damage but can be psychologically devastating.

Recovery: Words stolen by a Whisper-Weaver can be recovered if the creature is destroyed and the victim is within 60 feet when it dies. Otherwise, the words are gone—scattered into noise, never to be spoken again.

---

Encounter Ideas

  • A village at the edge of Azerach's domain has lost the word for "fire"—no one can warn of danger, request warmth, or describe flames. A Whisper-Weaver drifts through at night.
  • The party must infiltrate a silent library guarded by ancient Whisper-Weavers displaying messages like "TURN BACK" and "SHE SCREAMED FOR HOURS"
  • A mute survivor is found wandering—they've lost every word except one. That word is a name: someone the party knows.
---

Related

  • Azerach the Whispered King
  • Dominion of Still Air
  • Silence-Ticks
  • Sibilants
---

"It didn't hurt. That's the worst part. It just touched my lips, and I couldn't say her name anymore. Couldn't write it. Couldn't even think it without the thought sliding away. She's gone from my mouth forever."
items.raw:
# Whisper-Weavers

**Type:** Aberration  
**Domain Association:** [[Azerach the Whispered King]]  
**Habitat:** Dominion of Still Air, silent zones, places where language has been removed  
**Challenge Rating:** 3 (700 XP)

---

## Description

In the Dominion of Still Air, words are property—stolen, hoarded, weaponized. Whisper-Weavers are what happens when too many stolen words accumulate in one place. They are ambulatory masses of compressed language, tangled voices given form, and they hunge...
